## Iteration 42 — 2026-05-25

### WWWWW. CAESAR II — Pipe Stress Analysis Software
- **CAESAR II** (Hexagon PPM, formerly Coade) — **industry-standard pipe stress analysis software**. Most refineries, EPCs, design firms używają CAESAR II. Alternatives: AutoPIPE (Bentley), ROHR2 (Sigma), PASS/Start-Prof.
- **Cel pipe stress analysis**:
  - **Code compliance** — verify że pipe meets ASME B31.1, B31.3, B31.4, B31.8, EN 13480.
  - **Equipment loading** — verify że nozzle loads na pumps, vessels, exchangers NIE przekraczają allowables.
  - **Support design** — optimum location + type of supports.
  - **Thermal expansion compensation** — adequate flexibility.
  - **Vibration analysis** — natural frequencies, pulsation response.
  - **Seismic / wind loading** — environmental loads.
- **Load cases** (KRYTYCZNE pojęcia):
  - **W** (Weight) — pipe + insulation + fluid + fittings dead weight.
  - **P** (Pressure) — internal/external pressure thrust.
  - **T** (Temperature) — thermal expansion stress.
  - **WW** (Wind) — wind force.
  - **U** (Seismic) — uniform earthquake load.
  - **F** (Force) — additional applied loads.
  - **D** (Displacement) — imposed support/anchor displacement.
  - **SAM** (Static Acceleration Method) — pump pulsation, slug forces.
- **Common load case combinations**:
  - **Sustained (Sus)** = **W + P** — continuous static loads. Stress must be < **Sh** (allowable at operating T).
  - **Operating (Ope)** = **W + P + T** — full operating condition. Used dla equipment nozzle load check.
  - **Expansion (Exp)** = **T** isolated (operating − sustained). Stress range must be < **Sa** (allowable thermal range).
  - **Occasional (Occ)** = **W + P + Wind/Seismic** — short-duration. Stress < **k × Sh** (k = 1.2-1.33 zależnie od duration).
- **Stress equations** (ASME B31.3 §302.3.5):
  - **Sustained**: SL = (P × D) / (4 × t) + (Mb × Z) / (Z × c) — longitudinal sustained stress.
  - **Expansion**: SE = √(Sb² + 4 × St²) — bending + torsion expansion stress range.
  - **Sa** = f × (1.25 × Sc + 0.25 × Sh) — allowable expansion stress range, where f = stress reduction factor (cyclic life factor).
- **Modeling workflow**:
  1. **Geometry input** — node coordinates + element data (pipe size, schedule, material, T, P).
  2. **Boundary conditions** — anchors (fixed), supports (Y, ZY restraint), guides, snubbers.
  3. **Equipment connections** — pump, vessel nozzles (with allowable load limits per API 610, API 661, NEMA SM-23).
  4. **Material properties** — auto-loaded from CAESAR II database (E, α, Sh, Sc, etc.).
  5. **Run analysis** — solver computes stress + displacement + force at each node.
  6. **Review results**:
     - **Stress contour** — color-coded plot show high-stress areas.
     - **Displacement** — pipe movement at each node.
     - **Support loads** — calculated forces on each support / spring.
     - **Equipment loads** — pump nozzle forces + moments vs allowables.
  7. **Iterate** — modify supports / loops jeśli stress > allowable, repeat.
- **Output reports**:
  - **Sustained stress vs allowable** (ratio < 1.0 = OK).
  - **Expansion stress range vs Sa**.
  - **Operating displacement** all 3 axes.
  - **Spring support design** — variable rate, constant load identification + selection.
  - **Equipment load summary** — pump / turbine / exchanger nozzle loads.
- **Pump / equipment nozzle limits**:
  - **API 610** — centrifugal pumps. Strict limits Fx/Fy/Fz/Mx/My/Mz.
  - **API 660** — heat exchangers.
  - **API 661** — air-cooled heat exchangers (fin-fan).
  - **NEMA SM-23** — steam turbines.
  - **API 617** — centrifugal compressors.
  - **Allowables** depend on nozzle size + class. Exceeding = pump misalignment, seal failure, vibration, premature wear.
- **Spring support sizing**:
  - **Cold load** — pre-load on spring at installation (when pipe cold).
  - **Hot load** — operating load (pipe at T_design).
  - **Variability** — typowo ≤ 25% (variable spring) lub 0% (constant).
  - **Travel** — vertical pipe movement at support point.
  - **Spring selection** dla typowy hanger: **Anvil, Bergen, Witzenmann, Lisega catalogs**.
- **Modal analysis (vibration)**:
  - **Natural frequencies** of pipe segments calculated.
  - **Avoidance**: pump/compressor running speed (e.g., 50 Hz lub 60 Hz, 2× harmonics) NIE matching natural freq.
  - **Mitigation**: add supports, change pipe routing, install snubbers (vibration dampers).
- **Seismic / dynamic analysis**:
  - **Response spectrum** — input ground acceleration profile per region (ASCE 7 dla USA, EN 1998 dla Europe).
  - **Time history** — full earthquake event simulation (more rigorous, slower).
  - **Snubbers + restraints** added gdzie dynamic loads dominate.
- **Output / deliverables**:
  - **Stress analysis report** — code calculations, all stress cases, conclusions.
  - **Support drawings** — type, location, spring data.
  - **Equipment loading tables** — sent to vendor dla verification.
  - **Movement diagrams** — for piping designer to ensure clearances.

### XXXXX. Valve Gland Packing — types + maintenance
- **Gland packing** = sealing material w **valve stuffing box** (przestrzeń wokół stem) preventing leakage z medium do atmosphere wzdłuż valve stem.
- **Construction**:
  - **Stuffing box** — cylindrical cavity wokół stem.
  - **Packing rings** — typowo **5-8 rings**, alternating compression vs sealing.
  - **Lantern ring** (środkowy) — z grease injection port dla lubrication.
  - **Gland follower** — pushes packing rings down z gland bolts.
  - **Gland nuts / studs** — kontrolują compression force.
- **Packing materials** (zależnie od service):
  - **PTFE (Teflon)** — bardzo niska tarcie + chemical inertia. Limit T: −200°C do +260°C. Best for: control valves (low torque), clean water, food. **Limit cycling — cold flow under load + thermal cycling = relaxation**.
  - **Pure graphite (flexible graphite, Grafoil)** — high T (do +650°C), wysokie ciśnienia, chemical resistance. Limit: oxidation > 450°C w obecności O₂. **Standard dla steam valves, hot oil, refining**.
  - **Carbon-PTFE composite** — balance między graphite + PTFE properties. Mid-range service.
  - **Aramid (Kevlar) braided** — wear-resistant, slurry service. Often combined z PTFE impregnation.
  - **Asbestos (legacy)** — zakazane od lat 1990s. Many retrofit jobs replace asbestos packing w old valves.
  - **Kalrez (perfluoroelastomer)** — premium PFE compound (Chemours/DuPont):
    - **Self-adjusting** spring-loaded V-ring design.
    - **Maintenance-free dla life of valve** (10+ years claimed).
    - **Temperature**: do +288°C.
    - **Chemical inertness**: virtually all chemicals OK.
    - **Drogie**: $500-2000 per packing set vs $50-200 standard PTFE/graphite.
    - **Fugitive emission compliance** dla LDAR (patrz iter 31).
  - **Garlock Style 5000, John Crane SAFEMATIC, Chesterton** — common branded products.
- **Packing arrangements**:
  - **All-PTFE**: low-T standard services.
  - **All-graphite**: high-T, steam, hot oil.
  - **Composite stack** (graphite + PTFE alternating): combines benefits.
  - **Live-loaded** packing: **Belleville spring washers** maintain compression as packing wears → consistent sealing without re-tightening.
- **Stem leak management**:
  - **Initial sealing pressure** — gland nuts torqued dla compression sufficient to seal.
  - **Wear over time** — packing gradually consumes; periodic re-tightening required.
  - **Gradual re-tightening** — **¼-turn increments** as leak appears. NIE over-tighten (zatarcie stem, motor overload).
  - **Live-loading** eliminates manual re-tightening — Belleville springs adjust automatically.
- **Replacement procedure** (KRYTYCZNE — gate valve example):
  1. **DEPRESSURIZE + ISOLATE** valve. Drain pipe section. Lockout/Tagout.
  2. **Remove handwheel / actuator** dla access to gland follower.
  3. **Loosen gland nuts**, lift gland follower off stem.
  4. **Pull old packing** z special tool (corkscrew packing puller).
  5. **Inspect stuffing box** — clean, no scoring, no corrosion (replace stem if damaged).
  6. **Cut new rings** — exact length wokół stem, **45° angle cut** (NIE square — wzmacnia leak path), stagger join positions on consecutive rings (120° offset between rings).
  7. **Install rings one-by-one**, **compress lightly** between each.
  8. **Install lantern ring** (if used) at correct level (aligns with grease port).
  9. **Final gland follower** + tighten gland nuts **evenly + lightly** initially.
  10. **Pressure test** at half service pressure first.
  11. **Operate valve** several times to seat packing.
  12. **Adjust gland nuts** — final tightening dla zero visible leak ale stem still operates smoothly (NIE over-tightened).
- **Signs requiring packing replacement**:
  - **Persistent leak** even after gland adjustment.
  - **Visible packing wear** — frayed, darkened, hardened rings.
  - **Stem damage** — score marks, corrosion (replace stem AND packing).
  - **Operator override** — packing so worn that actuator can't seal.
- **API 622 / API 624 / ISO 15848** — fugitive emission tests dla valve packing systems:
  - **API 622**: low-emission packing certification — tested at high cycles + temperature.
  - **API 624**: emission test of complete valve assembly.
  - **ISO 15848-1**: international fugitive emission test method.
  - **Certified packing** required dla LDAR-regulated services.

### YYYYY. Pipe Expansion Joints (Bellows) — Types + Application
- **Expansion joint (bellows)** = flexible mechanical component wbudowany w pipe absorbing **thermal expansion, vibration, alignment offset**. Alternatywa do **expansion loops** (gdy brak miejsca na pętle U-shape).
- **Bellows construction**:
  - **Metal bellows** — concentric metal rings (corrugations) welded together. Typowo SS 304/316/321 lub Inconel.
  - **Multi-ply bellows** — multiple layers (2-5 ply) dla high pressure / fatigue resistance.
  - **Single-ply** — basic, low-pressure applications.
- **Movement types** (jaką deformację absorbuje):
  - **Axial compression** — pipe rośnie wzdłuż osi → bellows compresses.
  - **Axial extension** — pipe skraca się.
  - **Lateral offset** — pipe centerlines z różne osie (parallel offset).
  - **Angular deflection** — pipe lines pod kątem.
  - **Torsional** — twist around axis (rare, designed avoid).
- **Joint configurations** (jak constrains pressure thrust):
  - **Single (unrestrained)** bellows:
    - **Bellows alone** between pipe ends.
    - **Pressure thrust** = full force = **P × A_bellows** transmitted to anchors.
    - **Anchors must be DESIGNED for thrust** (large forces!).
    - **Smallest, simplest, cheapest** — limited application (must have main anchor structure).
  - **Tied expansion joint**:
    - **Bellows + tie rods (typowo 4 rods)** wokół bellows.
    - **Tie rods carry pressure thrust** → anchors NOT loaded by thrust.
    - **Allow only LATERAL + ANGULAR movement** — NOT axial (tie rods restraint axial).
    - **Reduce required anchor strength**.
  - **Universal expansion joint**:
    - **2 bellows joined by spool pipe** w środku.
    - **Large lateral movement** + angular + axial.
    - **Tied universal** — tie rods między outer ends (no thrust on anchors).
    - **Wide flexibility envelope**.
  - **Hinged expansion joint**:
    - **Bellows + hinges** (pivots) na obu końcach.
    - **Single plane angular movement** only (1 DOF).
    - **Pressure thrust restraint** through hinges.
    - **Used in pairs** w "Z" lub "L" configurations dla absorbing thermal expansion.
  - **Gimbal expansion joint**:
    - **Bellows + 2 pairs of hinges** w prostopadłych planes.
    - **Multi-axis angular movement**.
    - **Pressure thrust restrained**.
    - Used w **complex 3D piping routes**.
  - **Pressure-balanced expansion joint**:
    - **2 bellows w opposite directions**, balancing thrust internally.
    - **Anchor-friendly** — minimal thrust transmitted.
    - **Used przy critical equipment** (turbines, expanders) gdzie nozzle loads MUST be minimized.
- **Aplikacje**:
  - **Steam piping** — large thermal expansion (CTE × ΔT × length = significant).
  - **Hot exhaust** — flue gas ducts, FCC catalyst lines.
  - **Cryogenic** — LNG, LH₂ — opposite direction (contraction).
  - **Vibration isolation** — pump suction/discharge, compressor connections.
  - **Settling foundations** — buried pipe że settles over time.
  - **Seismic isolation** — earthquake-prone regions.
- **Design parameters**:
  - **Movement** required (mm).
  - **Cycle life** — fatigue (typically 1,000-10,000 cycles dla industrial bellows).
  - **Pressure** rating.
  - **Temperature** (material + cycling effects).
  - **Spring rate** — force vs movement (kN/m).
  - **Effective area** — pressure thrust calculation A = (D_o² + D_o × D_i + D_i²)/3 × π/4 (approx).
- **Installation rules**:
  - **Pre-position** bellows w neutral position (no compression / extension at install).
  - **Main anchors** at each end of bellows assembly (carry pressure thrust if unrestrained).
  - **Guides** — pipe alignment maintained between main anchors (no buckling).
  - **NIGDY install w extension** (pre-compressed lub stretched).
  - **Welding to bellows** — only at end welds; never weld onto convolutions.
  - **Sleeves** — internal liner protects bellows from flow erosion (large velocity / abrasive).
- **Maintenance considerations**:
  - **Visible inspection** — corrosion of convolutions, cracks, dents.
  - **Cycle counter** dla critical bellows.
  - **Pressure test** retest after suspect events (lightning strike, foundation settlement).
  - **Replacement** when:
    - Crack detected w convolutions.
    - Excessive corrosion (wall thickness reduced > 25%).
    - End of design fatigue life (cycle count exceeded).
    - Visible deformation > spec.
- **Vendors**: **U.S. Bellows, Senior Flexonics, Hyspan, Witzenmann, EagleBurgmann, Pathway**.
- **EJMA (Expansion Joint Manufacturers Association)** — standardy projektowe + selection guidelines. **EJMA Standard** = bible dla bellows engineers.

## Iteration 43 — 2026-05-25

### ZZZZZ. Wafer vs Lug Butterfly Valve — installation + dead-end service
- **Butterfly valve body styles** (różne sposoby mocowania do flange):
  - **Wafer** — najprostszy, taniejsze. **Body wsuwany MIĘDZY dwa flange'e**, bolt'y przechodzą przez 2 flange'e (NIE bolt holes w body).
  - **Lug** — **lugs (uchwyty) z gwintami** sticking out z body, bolt'y wkręcane w lug z każdej strony **NIEZALEŻNIE**.
  - **Flanged (double flange)** — body z **integral flanges** na każdej stronie. Bolted jak ANY flange.
  - **Butt weld** — body z **weld ends**, spawany na pipe (nie demountable).
- **Wafer style** detail:
  - **Body design**: thin disc-shaped body z central hole. Pipe flange bolts go AROUND body, through bolt-circle of flanges.
  - **Centering**: bolts (lub centering tabs) align body z pipe ID.
  - **Cheap, lightweight, compact**.
  - **KRYTYCZNE LIMITACJA**: **NIE dla dead-end service**!
- **Dead-end service problem (wafer)**:
  - Jeśli **odłączysz downstream pipe**, **WSZYSTKO się rozpada**:
    - Body NIE jest sztywno mocowane do upstream flange (held by ALL bolts, w tym downstream).
    - Loosen downstream bolts → body falls out z wszystkimi internals.
  - **NIE robić** napraw / maintenance na downstream pipe gdy upstream pod ciśnieniem z wafer valve!
- **Lug style** detail:
  - **Lugs** (zewn. uchwyty) z **threaded holes**.
  - **Bolts threaded INTO body lugs** z każdej strony niezależnie.
  - **Body mocowane do upstream flange BEZ downstream flange** required.
  - **Dead-end service possible** — upstream side bolted, downstream open dla maintenance.
  - **Higher cost** niż wafer (more material in body, additional machining).
- **Lug ratings** (KRYTYCZNE):
  - **Full rated pressure** w dead-end service — only IF rated dla full pressure (większość brand mark "DEAD END" lub "BLOCK" rating).
  - **Reduced rating** (50-75% normal) common dla cheap lug valves w dead-end.
  - **Manufacturer spec confirms** — read body marking.
- **Installation procedure (wafer + lug)**:
  1. **Pre-install valve in HALF-OPEN position** — disc fits between flange faces without binding.
  2. **Position valve** between flanges — disc oriented per FLOW arrow (some valves bi-directional, some not).
  3. **Insert bolts** — sequence specific:
     - **Wafer**: bolts pass through both flanges + around valve body.
     - **Lug**: bolts threaded into body lugs (use thread engagement min 1.5× bolt diameter).
  4. **Hand-tight** all bolts.
  5. **Open valve fully** before final tightening (disc clears full bore).
  6. **Torque w star pattern** — typically 3 passes (30%, 60%, 100% torque).
  7. **Re-check torque** after first thermal cycle (gasket compression set).
- **Torque values** (typical, lubricated bolts):

| Valve Size | Bolt Diameter | Torque (ft-lb) |
|-----------|--------------:|---------------:|
| 2"-4" | 5/8" | 70 ±5% |
| 6" | 3/4" | 130 ±5% |
| 8" | 3/4" | 130 ±5% |
| 10"-12" | 7/8" | 200 ±5% |
| 14"-18" | 1" | 350 ±5% |
| 20"-24" | 1-1/8" | 500 ±5% |
| 30"-36" | 1-1/4" | 800 ±5% |
| 42"-48" | 1-1/2" | 1200 ±5% |
| 54"-60" | 1-3/4" | 1800 ±5% |

- **KRYTYCZNE**: nadmierny torque **uszkadza body lugs** (cracks, deformation). Stosuj **minimum torque dla metal-to-metal contact** between flanges + valve body.
- **Gasket considerations**:
  - **NIE separate gaskets** typowo wymagane dla wafer/lug — valve body z built-in **resilient seal** (EPDM, NBR, FKM) który contact z flange face.
  - **Niektóre installations** (high-T, aggressive media) wymagają **additional gasket** (kammprofile, RTJ).
- **Dead-end test procedure** (lug valve):
  1. **Disconnect downstream pipe**.
  2. **Pressurize valve** do design pressure z upstream side.
  3. **Hold time**: 10 min minimum.
  4. **No visible leakage** at valve face / between body + flange.
- **NEVER**: install wafer valve w dead-end service even temporarily. **Catastrophic failure mode**.

### AAAAAA. Hot Wire TIG (HW-TIG / TIP TIG) — high-deposition GTAW
- **Hot Wire TIG (HW-TIG)** = GTAW z **resistively-heated filler wire** entering pool. **Wire pre-heated** to near-melting temperature przez **separate AC power source** w wire just before entering pool.
- **Cel**: zwiększyć **deposition rate** standardowego TIG **2-12×** bez compromising quality.
- **System komponenty**:
  - **Main TIG power source** — DC arc do workpiece.
  - **Hot wire power source** (AC typowo) — independent supply heats wire przez electrical resistance.
  - **Wire feeder** — mechanical drive, programmable speed.
  - **Special torch** — gas cup + filler wire guide + 2 power connections.
- **Deposition rates**:

| Process | Deposition rate | Travel speed (8 mm SS) |
|---------|----------------:|----------------------:|
| Manual cold-wire TIG | 1-2 kg/h (2-4 lb/h) | 4-8 in/min |
| Cold-wire TIG (machine) | 2-3 kg/h | 8-12 in/min |
| Hot wire TIG | 4-5 kg/h | 12-20 in/min |
| TIP TIG (dynamic feed) | 5-7 kg/h | 15-25 in/min |
| GMAW spray transfer | 5-8 kg/h | 20-40 in/min |

- **Mechanism advantage**:
  - **Cold wire** — entering pool absorbuje znaczne ciepło, **chills weld pool**. Limit speed.
  - **Hot wire** — pre-heated wire melts **instantly** when contacting pool, **bez chilling**. **All arc heat dla penetration**.
  - **Result**: higher deposition + maintained penetration depth.
- **TIP TIG** (specific brand, Tip-tig.com / Senior Flexonics):
  - **Dynamic wire feed** — wire **oscillates back-and-forth** ~ 30-100 Hz, **smaller droplets** transferred.
  - **Even higher deposition** vs standard hot wire.
  - **Better arc dynamics** — claims 5-12× regular TIG productivity.
- **Aplikacje**:
  - **Stainless / Inconel pipe + tube welds** dla pharmaceutical, food, semiconductor:
    - HIGHER quality niż MIG/FCAW (typowa requirement dla BPE compliance).
    - Multiple of typical TIG speed.
  - **Cladding / overlay** — nakładanie korozyjnie odpornej warstwy on CS substrate (Inconel 625 over CS):
    - Hot wire TIG = 5-7 kg/h deposition vs SMAW 1 kg/h.
    - Premium dla refinery hydroprocessing reactors.
  - **Boiler tube weld repair**.
  - **Power generation** — nuclear, BPV welds.
- **Advantages vs GMAW (which jest faster ale lower quality)**:
  - **HW-TIG** maintains GTAW quality (low porosity, fine grain, smooth bead).
  - **HW-TIG** dla mission-critical welds wymagających GTAW standard.
- **Limitations**:
  - **Drogie equipment** — $30,000-100,000+ system (vs $2,000 manual TIG).
  - **Skilled setup required**.
  - **Manual usage** rzadkie — głównie semi-auto / fully automatic (orbital pipe heads, robot arms).
- **Filler wire considerations**:
  - **Standard ER70S-2, ER308L, ER316L wire** OK.
  - **Wire diameter**: 0.045" (1.2 mm) lub 1/16" (1.6 mm) most common.
  - **Stable wire chemistry** wymagana (uniform resistance heating).
- **Inert gas**:
  - **100% Ar** standardowo.
  - **Ar + H₂ mixes** dla stainless (deeper penetration).
  - **He blends** dla heavy material.
- **Quality vs traditional TIG**:
  - **Same bead profile** quality.
  - **Same NDE acceptance** rates.
  - **Same HAZ characteristics**.
  - **Sometimes BETTER** consistency niż manual TIG (machine-controlled wire feed eliminates operator variability).

### BBBBBB. Engine-Driven Welder / Generator — field welding power source
- **Engine-driven welder/generator (EDWG)** = portable welding power source + AC generator powered by internal combustion engine (diesel lub gasoline). **Standard dla field welding** gdzie no grid electricity (pipeline, construction, remote sites).
- **Main components**:
  - **Engine** — diesel (most popular dla heavy duty) lub gasoline (lighter, smaller).
  - **Alternator / generator** — typowo **brushless** lub older "brush-type" pure DC generator.
  - **Welding output** — CC (constant current) for SMAW/GTAW, sometimes CV (constant voltage) for MIG.
  - **Auxiliary power** — 110-220V AC dla power tools, grinders, lighting (1-25 kW).
  - **Fuel tank** — typically 4-25 gal capacity, allowing 4-12 hour run.
- **Top models w industry**:
  - **Lincoln Electric SAE-300 MP** — flagship dla pipeline welding:
    - **Pure DC generator** technology (NOT inverter) — old-school, ale **smoothest arc** dla cellulose electrodes (E6010 root pass).
    - **All-copper windings** + **mechanical commutator** — durable, long life.
    - **Output**: 50-390 A DC, 3000 W AC auxiliary.
    - **Perkins** lub Kubota diesel engine, ~25 HP.
    - **Weight**: ~1432 lb (650 kg).
    - **Cost**: $15,000-25,000.
    - **Pipeline tractor classic** — preferred by experienced pipeliners dla SMAW E6010 root + E7010 fill.
  - **Miller Big Blue series**:
    - **Big Blue 400X Pro**: 400 A.
    - **Big Blue 600 Pro**: 600 A — heavy industrial + multi-arc operations.
    - **EPA Tier 4 Final** emissions compliance — required dla US/EU regulations.
    - **Inverter technology** — modern arc characteristics, multi-process flexibility (SMAW/GTAW/MIG/FCAW).
  - **Lincoln Ranger 305D**: budget option, Kubota diesel, 300 A.
  - **Bobcat (Miller)**: smaller, 225 A, gasoline lub diesel.
  - **Ranger 9 / Ranger 250 (Lincoln)**: heavy 250 A engine drive.
- **Generator technology comparison**:
  - **Pure DC generator** (SAE-300):
    - Mechanical commutator + DC output direct (no rectifier).
    - **Smoothest, most natural arc**.
    - **Heavy + bulky** — pure copper coils, mass.
    - **Long life** — mechanical robustness.
    - **Best dla cellulose stick** electrodes (E6010, E6011, E7010).
  - **Inverter** (modern):
    - **Electronics convert AC to DC**.
    - **Lighter, smaller, more efficient**.
    - **Multi-process flexible** (SMAW/GTAW/MIG/FCAW).
    - **More features** (pulse, AC TIG, programmable parameters).
    - **Electronics susceptible** to dust, vibration, moisture (industrial grade IP 23-45 typically).
- **Welding processes supported**:
  - **SMAW (stick)** — most common, all engine drives.
  - **GTAW (TIG)** — newer multi-process units (Big Blue 400X Pro, SAE-300 MP).
  - **FCAW** — most multi-process units.
  - **GMAW (MIG)** — multi-process units z CV mode.
  - **CAC-A (air carbon arc gouging)** — high-output models (SAE-300 MP, Big Blue 600).
- **Fuel + duty cycle**:
  - **Diesel** — preferred dla industrial use (longer life engine, better fuel economy).
  - **Gas (gasoline)** — cheaper machine, lighter, ale shorter engine life.
  - **Duty cycle 100% at 250-300 A**: standard rating dla heavy field use.
  - **Continuous welding capability**: 8+ h shifts feasible (with fuel refill).
- **Pipeline applications**:
  - **2-welder team** — both work on pipeline simultaneously, often two engine drives + 1 air compressor truck.
  - **Mainline pipeline construction** — typowy field uses SAE-300 MP, downhill SMAW.
  - **Cellulose-loving operators** prefer pure DC generator over inverter dla 5G downhill (less prone do "spitting", smoother arc).
- **Maintenance**:
  - **Engine oil** — change every 100-250 hours.
  - **Fuel filter, air filter** — replace per manufacturer schedule.
  - **Spark plugs** (gas only) — clean / replace.
  - **Battery** — for engine start.
  - **Generator brushes** (DC pure gen) — inspect / replace.
  - **Welding output terminals** — clean, no corrosion.
- **Costs**:
  - **Small portable** (225 A gasoline): $3,000-6,000.
  - **Mid-range** (300 A diesel): $8,000-15,000.
  - **Pipeline-grade** (300 A pure DC, SAE-300): $15,000-25,000.
  - **Heavy duty** (600 A multi-process): $20,000-40,000.
  - **Rental** option common: $300-1500/week.
- **Trailer-mounted** dla mobility:
  - **Tow behind pickup truck**.
  - **Includes welder + air compressor + cargo space**.
  - **Lincoln Ranger Skid Mount** lub **Custom trailer builds** by welder shops.

## Iteration 44 — 2026-05-25

### CCCCCC. Plant Turnaround / Shutdown / Outage (STO) Management
- **STO (Shutdown, Turnaround, Outage)** = collective term dla **planned production halts** dla maintenance, inspection, modification, regulatory compliance. **Largest single operating expense** w refineries + chemical plants.
- **Terminology distinction**:
  - **Shutdown** — *small scale*. Single unit lub equipment OFF dla **days to 2-3 weeks**. Rest of plant continues operating.
    - **Examples**: heat exchanger cleaning, single pump replacement, isolated repair.
  - **Turnaround (TA)** — *large scale*. **Significant part lub entire plant** OFF dla weeks to months. **Major maintenance + inspection + upgrades**.
    - **Examples**: refinery FCC unit cleaning + catalyst replacement, full reformer inspection, vessel internals replacement.
  - **Outage** — *power industry term* (similar to turnaround). Used w utilities, nuclear, conventional power generation.
- **Frequency typowa**:
  - **Refinery**: **3-7 years** between major turnarounds.
  - **Petrochemical** (cracker, ammonia plant): **3-5 years**.
  - **Power plant boilers**: **annual lub bi-annual** inspections.
  - **Nuclear**: every **18-24 months**, refueling cycle.
- **Phases (typowy lifecycle)**:
  1. **Pre-planning** (12-24 months ahead):
     - **Scope definition** — what work needs doing (inspection findings, performance data, regulatory).
     - **Initial budget** + ROM (Rough Order of Magnitude) cost.
     - **Major equipment purchase** lead time (custom vessels, complex machinery 12+ months).
     - **Long-lead materials** (specialty alloys, custom valves).
  2. **Detailed planning** (6-12 months ahead):
     - **Work order packages** —每 task scoped with: scope, drawings, materials, tools, labor hours, dependencies.
     - **CPM (Critical Path Method)** scheduling — critical path identified, milestones.
     - **Resource allocation** — contractors, equipment rental, scaffolding.
     - **Procurement** — bulk materials, consumables.
  3. **Pre-turnaround** (1-3 months ahead):
     - **Mobilization** — contractors arrive, lay-down areas prepared.
     - **Inspection preparation** — scaffold, NDE crews on standby.
     - **Permitting** — confined space, hot work, scaffolding permits.
  4. **Execution** (turnaround period):
     - **Shutdown sequence** — depressurize, drain, purge units.
     - **Isolation** — LOTO + spectacle blinds.
     - **Cool-down + decontamination** — vessels purged of toxic / flammable.
     - **Manway opening, internal inspection** — boroscope + NDE.
     - **Work performed** — repairs, replacements, upgrades.
     - **Reassembly, leak test, hydrotest**.
     - **Start-up sequence** — purge, pressurize, fluid charge, heat up.
  5. **Post-turnaround review**:
     - **Lessons learned** — what worked, what didn't.
     - **Cost vs budget**.
     - **Schedule vs plan**.
     - **Safety incidents** review.
     - **Inputs to next turnaround** planning.
- **Critical success factors**:
  - **Scope freeze** — typowo **6 months before TA**, NO new work added (except emergency). Scope creep = budget + schedule blow-up.
  - **Detailed planning** — typical refinery turnaround = **30,000-100,000+ tasks** in master schedule.
  - **Resource availability** — skilled contractors, specialty tools, parts.
  - **Daily coordination meetings** during execution.
  - **Real-time progress tracking** — dashboards visible to all stakeholders.
- **Costs**:
  - **Small unit shutdown**: $1-10 million.
  - **Mid-size refinery turnaround**: $50-200 million.
  - **Major refinery turnaround** (full plant): $300 million - $1 billion.
  - **LNG plant**: similar massive scale.
  - **Daily lost production** during turnaround can exceed $1 million/day dla large refinery.
- **Key stakeholders**:
  - **Operations** — defines scope, schedules production loss.
  - **Maintenance** — executes mechanical work.
  - **Engineering** — designs modifications.
  - **Inspection / NDE** — verifies condition + new welds.
  - **Procurement** — materials + contractor management.
  - **HSE** — safety oversight (highest accident period dla plants).
- **Software tools**:
  - **CMMS (Computerized Maintenance Management Systems)**: IBM Maximo, SAP PM, Infor EAM.
  - **TA-specific**: Cleopatra Enterprise, EZTRAK, Primavera P6.
  - **Digital twin** — model of plant dla simulation, training, real-time monitoring.
  - **CAD integration** — drawings, isometrics, P&IDs available offline.
- **Pipefitter / welder role**:
  - **Bulk welding** dla pipe modifications, new tie-ins.
  - **Critical repair welds** under tight schedule pressure.
  - **Bolt-up** flanges po inspection.
  - **Hydrotest support**.
  - **Insulation reinstatement**.
  - **Long hours common** (12 h shifts × 7 days/week) — fatigue management critical dla quality + safety.
- **HSE focus areas** during STO:
  - **Hot work permits** — many simultaneous welds.
  - **Confined space entry** — vessel inspection.
  - **Working at heights** — scaffold installations.
  - **Lifting** — heavy equipment, complex rigging.
  - **Chemical exposure** — decontamination residuals.
  - **Fatigue + workload** — multiple 12+ h shifts.

### DDDDDD. API 618 / API 619 — Reciprocating Compressor Pulsation + Vibration
- **Reciprocating compressor** (piston-type) = volumetric machine generujący **discrete flow pulsations** with crank rotation. Pulsations propagują w pipe → acoustic resonances → vibration → fatigue / failure.
- **API 618** ("Reciprocating Compressors for Petroleum, Chemical and Gas Industry Services"):
  - **Industry standard dla recip compressor + piping design**.
  - **Mandatory pulsation studies** dla critical service.
  - Defines design approaches based on **risk level**.
- **API 619** ("Rotary-Type Positive Displacement Compressors for Petroleum, Petrochemical, and Natural Gas Industries"):
  - **Screw compressors, rotary lobe** (Roots-type), rotary vane.
  - **Less pulsation issues** niż recip (smoother flow) ale still requires acoustic study dla critical service.
- **Pulsation problem details**:
  - **Source**: piston motion generates **sinusoidal pulsations** at compressor running speed + multiple harmonics (1×, 2×, 3×, ... times running freq).
  - **Pipe acts as organ pipe** — gas inside pipe ma **natural acoustic frequencies**.
  - **Resonance**: gdy compressor frequency matches pipe natural frequency → **amplitude amplification** 10-100×.
  - **Pulsation force on piping** → **mechanical vibration** → cracks, fatigue, equipment failure.
- **API 618 Design Approaches** (3 levels):
  - **Approach 1**: empirical design rules (basic). Skuteczne dla low-risk, simple piping.
  - **Approach 2 (acoustic analysis)**: **MATHEMATICAL PULSATION STUDY** — most common. Uses **TLM (Transmission Line Modeling)** lub **finite difference** software.
  - **Approach 3 (advanced)**: full **mechanical + acoustic + thermodynamic coupled analysis**. Used dla **critical service** (offshore, high-pressure, hydrogen).
- **Pulsation study process** (Approach 2):
  1. **System modeling** — pipe, fittings, valves, vessels, compressor model.
  2. **Compressor flow source** — simulated flow vs time (varies with valve dynamics, gas properties).
  3. **Acoustic simulation** — solves wave equation in piping, finds pressure pulsation amplitudes.
  4. **Compare to API 618 allowables**:
     - **Pulsation peak-to-peak** ≤ specific % of line pressure (e.g., 7% dla typical service).
     - **NIE acceptable**: > 1% pulsation w some critical service.
  5. **Modifications** if exceeded:
     - **Pulsation dampener (bottle)** — large volume vessel near compressor.
     - **Orifice plate** — choking specific resonance.
     - **Pipe routing change** — eliminate resonant length.
     - **Branch line / valve repositioning**.
- **Pulsation dampeners (bottles)**:
  - **Surge bottle** = simple expansion vessel.
  - **Sized**: typowo **5-15× compressor swept volume per cylinder**.
  - **Effectiveness**: **3-15 dB attenuation** at fundamental frequency.
  - **Multi-stage** bottles dla wider frequency range.
- **Mechanical analysis (Phase 2 API 618)**:
  - **After pulsation acceptable**: separate **mechanical vibration study**.
  - **Pipe natural frequencies** computed (FEM analysis).
  - **Avoidance**: mechanical natural frequencies NIE mogą match compressor harmonics.
  - **Support locations + types** optimized dla shifting natural frequencies away from harmonics.
- **Vibration acceptance**:
  - **API 618**: pipe vibration **≤ 0.4 mm/s velocity** (peak-to-peak) at API 618 design points.
  - **Some operators**: stricter 0.2 mm/s dla mature plants.
- **Service consequences (poor pulsation control)**:
  - **Pipe weld cracking** — fatigue from cyclic loading.
  - **Valve damage** — compressor + downstream valves wear.
  - **Pipeline leaks** — flange + threaded connections vibrate loose.
  - **Equipment foundation cracks** — vibration transmitted to structure.
- **Pre-FEED + FEED stage**:
  - **Front-End Engineering Design (FEED)** dla new plant — pulsation study early.
  - **Catches design issues** before construction begins.
  - **Cost savings**: fixing pulsation during design = $$, fixing in field = $$$$$$$.
- **Vendors / consultants**: **Beta Machinery Analysis** (Canada/USA — premier), **Dynaflow Research Group** (Netherlands), **Spectrum Fluid Dynamics**, **Wood plc Vibration Dynamics & Noise**, **Mechartes**, **Atos Group**.

### EEEEEE. Steam Methane Reformer (SMR) Pigtails + Outlet Headers — high-T weld
- **Steam Methane Reformer (SMR)** = high-T furnace dla producing **hydrogen + CO** z natural gas (CH₄) + steam:
  - Reaction: **CH₄ + H₂O → CO + 3H₂** (endothermic, **700-900°C**).
  - **Heart of ammonia plants** (H₂ feedstock dla NH₃ synthesis).
  - **Hydrogen production plants** (refinery, industrial gas suppliers).
- **Pigtail definition**:
  - **Pigtail** = small-diameter (DN 25-50) **outlet tube** connecting **catalyst tube** (vertical reactor tube w furnace) do **outlet manifold (collector header)**.
  - **U-shaped** lub helical coil shape (jak ogon świński — stąd nazwa).
  - **Flexibility** dla thermal expansion accommodation (catalyst tubes grow 50-150 mm during heating).
- **Service conditions** (extreme):
  - **Temperature**: **700-900°C** outlet gas (depends on plant design).
  - **Pressure**: **30-40 bar** (450-580 psi).
  - **Atmosphere**: **H₂ + CO + CO₂ + H₂O** — **highly carburizing** at high T, hydrogen embrittlement risk.
  - **Cyclic operation**: trips, startups, hot bypass periods.
- **Materials**:
  - **Pigtails**: **Alloy 800H / 800HT** (UNS N08810 / N08811):
    - **Fe-Ni-Cr** (32 Ni, 21 Cr, balance Fe).
    - **Creep strength** to 1000°C.
    - **Excellent oxidation + carburization resistance**.
    - Annealed condition dla improved fabrication.
  - **Inconel 800HT** = "high-temp" variant z slightly higher Al + Ti dla creep enhancement.
  - **Catalyst tubes (different material)**: **Centrifugally cast HK / HP / Microalloyed** (Fe-25Cr-35Ni).
  - **Outlet header**: typowo **Alloy 800H** lub **Centrifugal Cast HK-40**.
  - **DMW (Dissimilar Metal Weld) issue**: pigtail (wrought Alloy 800H) → catalyst tube (cast HP) → outlet header (different alloy).
- **Welding challenges** (KRYTYCZNE):
  - **DMW failures** — common between cast tubes + wrought pigtails:
    - **Carbon migration** from low-C to high-C side during creep.
    - **Sigma phase precipitation** w HAZ.
    - **Stress relaxation cracking** — small cyclic stresses w high T.
  - **Filler metal** selection:
    - **309 / 309L stainless** (high-Cr) → buffer layer dla DMW.
    - **Inconel 82 / 182** (NiCr) — superior dla high-T DMW (less DMW interface issues, better creep match).
    - **Buttering technique** preferred (patrz iter 19): Inconel layer buttered onto cast tube → pigtail welded to buttered layer.
  - **PWHT** complicated — full structure cannot easily be heat treated (giant furnace permanent).
- **Stress Relaxation Cracking (SRC)**:
  - **Mechanism**: residual welding stresses + creep strain w high-T service → **grain boundary cavities** → cracks.
  - **Critical temperature range**: 550-750°C — exactly SMR service zone.
  - **Particularly bad** dla 800H/HT family + 304/316L SS w long-term high-T service.
  - **Mitigation**:
    - **PWHT** even if difficult.
    - **Solution annealing** w shop pre-installation.
    - **Avoid notch geometries** (smooth weld transitions).
    - **Minimize restraint** during welding.
- **Damage mechanisms (in service)**:
  - **Creep rupture** — 1° priority dla 800H, finite life 5-15 years typical.
  - **Stress relaxation cracking** (welds + HAZ).
  - **Carburization** — surface attack from C in process gas.
  - **Sulfidation** — if S in feed gas.
  - **Nitriding** — if N₂ purges during startup/shutdown.
- **Inspection (turnaround)**:
  - **Visual external** — bulge / deformation visible.
  - **Diametrical measurement** (creep growth) — calipers, laser:
    - **Bulge > 3-5%** OD = approaching end of life.
  - **Microsampling (Replication)** — small replica of surface microstructure → grain boundary cavities visible w SEM lab.
  - **PMI** verify alloy.
  - **Boroscope ID** — coke deposits, scaling.
  - **Internal UT** specialized tools.
- **Replacement strategy**:
  - **Routine pigtail replacement** every **5-10 years** (per inspection findings).
  - **Catalyst tubes** replaced każde **8-15 years** (very expensive — typically all tubes replaced together podczas major TA).
- **Specialty repair**:
  - **Hot tap during shutdown** — partial repair without full unit replacement.
  - **Specialty welders** (Inconel + Alloy 800H certified) — niewielu w field, premium pay.
  - **Manufacturer-specific WPS** — each SMR vendor (Topsoe, KBR, Linde) has own qualifications.

---

## Iteration 45 — 2026-05-25

### FFFFFF. Pump Baseplate Grouting — Epoxy vs Cementitious
- **Grouting** = wypełnianie przestrzeni między **baseplate (skid)** rotating equipment + **concrete foundation**. **Kluczowe** dla load transfer, vibration damping, dimensional stability.
- **Cel**:
  - **Load transfer** — operating + dynamic loads od pompy/motor do foundation równomiernie.
  - **Anchor bolt grip** — encapsulates anchor bolts, prevents loosening.
  - **Vibration damping** — grout absorbs vibration, prevents resonance amplification.
  - **Dimensional stability** — utrzymuje shaft alignment over years.
  - **Corrosion protection** anchor bolts.
- **Grout types**:
  - **Cementitious grout** (Portland cement + sand + additives):
    - **Tańsze** — $50-100 per 50-lb bag.
    - **Workable** — fluid consistency, łatwo pour.
    - **Limited compressive strength**: 50-80 MPa typical.
    - **Shrinkage** — significant (0.05-0.2% volume loss during cure). **PROBLEM**: shrinkage może wypchnąć baseplate z alignment.
    - **Sensitive do oil** — absorbs hydrocarbons, weakens.
    - **NIE dla retrofit** — gdy old grout removed, fresh cementitious shrinks gap.
    - **Stosowane**: nowe instalacje on fresh concrete, **non-critical pumps**, light loads.
  - **Epoxy grout** (epoxy resin + aggregates):
    - **Drogie** — $30-50 per kg (10× cementitious cost).
    - **High compressive strength**: 80-130 MPa.
    - **Near-zero shrinkage** (< 0.001%) — minimal effect na baseplate alignment.
    - **Chemical resistance** — oil, acids, alkalis.
    - **High impact + vibration absorption**.
    - **Better load transference**.
    - **Standard dla rotating equipment** (pumps, compressors, turbines).
    - **Stosowane**: critical service, high horsepower, retrofit, refinery + petrochem.
- **Application procedure** (epoxy grouting):
  1. **Foundation preparation**:
     - **Surface chipping** — concrete na rough texture (chip off laitance 1-2 cm depth dla mechanical key).
     - **Cleaning** — vacuum, oil removal (TSP + degreaser).
     - **Inspection** — no cracks, no oil ponding, no debris.
  2. **Form setup**:
     - **Wooden forms** wokół baseplate, sealed against leaks.
     - **Sufficient height** dla grout depth (typowo 50-100 mm under baseplate).
     - **Vent holes** w forms dla air escape.
  3. **Baseplate positioning**:
     - **Place baseplate** on foundation, level w X/Y axis with **leveling screws**.
     - **Shaft alignment** — pump + motor cold-aligned (within tolerance, considering thermal growth — patrz iter 38).
     - **Anchor bolts** torqued lightly (hold in place, NOT final torque yet).
     - **Soft foot check** — none.
  4. **Mix epoxy** — 2-part (resin + hardener), specific ratio (typowo 1:1 lub 4:1 by volume).
  5. **Pour from one end** — slow, continuous, allow air to escape from vents.
  6. **Vibrate / consolidate** — eliminate voids.
  7. **Cure**:
     - **Initial set**: 4-8 h.
     - **Full cure**: 7-14 days dla full strength.
     - **Temperature range**: 10-32°C during cure (most epoxies).
  8. **Final torque anchor bolts** — only after full cure.
  9. **Re-check shaft alignment** — verify final position post-cure.
- **API 686 (Pre-commissioning Machinery)** — references:
  - **Cementitious dla budget installations**.
  - **Epoxy mandatory dla critical service** (refinery rotating equipment > 75 kW typically).
- **Cementitious vs epoxy decision matrix**:

| Factor | Cementitious | Epoxy |
|--------|--------------|-------|
| Cost | Low | High |
| Strength | Medium | High |
| Shrinkage | High (problematic) | Near zero |
| Oil resistance | Poor | Excellent |
| Cure time | 7-28 days | 7-14 days |
| Retrofit (over old grout) | Poor (shrinks) | Good |
| Pour thickness | 25-100 mm | 25-150 mm |
| Vibration absorption | Moderate | Excellent |
| Critical rotating | Avoid | **Standard** |

- **Common failures** (grout):
  - **Shrinkage cracks** (cementitious) — visible cracks w grout body, baseplate movement.
  - **Oil penetration** (cementitious) — soaks oil from pump leaks, loses strength.
  - **Improper cure** — too cold = soft grout, too hot = brittle.
  - **Inadequate mixing** — epoxy components NIE proper ratio → soft / brittle local spots.
  - **Voids under baseplate** — air pockets reduce load transfer area.
- **Re-grout** when:
  - **Cracks** visible w existing grout.
  - **Pump vibration increased** without identifiable cause.
  - **Baseplate movement** (alignment drifts post-tightening).
  - **Oil soaking** through grout.
- **Vendors / products**:
  - **ITW Performance Polymers** (Escoweld, Chockfast) — premier epoxy grouts.
  - **Five Star Products** — Five Star Epoxy Grout.
  - **BASF MasterFlow** (formerly Masterflow 928, 648).
  - **Sika Sikadur** — epoxy line.
  - **Quikrete cementitious** — budget option.

### GGGGGG. Anti-Spatter Sprays + Nozzle Gel — MIG/FCAW Consumables Protection
- **Spatter** = drobinki roztopionego metalu wyrzucane z weld pool podczas welding (głównie GMAW/FCAW gdzie metal transfer NIE jest perfect). **Problem**:
  - **Klei się do**: nozzle, contact tip, gas diffuser, workpiece, painted surfaces.
  - **Reduces gas flow** (build-up w nozzle).
  - **Cleaning cost** — operator time + tools.
  - **Cosmetic damage** finished products.
  - **Increased consumable wear**.
- **Anti-spatter products**:
  - **Spray (aerosol)** — applied **PRZED welding** na workpiece + nozzle:
    - **Quick application**, large area coverage.
    - **Various formulas**: water-based (safer), petroleum-based (more durable), citrus-based (eco-friendly).
    - **Brands**: CRC SP-400, Walter E-WELD 4, ZEP, Weld-Aid.
    - **Cost**: $10-25 per can.
    - **Coverage**: typowo 100-200 ft² per can.
  - **Nozzle gel / tip dip** — paste w container, applied **DO HOT NOZZLE**:
    - **Dip nozzle ~25 mm deep** w container of gel.
    - **Excess drips back** into container.
    - **Coats nozzle/tip interior** + exterior.
    - **Forms protective barrier** — spatter NIE klei.
    - **Brands**: Hobart 770120, Blue Demon BDNG, Bernard NG-1, Lincoln KP3070.
    - **Cost**: $15-30 per 16 oz container.
    - **Reusable** — same gel container używany przez setki dips.
- **Application protocol**:
  - **Spray**:
    1. **Shake can** thoroughly.
    2. **Wipe workpiece** if dirty (oil, paint).
    3. **Spray light coat** 15-30 cm from surface.
    4. **Let dry** 30-60 seconds.
    5. **Weld**.
    6. **Reapply** every 30-60 min during continuous welding.
  - **Nozzle gel**:
    1. **Weld for 5-15 min** (heats nozzle).
    2. **Dip hot nozzle** in gel container ~25 mm deep.
    3. **Lift quickly**, let excess drip.
    4. **Continue welding** — gel re-applies as nozzle heats.
    5. **Repeat** every 30-60 min lub when spatter buildup visible.
- **Composition**:
  - **Water-based**: water + emulsifiers + silicones (low odor, biodegradable).
  - **Petroleum-based**: light mineral oil + waxes (better durability, slight smell).
  - **Citrus / soybean-based**: d-limonene + esters (eco, food-grade).
- **Health considerations**:
  - **Older formulas** (with silicone)** — concern dla painted surfaces (silicone interferes with paint adhesion downstream). Modern formulas often silicone-free.
  - **Petroleum-based** — flammable, ventilation w confined space.
  - **Aerosol propellants** — environmental concerns (some restricted by EU regulations).
- **Specialized products**:
  - **Permanent ceramic coating** — long-life (months), expensive (only dla high-volume production).
  - **Plasma-applied coating** — manufacturer-applied dla nozzle longevity.
  - **Self-cleaning nozzles** — special design + integrated wire brush mechanism (e.g., Bernard Centerfire).
- **Beyond spatter control**:
  - **Tip dip** also lubricates contact tip — reduces wire feed friction.
  - **Heat sink effect** — gel absorbs heat, prolongs tip life.
- **Surface preparation alternatives**:
  - **Aluminum tape** on surfaces requiring zero spatter (high-end manufacturing).
  - **Polyethylene sheeting** dla overspray protection w paint area.
  - **Welding blanket** under work area (catches falling sparks + spatter).
- **Cost-benefit**:
  - Spray + gel: **$0.50-2.00 per hour of welding**.
  - **Cleanup labor saved**: 10-20% of welder time = **$5-15/hour cost savings**.
  - **Consumable life extension**: 2-3× longer nozzle/tip life.
  - **Payback**: immediate.

### HHHHHH. BOP (Blowout Preventer) — Offshore + Onshore Drilling Safety
- **BOP (Blowout Preventer)** = **safety device** mounted on wellhead dla **emergency well control** during drilling, completion, workover. Designed to **seal well** if gas/oil surge encountered.
- **Critical context**: **Macondo Well Deepwater Horizon (2010)** disaster — failed BOP led to oil spill catastrophe. **Massive regulatory tightening** post-Macondo (BSEE / NTL regulations).
- **BOP stack** = vertical assembly of multiple BOPs (annular + rams + valves) atop wellhead. Different layers handle different scenarios.
- **Two main BOP types**:
  - **Annular BOP** (ANNULAR Preventer):
    - **Donut-shaped rubber packing element** ("packer") surrounds drill pipe.
    - **Hydraulic actuated** — inflates inward, sealing around any pipe size.
    - **Versatile**: works dla different pipe diameters (different OD), open hole, BHA (Bottom Hole Assembly).
    - **Position**: usually **on TOP of stack** (first line of seal).
    - **Pressure rating**: typowo 5K-15K psi (350-1000 bar).
    - **Brand examples**: Cameron Type D, NL Shaffer, Hydril GK/GX.
  - **Ram BOP**:
    - **Two ram blocks** moving horizontally toward each other, sealing well.
    - **Hydraulically driven** ram cylinders, large force (200-1000 kN).
    - **Multiple types** (specific function each):
- **Ram types** (specific):
  - **Blind ram** — solid steel rams, seal **open hole only** (NO drill pipe through). Used when no pipe in well.
  - **Pipe ram** — rams z **half-circle cutouts** matching specific pipe OD. Seals **around drill pipe** w well. Variable pipe sizes wymagają different rams.
  - **Variable bore ram (VBR)** — rams adjustable dla range of pipe sizes (e.g., 3½"-5½"). More expensive, eliminates ram changes.
  - **Shear ram (blind shear)** — **STRONG steel blades cut THROUGH drill pipe** + seal well. **LAST RESORT** — destroys pipe + sometimes everything below.
  - **Casing shear** — even stronger, cuts thick casing pipe. Macondo-tier emergency capability.
- **Typical BOP stack** (drilling rig, top-to-bottom):
  1. **Flow head / mud return** — top of stack, normal mud flow.
  2. **Annular BOP** — first seal element.
  3. **Top blind ram** — seals empty hole.
  4. **Pipe ram (or VBR)** — seals around drill pipe.
  5. **Second pipe ram** — redundancy.
  6. **Blind shear ram** — emergency cut + seal.
  7. **Casing shear ram** (some stacks) — extreme emergency.
  8. **Bottom flange** — connects do wellhead.
- **Hydraulic control system**:
  - **Accumulators** (pressurized N₂ + hydraulic fluid) — store energy dla emergency operation.
  - **Manifold + control panels** — driller's panel + remote panel.
  - **MUX (multiplexed) electronic control** — modern subsea BOPs.
  - **ROV intervention** dla subsea BOPs — Remotely Operated Vehicle.
- **Subsea vs surface BOPs**:
  - **Surface BOP**: rig floor located (jack-up rig, platform). Smaller, easier maintenance.
  - **Subsea BOP**: ON seabed (deepwater). Larger, more redundant. Macondo had subsea BOP (failed).
- **Testing**:
  - **Function test** — every 7-14 days.
  - **Pressure test** — every 14-21 days (BOPE / BSEE regulations).
  - **Function test** = full open/close all rams + annular (verify hydraulics work).
  - **Pressure test** = sealing tests at design pressure with test plug installed.
- **Connections to BOP**:
  - **Kill line** — pump heavy mud into well dla "kill operation" (overcome formation pressure).
  - **Choke line** — controlled bleed-off of pressure (instead of full venting).
  - **Hydraulic supply lines** — operate rams + annular.
  - **Spacer spools** — adjust stack height dla rig accommodations.
- **API 16A** standard — BOP equipment specifications.
- **API 53** standard — BOP equipment systems dla drilling wells.
- **Pipefitter / welder involvement**:
  - **Choke + kill line piping** — high-pressure (5K-15K), specific BOP-rated valves (API 6A specs).
  - **Hydraulic line piping** — small-bore, Swagelok-style fittings.
  - **Stack assembly + bolt-up** — heavy bolts, hydraulic tensioners (heavy gauges 1-3").
  - **BOP transportation** — heavy lift cranes, BOP weighs 50-500 tons.
  - **Test ports + plugs** dla BOP testing.
- **Failure modes (historical)**:
  - **Blind shear ram failure** — bad design, slow shear, drill pipe joints (thicker than tube body) block shear.
  - **Hydraulic fluid leak** — single point of failure dla operation.
  - **Communication failure** subsea (lost umbilical).
  - **Macondo lessons**: redundancy, automated dead-man systems, regulatory testing increases.

## Iteration 46 — 2026-05-25

### IIIIII. Heat Exchanger Tube Bundle Extraction + Cleaning
- **Heat exchanger** (shell + tube) = primary heat transfer equipment w refineries, chemical plants, power plants. **Tube bundle** = removable assembly z tubes mounted w tubesheets — periodically must be extracted dla cleaning + inspection.
- **Removal Methods**:
  - **Fixed tube bundle** — bundle integral with shell, can't be extracted. **Cleaned in-place** (chemical lub mechanical via tube ID).
  - **Removable tube bundle (floating head, U-tube)** — bundle slides out shell dla offline cleaning + inspection.
- **Equipment dla bundle extraction**:
  - **Tube bundle puller (extractor)** — hydraulic ram system:
    - **Truck-mounted (mobile)** — preferred dla site work, brings own crane lift.
    - **Aerial bundle extractor** — z built-in crane, dla high elevation exchangers (refineries on pipe rack levels).
    - **Capacity**: typowo 5-100 ton (depends on bundle weight).
    - **Pull force**: 50-500 ton hydraulic ram.
  - **Crane / hoist** — lifts pulled bundle to cleaning area.
  - **Slings + spreader bars** — certified rigging (per ASME B30.9, see iter 37).
  - **Bundle cradle** — dedicated support during transport + cleaning.
- **Extraction procedure**:
  1. **Shutdown + isolation** — block valves closed, drain shell + tube sides.
  2. **Depressurize + cooldown** — wait minimum 4-24 h depending on T.
  3. **Open channel head + cover** — access to tubesheet.
  4. **Disconnect tube-side piping** (if needed).
  5. **Remove bonnet / cover bolting**.
  6. **Attach puller** do tubesheet face (using stud holes).
  7. **Hydraulically pull bundle** out shell slowly (1-2 m/min).
  8. **Inspect during extraction** — visible scale, damaged tubes, broken baffles.
  9. **Transfer bundle do cleaning area** (lay-down yard).
- **Cleaning methods**:
  - **Hydroblasting** (najczęstsze nowoczesne):
    - **High-pressure water jet** — 10,000-40,000 psi (700-2800 bar).
    - **Lance** (długa rurka) wsuwana w each tube bore.
    - **Rotating nozzle head** — multiple jets clean tube ID circumference.
    - **No chemicals** — environmentally friendly.
    - **Disposal**: contaminated water → API separator → treatment plant.
    - **Productivity**: 50-200 tubes/hour (skilled operator).
  - **Chemical cleaning** (mniej common, more controlled):
    - **HCl + inhibitors** dla iron oxide / carbonate scale.
    - **Citric acid + buffers** dla mild scale.
    - **Solvents** (toluene, xylene) dla heavy hydrocarbon fouling.
    - **Hazardous** — chemical waste handling + worker exposure.
    - **Used when**: hydroblast can't access (gummy fouling), tube ID restrictions.
  - **Mechanical brushing**:
    - **Powered brushes** rotated through tubes.
    - **Effective dla soft scale**.
    - **Limited dla hard deposits** (silicates, sulfates).
  - **Hydrolazing** — combined high-pressure water + brush rotation.
  - **Ultrasonic cleaning** dla small exchangers (tank submersion).
- **Inspection post-cleaning**:
  - **Visual + boroscope** of tube IDs.
  - **Tube ID drift test** — gauge passes verify no restriction (drift mandrel).
  - **Eddy Current Testing (ECT)** — 100% tube inspection dla wall thinning, pitting, cracking.
  - **Helium leak test** (after re-installation) — verify tube-to-tubesheet integrity.
- **Reinsertion**:
  - **Reverse of extraction** — hydraulic puller now pushes bundle back into shell.
  - **Alignment critical** — tubes must thread through baffle holes.
  - **Damaged tubes plugged** — solid tapered plugs (typowo SS, hammered into both ends of damaged tube). NIGDY MORE niż 10% of tubes plugged before bundle retubing.
  - **Bolt-up channel head + cover** with fresh gasket.
  - **Pressure test** before return to service.
- **Frequency** (typical refineries):
  - **Heavy fouling service** (crude preheaters, lube oil residue): **6-18 months** cleaning interval.
  - **Clean service** (water cooling, refrigeration): **3-5 years** between cleanings.
  - **Driven by**: ΔP increase, heat transfer efficiency drop.
- **Cost** (typical):
  - **Hydroblast cleaning**: $20,000-100,000 per bundle (depending on size + fouling).
  - **Chemical cleaning**: $50,000-300,000.
  - **Bundle puller rental**: $5,000-25,000 dla full operation.

### JJJJJJ. TIG Welding Inverter vs Transformer Power Source
- **Welding power source** = AC mains → welding output (DC lub AC, current-controlled). Two main technologies:
  - **Transformer** (legacy, simple): main → step-down transformer → rectifier (if DC) → arc.
  - **Inverter** (modern, complex electronics): main → rectifier → high-frequency switching (8-25 kHz) → small transformer → final output.
- **Transformer welder**:
  - **Construction**:
    - **Large copper/aluminum coils** wound on steel core.
    - **Mechanical tap selection** dla amperage (or moving coil control).
    - **Diodes** dla DC rectification (no diodes = AC machine).
  - **Weight**: 300A unit = **200-400 lb (90-180 kg)**.
  - **Size**: refrigerator-sized cabinet.
  - **Efficiency**: ~70-75% (heat loss w transformer core + windings).
  - **Power factor**: 0.5-0.7 (high reactive load, poor PF).
  - **Cost**: $1,000-5,000 typical.
- **Inverter welder**:
  - **Construction**:
    - **Rectifier** stage (main AC → DC bus).
    - **High-frequency switching** transistors (IGBTs / MOSFETs) at 8-25 kHz.
    - **Small ferrite transformer** (high-frequency = much smaller than 50/60 Hz transformer).
    - **Output rectifier** (if DC) + filter.
    - **Microcontroller** dla precise output control.
  - **Weight**: 300A unit = **30-50 lb (14-23 kg)**.
  - **Size**: lunch-box to briefcase size.
  - **Efficiency**: **85-90%** — less heat loss.
  - **Power factor**: 0.9+ (PFC corrected — modern units).
  - **Cost**: $1,500-8,000 typical (premium feature units higher).
- **TIG-specific inverter advantages**:
  - **AC TIG aluminum welding**:
    - **Adjustable wave balance** — % cleaning vs % penetration (50/50 standard, 30/70 for thick aluminum penetration, 70/30 dla cleaning oxide).
    - **Wave shape selection** — square wave (sharp transitions, fast), sine wave (smooth, traditional), triangular (focused arc), soft square (smoothest, premium machines).
    - **AC frequency adjustment** — 30-400 Hz (vs transformer fixed 50/60 Hz). Higher freq = narrower arc, deeper penetration.
  - **Pulse TIG**:
    - **Peak + background amperage** w pulses.
    - **Pulse frequency**: 0.5-500 Hz adjustable.
    - **Reduce heat input** dla thin material (eliminates burn-through).
    - **Improve out-of-position welding** (background freezes pool).
  - **Hot start**:
    - **Higher initial amperage** dla 50-300 ms — prevents electrode sticking, ensures arc.
  - **Anti-stick / arc force**:
    - **Reduces current** if arc shortens excessively, prevents stuck electrode.
  - **Slope-in / slope-out**:
    - **Ramp up + ramp down** amperage at start/end (eliminates crater, smooths transitions).
- **Other inverter benefits**:
  - **Multi-process** (SMAW + TIG + MIG + FCAW from one unit).
  - **Wireless remote** control (Lincoln, Miller premier units).
  - **Digital displays** + memory dla 100+ parameter sets.
  - **USB / Bluetooth** dla data logging (per iter 43 wireless monitor).
  - **CAN-bus interfaces** dla robot integration.
- **Transformer advantages** (why still used):
  - **Durability** — proven 30-50+ year service life w field conditions.
  - **Dirt / moisture resistance** — fewer electronics, simpler construction.
  - **High-amperage** capability (some industrial 1500A+ machines still transformer).
  - **EMI immunity** — fewer electronics to interfere with sensitive instruments.
  - **Cost** — initial purchase cheaper.
- **When to choose**:
  - **Inverter**: portable applications, multi-process needs, AC TIG aluminum, precision welding, modern shop.
  - **Transformer**: heavy industrial, high-amperage SMAW dla pipeline (SAE-300 pure DC generator), legacy systems with proven track record.
- **Modern industry trend**: **inverter ~90% of new sales**. Transformers still selling dla specific applications (pure DC pipeline pure generator, heavy industrial fixed installation).
- **Maintenance considerations**:
  - **Inverter**: electronics-sensitive (dust, moisture). IP 23-45 enclosure rating standard.
  - **Transformer**: virtually maintenance-free except brushes (DC generator types).
- **Power input flexibility**:
  - **Inverter**: often auto-detects 110V/220V/380V/440V single + three-phase. Field-deployable.
  - **Transformer**: usually fixed single voltage configuration.

### KKKKKK. Casing Centralizer + Cementing Operations (Downhole)
- **Casing centralizer** = mechanical device clamped wokół casing pipe dla keeping casing **centered w wellbore** during cementing operations. **Critical dla zonal isolation** of well.
- **Why needed**:
  - **Cement bond integrity** — wymaga **uniform 360° annular space** wokół casing.
  - **Without centralizer**: gravity pulls casing do lower side of wellbore → uneven cement (thin top side, thick bottom). **Bond failure** = oil/gas migration between zones (contamination, blowouts).
- **Centralizer types**:
  - **Bow Spring Centralizer**:
    - **Curved (bowed) steel spring strips** between two end collars.
    - **Spring force** pushes casing away from wellbore wall.
    - **Flexible** — compresses dla wellbore variations (washouts, narrows).
    - **Resist drag** during running casing.
    - **Best dla**: deviated + horizontal wells, irregular wellbore profile.
    - **Material**: spring steel, sometimes coated for corrosion.
  - **Solid (Rigid) Centralizer**:
    - **Cast / forged solid steel** body with **fixed-OD blades / vanes**.
    - **NO spring action** — set fixed distance from casing wall.
    - **Higher strength** — won't crush w tight spots.
    - **Lower drag** during running.
    - **Best dla**: open-hole, hard formations, high-side push direction.
  - **Semi-rigid** — hybrid bow + solid features.
  - **Composite centralizer** — plastic / carbon-fiber, lower friction, used dla soft formations.
- **Spacing** dla cementing:
  - **Typowo 1-2 centralizers per joint** (joint = 30-40 ft pipe length).
  - **Vertical wells**: every 2-4 joints (less critical).
  - **Deviated wells**: every joint (closer spacing).
  - **Horizontal**: every joint + sometimes 2 per joint.
  - **Critical zones** (production interval): closer spacing.
- **Stop collars**:
  - **Mounted ABOVE + BELOW centralizer** — prevent centralizer slide along casing.
  - **Clamp-type** (bolt-on, removable) lub **slip-on** (permanently swaged).
  - **Coil clamps** ekstra style.
- **Casing equipment additional components**:
  - **Float shoe** — bottom of casing, check valve preventing cement backflow.
  - **Float collar** — secondary check valve 1-2 joints above shoe.
  - **Cement plug** — physical separator between drilling mud + cement slurry.
  - **Casing scratcher** — wire bristles, scrape mud cake from wellbore wall (improve cement bond).
  - **Wiper plug** — wipes residual fluid z casing after cementing.
- **Cementing operation sequence**:
  1. **Run casing string** — joints connected on rig floor + lowered into well.
  2. **Centralizers** mounted at intervals during running.
  3. **Bottom plug** circulated down.
  4. **Cement slurry** pumped down — typowo Portland cement + additives (retarder, accelerator, weight materials).
  5. **Top plug** circulated down.
  6. **Drilling fluid (mud)** displaces cement, forcing it **up the annulus**.
  7. **Bumping plug** — plug reaches float collar, hard "bump" pressure spike confirms completion.
  8. **Wait on cement** (WOC) — typowo 4-24 h dla initial set.
  9. **Pressure test** casing integrity.
- **Cement bond evaluation** (post-cementing):
  - **Cement Bond Log (CBL)** — sonic tool measures bond quality.
  - **Variable Density Log (VDL)** — visualize bond pattern.
  - **Ultrasonic Imager** — high-resolution bond imaging.
  - **Poor bond** = remediation (squeeze cement job, additional perforations).
- **API specifications**:
  - **API Spec 10D** — centralizer specifications + testing.
  - **API Spec 10A** — cement materials.
  - **API RP 65** — well construction practices.
- **Industries**:
  - **Oil & gas wells** — primary.
  - **Water wells** — large diameter, similar concept.
  - **Geothermal wells** — high-T cement formulations.
  - **CCS (Carbon Capture & Storage)** — strict bond requirements dla CO₂ injection wells.
- **Pipefitter relation**:
  - **Wellhead surface piping** — connected to BOP + Christmas tree (patrz iter 41).
  - **Cementing pumps + lines** — temporary high-pressure piping (5,000-15,000 psi).
  - **Cement supply lines** — bulk handling, abrasion-resistant.

## Iteration 47 — 2026-05-25

### LLLLLL. Automated Pipeline Welding — CRC-Evans IWM + PWT
- **Automated pipeline welding** = mechanized welding systems dla **mass production of cross-country pipelines** (oil & gas transmission). Replace manual SMAW (E6010 root + E7010 fill) z mechanized GMAW/FCAW dla **dramatically higher productivity**.
- **CRC-Evans Pipeline International** — established 1969, **market leader** w automated pipeline welding. Owned by Lincoln Electric (since 2018). Technology standard dla pipeline construction.
- **Key CRC-Evans systems**:
  - **IWM (Internal Welding Machine)**:
    - **Tractor-style robot** crawls **INSIDE** pipe.
    - **Multi-torch design** — 4-6 GMAW torches arranged around circumference.
    - **Welds root pass z INTERNAL side** in less than 1 minute.
    - **Simultaneous welding** all positions (4-6 torches working w parallel).
    - **Wire**: 0.9-1.2 mm ER70S-6 typowo.
    - **Gas**: 75/25 Ar/CO₂ lub similar.
    - **Productivity**: 60-80 welds per day (vs 15-25 manual).
  - **M500 External Welding System**:
    - **Single-torch external bug** crawls around pipe OD.
    - **Bidirectional** (CW + CCW), one welder operates both directions z single station.
    - **Mechanized FCAW** dla fill + cap passes.
    - **High deposition** — competitive z stick weld speeds.
  - **Pipe Facing Machine (PFM)**:
    - **Lathe-style end prep machine** — bevel + counterbore w one operation.
    - **Mounted on pipe end** for in-situ end preparation.
    - **CNC controls** dla precise bevel angles + dimensions.
    - **Used w**: pipeline tie-ins, repair sections, lay-down yard fabrication.
- **PWT (Italy)** — competitor:
  - **Similar automated systems** — internal + external welding heads.
  - **Marketed dla**: better repair cost + parts availability w Europe / Asia / Middle East.
  - Side-by-side comparison studies show **comparable performance** vs CRC-Evans.
- **Welding sequence** (modern automated pipeline):
  1. **Pipe yard pre-fab**: PFM bevels both ends.
  2. **Line-up clamp** sets root gap precisely (typowo 1-3 mm).
  3. **IWM internal root** — multi-torch simultaneous all-around weld w < 1 min.
  4. **External M500 hot pass** — burns out internal slag, builds material.
  5. **Fill passes** (multiple, FCAW mechanized).
  6. **Cap pass** — visual + cosmetic final.
- **Quality control**:
  - **Real-time arc monitoring** — current, voltage, wire feed, gas flow each torch.
  - **Auto-rejection** when parameters out of WPS range.
  - **Data logging** każda spoina — audit trail.
  - **100% NDE typical** — automated UT (AUT) z **TOFD + Phased Array**.
- **Productivity**:
  - **Pipeline weld rate**: 300-500 km of welded pipeline w 6-month construction season (top Russian + Canadian pipelines).
  - **Welds per day**: 80-120 per crew (vs manual 15-25).
  - **Quality**: ≤ 1% repair rate (manual: 3-5%).
- **Pipeline standards covered**:
  - **API 1104** (USA pipelines).
  - **CSA Z662** (Canada).
  - **DNV-OS-F101** (offshore + Norwegian Continental Shelf).
- **Limitations**:
  - **Long lead time setup** (15-30 min per tie-in) — manual faster dla single welds.
  - **NIE all positions** — typowo 5G horizontal pipe (rolling pipe) OR 5G fixed (rolling welding head).
  - **6G overhead complicated** — manual welding często faster dla overhead.
  - **Heavy equipment** — IWM weighs 200-500 kg, requires crane support.
- **Pipeline applications**:
  - **Cross-country** oil + gas transmission (Trans-Saharan, Nord Stream, TAPI).
  - **Offshore** S-lay vessel (pipe horizontal layed from ship).
  - **Yard fabrication** large-diameter sections.

### MMMMMM. Eddy Current Testing (ECT) — Heat Exchanger Tube Inspection
- **Eddy Current Testing (ECT)** = electromagnetic NDE method. **AC coil** generates magnetic field → induces **eddy currents** w conductive material → defects disturb pattern → detected.
- **For pipe applications** (NOT heat exchanger): **limited use** — mostly dla heat exchanger tubes (DN ≤ 50 small diameter, long length, hundreds of identical tubes).
- **Bobbin probe** (standard heat exchanger tube probe):
  - **Two coils encircling tube ID**, ~differential mode.
  - **Slides** through tube ID za pomocą wand lub air drive.
  - **Signal analysis** — phase + amplitude indicates defect type + size.
- **ECT detection capabilities**:
  - **Pitting**: 90% detection rate (vs 67% UT for same defects).
  - **Wall loss**: ±5% accuracy dla overall thickness.
  - **Cracks**: limited dla circumferential (perpendicular to coil), better dla axial.
  - **Discontinuities**: dents, bulges, OD/ID defects.
- **Material compatibility**:
  - **Non-ferrous**: SS 304/316, brass, copper, Cu-Ni, Inconel, Monel, titanium. **PRIMARY application**.
  - **Ferromagnetic** (CS, low-alloy): requires **saturation** with high-field magnet (specialized probes). Less effective.
- **Probe types** (heat exchanger tube inspection):
  - **Bobbin coil**: standard, fast, good dla general inspection. Limit: poor circumferential resolution.
  - **Array probe** (Eddyfi I-Flex): multiple coils circumferentially arranged. **Best dla**: pitting location, crack characterization, ID/OD discrimination.
  - **RFT (Remote Field Testing)** — dla **carbon steel tubes** (overcomes saturation limit). Slow.
  - **MFL (Magnetic Flux Leakage)** — dla carbon steel, similar do RFT.
  - **IRIS (Internal Rotating Inspection System)** — ultrasonic-based, NOT eddy current. Often used as complementary technique.
- **Inspection rates** (skilled operator):
  - **Bobbin scan**: 500-1000 tubes per shift (8 h).
  - **Array probe**: 200-500 tubes (slower due to higher data density).
  - **RFT**: 100-300 tubes per shift.
- **Defect detection limits**:
  - **Bobbin**: 5-10% wall loss minimum.
  - **Array**: 2-5% wall loss, plus location.
  - **Pinhole < 1 mm**: bobbin OK, array better.
- **Standard practices**:
  - **ASME V Article 8**: ECT general guidelines.
  - **ASTM E309 / E426**: heat exchanger tube ECT.
  - **ASNT SNT-TC-1A**: technician qualification (Level I, II, III).
- **Calibration**:
  - **Reference tube** (calibration standard) — identical material + dimensions z **manufactured defects** (drilled holes, machined grooves, EDM notches at specific depths 10%, 20%, 40%, 60% wall).
  - **Tester calibrates probe** on reference tube before each batch of production tubes.
- **Inspection plan**:
  - **100% tube inspection** typical w major TA.
  - **Plug criteria**: tubes z defects > 50% wall typically plugged.
  - **Re-tube criteria**: gdy > 10-20% of tubes need plugging — full bundle replacement economical.
- **Pipefitter / millwright role**:
  - **Bundle pulled** (per iter 46) → cleaned → inspected by NDE technician.
  - **Identification of bad tubes** — plug with tapered plug + weld seal (tube ID specific).
  - **Damaged tube replacement** — pulled from tubesheet + new tube installed + re-rolled + re-welded.

### NNNNNN. Cosasco / Coupon Access Fittings — Corrosion Monitoring
- **Cosasco** (Rohrback Cosasco Systems) — pioneer w **retrievable corrosion monitoring** devices. Industry standard dla refinery, pipeline, chemical plant corrosion tracking. Brand often used generically.
- **Cel**: install + remove **corrosion coupons, ER probes, chemical injection devices** **WITHOUT shutdown**. Live-pressure access.
- **Two-inch system** (najpopularniejsze):
  - **2" raised face flange** mounted na pipe lub vessel.
  - **Access fitting body** (welded lub flanged to host pipe):
    - **Model 50 (Flarweld)**: welded to pipe, weld neck flange.
    - **Model 54 (NPT)**: threaded into pipe.
    - **2" ANSI flange** type dla bolt-on installation.
  - **Service valve** (typowo ball valve) — isolation between process + atmosphere podczas device installation/removal.
  - **Retriever tool** — hydraulic mechanism dla pushing/pulling device under pressure.
  - **Access fitting plug** — carrier holding device (coupon, ER probe, chemical injector). Removable.
- **Components installed via access fitting**:
  - **Corrosion coupon holder** — 3" lub 6" coupon strip:
    - **Material matches process pipe** (CS, SS, alloy).
    - **Pre-weighed** w lab.
    - **Inserted** dla 30-90 days exposure.
    - **Retrieved + cleaned + weighed** — weight loss = **corrosion rate** (mm/year lub mpy).
  - **ER (Electrical Resistance) probe**:
    - **Element wire / strip** w process — measures resistance change as corrosion thins element.
    - **Real-time monitoring** (data logger) vs coupon (post-removal analysis).
  - **LPR (Linear Polarization Resistance) probe**:
    - **3 electrodes** w electrolyte (aqueous solution).
    - **Real-time corrosion rate** measurement.
    - **Online** corrosion monitoring system inputs.
  - **Chemical injection nut**:
    - **Atomizing nozzle** dla injecting corrosion inhibitor, biocide, scale inhibitor into process flow.
  - **Hydrogen probe** — monitors atomic H permeation through steel.
- **Pressure ratings**:
  - **Standard**: do **1500 psi (100 bar)**.
  - **High-pressure**: 2500-6000 psi (165-410 bar) — special variants.
  - **API 6A** wellhead versions: 10K-15K psi.
- **Procedure** (retrieval under pressure):
  1. **Verify pressure rating** vs operating pressure.
  2. **Attach retriever** to access fitting service valve.
  3. **Open service valve** — pressure equalizes z retriever chamber.
  4. **Engage retriever to plug**.
  5. **Withdraw plug** through service valve into retriever housing.
  6. **Close service valve** — isolates process.
  7. **Retriever depressurized** (vented to safe area).
  8. **Remove plug** from retriever.
  9. **Inspect / replace device** (coupon for analysis).
  10. **Reverse procedure** dla reinsertion.
- **Safety considerations**:
  - **Skilled operator** required — improper procedure can lead to **device blow-out** under pressure (potentially lethal).
  - **Two-person operation** standard.
  - **Hot work permit** required (proximity hot work hazards).
  - **PPE**: face shield, FR clothing, gas detector.
  - **Emergency stop** equipment ready (block valve closure, depressurization).
- **Industries**:
  - **Refining**: crude unit overheads (corrosive HCl), sour water service, cooling water.
  - **Oil & gas production**: well streams (CO₂, H₂S corrosion).
  - **Chemical**: process streams w aggressive media.
  - **Water treatment**: monitor cathodic protection effectiveness.
- **Data integration**:
  - **Coupon weight loss** → CR formula = (mass_initial − mass_final) × constants / (density × area × time).
  - **ER / LPR probes** → continuous Modbus / OPC output to plant DCS.
  - **Predictive maintenance** algorithms — trend corrosion rate, predict failure date.
- **Standards**:
  - **NACE TM0169** — laboratory immersion test methods.
  - **ASTM G1, G4, G46** — corrosion measurement standards.
  - **API RP 571** — damage mechanisms in refineries.
- **Vendors**:
  - **Cosasco (Rohrback)** — premier.
  - **Caproco International** (Canada) — competitor.
  - **Metal Samples Company** — coupons + access fittings.
  - **Honeywell Smart Wireless** — modern wireless ER probes z transmitters.
