## Iteration 54 — 2026-05-25

### GGGGGGG. CIPP (Cured-In-Place Pipe) — Trenchless Rehabilitation
- **CIPP (Cured-In-Place Pipe)** = trenchless rehabilitation z **resin-saturated fabric liner** inserted into existing pipe, cured to form **new structural pipe inside old pipe**. Standard dla aging buried infrastructure renewal.
- **~50% market share** rehabilitation sewer + water lines globally.
- **Process steps**:
  1. **Pre-clean** existing pipe — hydro-blasting, robotic cuttering of roots / scale.
  2. **CCTV inspection** — verify defects + diameter mapped.
  3. **Liner preparation** — felt/fiberglass fabric tube **vacuum-impregnated** z thermosetting resin (polyester, vinylester, epoxy).
  4. **Insertion**:
     - **Inversion method**: hot water lub air pressure inverts liner (turns inside-out as it enters) — resin-side becomes new ID.
     - **Pull-in method**: liner pulled through pipe + then inflated.
  5. **Curing**:
     - **Hot water** — circulated through liner, 60-95°C, 4-12 hours.
     - **Steam** — faster (1-3 hours), works dla larger diameter.
     - **UV light** — UV-curable resins + light train pulled through. 20-60 min cure (fast).
     - **Ambient cure** (small diameter) — slow but no equipment.
  6. **End trimming** — cut liner at manhole / access points.
  7. **Lateral re-connection** — robotic cutters reopen branch connections (for sewers).
  8. **Final CCTV inspection**.
- **Liner materials**:
  - **Polyester resin** — economy, sewer + general.
  - **Vinylester resin** — moderate chemical resistance.
  - **Epoxy** — high chemical + structural strength, potable water OK.
  - **Felt fabric** — traditional, low-cost (NEEDLED, NON-WOVEN).
  - **Fiberglass-reinforced** — STRUCTURAL liner, higher strength, often UV-cured.
  - **Carbon fiber** — premium, high-pressure.
- **Sizes**:
  - **Range**: DN 100 - DN 2800 (4" - 110").
  - **Most common**: DN 150-600 (sewer mains).
- **Aplikacje**:
  - **Sewer** — gravity sanitary + storm sewers. Largest market.
  - **Water mains** — pressurized water distribution.
  - **Gas pipelines** (specialty CIPP).
  - **Chemical** — chemical-compatible epoxy.
  - **District heating** — hot water service.
- **Advantages vs dig-and-replace**:
  - **NO trenching** — minimum surface disturbance.
  - **Traffic / utility avoidance** — no excavation streets, gas, electric.
  - **2-5× faster** installation.
  - **30-50% cheaper** than full pipe replacement (smaller projects).
  - **50+ year service life** designed.
- **Limitations**:
  - **Reduces ID** — typowo 3-10 mm wall, depending on size + structural requirement.
  - **Hydraulic capacity** slightly reduced (smaller ID).
  - **NOT dla completely collapsed pipe** — needs partial existing structure.
  - **Smell concerns** — styrene (polyester resin) odors during cure.
  - **Health risk** styrene exposure dla workers + nearby residents.
- **Environmental concerns**:
  - **Styrene emissions** — local air quality issue.
  - **Resin spills** — during impregnation lub install accidents.
  - **Curing water disposal** — sometimes contaminated z resin (controlled discharge).
- **Modern improvements**:
  - **Low-styrene + styrene-free resins** — UV-cured glass fiber preferred dla pharma + drinking water.
  - **LED curing** — replacing UV (safer, faster, more energy-efficient).
  - **Robotic installation** — automated dla repeatable, consistent results.
- **Vendors**:
  - **Insituform Technologies** — pioneer + market leader.
  - **Aegion** — Insituform parent, various CIPP brands.
  - **National Liner** — large North American contractor.
  - **Reline America** — UV-cured specialty.
  - **Saertex** — fiberglass liner manufacturer.
- **Standards**:
  - **ASTM F1216** — CIPP installation by inversion + cured-place.
  - **ASTM F1743** — CIPP installation by pull-in + place.
  - **ASTM F2019** — Fiberglass CIPP rehabilitation.
  - **ASTM D5813** — Quality assurance.
  - **NASSCO** (National Association of Sewer Service Companies) — industry guidelines.

### HHHHHHH. AR Welding Helmets — Augmented Reality + Smart Helmets
- **AR (Augmented Reality) welding helmet** = next-generation welding helmet integrating **digital information overlay** w welder's field of view. Status: emerging, premium products available + research projects ongoing.
- **Concept**:
  - **Internal cameras + display** — operator views welding through screen (NOT direct optical view through filter).
  - **Real-time data overlay** — voltage, current, wire feed, gas flow displayed w field of view.
  - **AR guidance** — virtual cues showing weld path, optimal angles, parameter recommendations.
  - **Recording** — capture welding sessions dla training + QC review.
- **Available products**:
  - **Miller AugmentedArc Augmented Reality Welding System**:
    - **Training-focused** — primarily for welding education.
    - **Virtual workpiece** projected w helmet view.
    - **Real torch + real motion** — operator handles physical equipment.
    - **Software grades** weld quality based on technique parameters (speed, angle, distance).
    - **Used by**: welding schools, apprentice programs.
  - **Miller MobileArc**:
    - **Portable training kit** — same AR concept, classroom-deployable.
- **High-light-transmittance helmets** (alternative approach — not full AR, but improved vision):
  - **Optrel Crystal 2.0**:
    - **Crystal Lens Technology (CLT 2.0)** — 31% light transmittance w light state (vs typical 20-25%).
    - **Shade 2 light state** — better visibility dla tacking, positioning.
    - **Auto-darkening** to shade 4-12 dla welding.
    - **NOT full AR** — just better optics.
  - **3M Speedglas 9100 / G5-01** — premium auto-darkening, high-clarity.
  - **Lincoln Viking 3350 / 4400** — economical + good vision.
- **AR research projects**:
  - **INTARWELD (EU)** — research initiative, AR welding helmet z external camera + heads-up display.
  - **MIT** — early AR welding prototypes.
  - **Various university research** — sensor fusion + augmented welding.
- **Potential AR capabilities**:
  - **Tungsten tip distance** monitoring (real-time).
  - **Travel speed** indicator (red/green dla too fast/slow).
  - **Bead profile** projection (predicted weld result).
  - **Parameter compliance** alerts (out of WPS range warnings).
  - **WPS instructions** displayed during welding.
  - **Voice commands** dla parameter changes.
- **Challenges to mainstream adoption**:
  - **Cost** — premium AR helmets ~$2000-5000+ vs $200-400 standard auto-darkening.
  - **Battery life** — internal cameras + display drain quickly (1-4 hour use).
  - **Lag / refresh rate** — display latency dla fast welding moves.
  - **Reliability** — electronics + welding environment (heat, smoke, EMI).
  - **Welder acceptance** — experienced welders prefer direct optical view.
- **Data integration**:
  - **Bluetooth** to weld data logger (per iter 52).
  - **WPS sync** — automatic parameter loading per joint.
  - **Cloud sync** — recorded sessions dla review.
- **Future direction**:
  - **Smart glasses + helmet integration** — Microsoft HoloLens-style devices fused z welding helmet.
  - **Eye-tracking** — focus where welder's looking.
  - **AI weld coaching** — real-time guidance based on AI analysis.
- **Adjacent technologies**:
  - **Welding simulators (VR)** — Lincoln VRTEX 360, Soldamatic — full immersive virtual training without burning consumables. Different from AR (no real welding) but related.
  - **Robotic welding HMI** — operator screens dla remote-controlled welding stations.

### IIIIIII. Cooling Tower Piping System — Power Plant + Industrial Cooling
- **Cooling tower** = heat rejection equipment dla **process cooling water cycle**. Critical infrastructure dla:
  - **Power plants** — steam condenser cooling.
  - **Refineries / chemical** — process cooling, distillation column overheads.
  - **HVAC large buildings** — chiller condenser water.
  - **Industrial processes** — quenching, heat exchangers.
- **Open recirculating water loop** (most common):
  - **Hot water** from heat source (condenser) → **distributed across cooling tower top** → **falls through fill media** → **air pulled across** by fans → **evaporative cooling** → **cool water collected w basin** → **pumped back** to heat source.
- **Pipe sizes** (large!):
  - **1,000 MW power plant**: ~600,000 GPM (140,000 m³/h) circulating water.
  - **2 circuits × 9-foot ID pipe** each (DN 2700) dla maintaining 10 ft/s velocity.
  - **Velocity**: typowo **2-3 m/s (7-10 ft/s)** — balance między friction loss + flow rate.
- **Hydraulic design**:
  - **Continuity equation**: Q = A × V (flow = area × velocity).
  - **Pipe sizing**: D = √(0.408 × GPM / velocity) [imperial] lub D = √(4Q/πV) [metric].
  - **Pressure drop calc**: Darcy-Weisbach lub Hazen-Williams.
  - **Manning equation** dla gravity flow w open channels.
- **Pipe materials** (typowo):
  - **Carbon steel** — economical, requires coating + CP.
  - **Concrete pipe (Pre-stressed Concrete Cylinder Pipe — PCCP)** — large diameter (DN > 1500), buried.
  - **FRP** — corrosion-resistant, water treatment chemicals OK.
  - **Cement-lined steel** — internal coating prevents corrosion.
  - **HDPE** — smaller diameter, modern installations.
- **Common components**:
  - **Suction pipe** (z basin do pumps) — large, low velocity, vortex breakers.
  - **Discharge pipe** (z pumps do condenser/heat exchanger) — high pressure.
  - **Return pipe** (z condenser back to tower) — gravity OR low-pressure pumped.
  - **Distribution piping** atop tower — spray nozzles lub gravity troughs.
  - **Bypass loops** dla chemical treatment, filtration, side-stream.
  - **Make-up water** — replaces evaporation losses (~1-2% per hour).
  - **Blowdown line** — controlled discharge dla concentration control (drain dissolved solids).
- **Layout best practices**:
  - **Suction line FLOODED** — basin elevation above pump centerline (NPSH compliance).
  - **Short suction line** — minimize friction losses.
  - **Long-radius elbows** w turbulent flow areas.
  - **Strainer / screen** on suction (prevent debris entering pumps).
  - **Air relief valves** at high points (prevent air binding).
  - **Drain valves** at low points (maintenance + winterization).
  - **Expansion joints** dla thermal growth (long runs).
- **Pump arrangement**:
  - **Multiple pumps w parallel** — typowo 2-4 cooling water pumps, n+1 redundancy.
  - **Vertical turbine pumps** — for large flow, common.
  - **Variable frequency drives (VFD)** — modern installations dla flow control + efficiency.
- **Water treatment piping** (parallel to main piping):
  - **Side-stream filtration** — 5-15% of total flow through filters (sand, multimedia).
  - **Chemical injection** — corrosion inhibitor, biocide, scale inhibitor, dispersants.
  - **pH adjustment** — sulfuric acid lub caustic.
  - **Conductivity sensors** + automatic blowdown control.
- **Common issues**:
  - **Corrosion** — circulating water has oxygen → corrosion of CS pipe.
  - **Biofouling** — algae, bacteria, Legionella w stagnant areas.
  - **Scale** — CaCO₃ deposit on hot heat exchanger tubes.
  - **Erosion** — high velocity at elbows.
  - **Vibration** — pump-induced w piping.
- **Chemical dosing pipes** (small but critical):
  - **PVC / CPVC** typically — chemical-resistant.
  - **Stainless steel** dla high-T injection (boiler feedwater).
  - **Backflow preventers** — chemicals not migrating back to main loop.
- **Maintenance** considerations:
  - **Periodic flushing** — remove sediment, scale.
  - **Inspection access** — manways, drain points.
  - **Catwalks + ladders** dla tower access.
  - **Spare pump replacement** — typowo every 5-10 years.
- **Codes / standards**:
  - **ASME B31.1** dla power plant piping.
  - **CTI (Cooling Technology Institute)** — tower design + operation guidelines.
  - **ASHRAE** dla HVAC cooling tower applications.

## Iteration 55 — 2026-05-25

### JJJJJJJ. Robotic Welding Cells — GMAW Automation dla Pipe Spool
- **Robotic welding cell** = **6-axis industrial robot arm** z welding torch + workpiece positioner (rotators / tilt tables) + sensors + safety enclosure. **Production-volume welding** w shop fabrication.
- **Główne komponenty cell'a**:
  - **6-axis robot** (Fanuc, ABB, KUKA, Yaskawa) — wszystkie kierunki ruchu.
  - **Welding power source** — inverter GMAW/FCAW (Lincoln Power Wave / Miller PipeWorx with CAN-bus integration).
  - **Wire feeder** — synchronized z robot.
  - **Positioner** — rotates pipe / spool dla optimal welding position.
  - **Vision system** — laser line scanner lub camera dla seam tracking.
  - **Through-arc sensing** — detects pool z arc parameters (alternative to vision).
  - **Cell controller** — PLC / HMI coordinating wszystko.
  - **Safety enclosure** — light curtains, interlocked doors, smoke extraction.
- **Pipe spool welding** specific challenges:
  - **3D geometry** — many spools z multiple angles, fittings, branches.
  - **NIE każdy weld accessible** — robot arm reach limited.
  - **Setup time** dla każdej unique configuration.
  - **Tack welding** still often manual (or auto-tack system).
- **Spool Welding Robot (SWR)** — Novarc Technologies (Canada):
  - **Dedicated dla pipe spool** welding.
  - **Pipe diameters**: **DN 50 do DN 1500** (2"-60") z appropriate positioner.
  - **Processes**: GMAW, MCAW (metal-cored), FCAW.
  - **Auto-features**:
    - **Root seam tracking** — vision identifies root gap.
    - **Root pass memorization** — replicates root profile dla fill passes.
    - **Auto distance control** — torch-to-work distance consistent.
    - **Auto parameter adjustment** dla different positions (uphill, downhill).
  - **Productivity claim**: **3-5× manual welder output**.
- **Programming** robot welding:
  - **Teach pendant programming** — operator manually guides robot, records points.
  - **Offline programming** (OLP) — CAD-based programming (Delmia, RoboGuide, FANUC ROBOGUIDE):
    - **Import 3D CAD model** of part.
    - **Define weld paths** w software.
    - **Simulate** dla collision detection, reach verification.
    - **Generate robot program** automatically.
  - **Auto-programming** z laser scanning — robot scans part, generates weld path automatically.
- **Process selection**:
  - **Thin wall (≤ 6 mm)**: GMAW pulse (cleaner welds, less distortion).
  - **Medium wall (6-12 mm)**: GMAW root + FCAW fill/cap (productivity).
  - **Heavy wall (≥ 12 mm)**: GMAW root + SAW fill/cap (max deposition rate).
- **Aplikacje**:
  - **Pipe spool fabrication** dla refinery, chemical, power plant.
  - **Pressure vessel longitudinal welds**.
  - **Heat exchanger** tube-to-tubesheet (orbital robot variant).
  - **Wind tower** large diameter sections.
  - **Shipbuilding** — hull + decks.
- **ROI** dla robotic welding cells:
  - **Initial cost**: $200K-$1M dla complete cell.
  - **Productivity**: 2-5× manual welder.
  - **Quality consistency** — improved repeatability.
  - **Payback**: 1-3 years dla high-volume shop.
- **Limitations**:
  - **Setup time** dla one-off parts uneconomical.
  - **Skill required** dla programming + maintenance.
  - **NOT field-deployable** typically.
  - **Special tooling** for complex spool geometries (custom fixtures).
- **Vendors / system integrators**:
  - **Novarc Technologies** — pipe spool specialists.
  - **Lincoln Electric Automation** — full integration.
  - **Miller Welding Automation**.
  - **ABB Robotics** — robot arm supplier.
  - **FANUC** — robot arm + cell integration.
  - **CLOOS / Yaskawa Motoman**.

### KKKKKKK. Hot-Dip Galvanizing Pipe — ASTM A123 / A123M
- **Hot-dip galvanizing (HDG)** = corrosion protection by **immersion of steel** w **molten zinc** at ~450°C. **Forms metallurgically-bonded zinc-iron alloy + pure zinc topcoat**. Different from coating (FBE/3LPE) — galvanizing is **integral metallurgical bond**, not painted layer.
- **ASTM A123/A123M** ("Standard Specification for Zinc (Hot-Dip Galvanized) Coatings on Iron and Steel Products") — primary standard. Other standards:
  - **ASTM A153** — galvanized fasteners (bolts, nuts).
  - **ASTM A767** — galvanized rebar.
  - **EN ISO 1461** — European equivalent.
- **Process steps** (typical HDG line):
  1. **Caustic cleaning** — degreasing tank (NaOH solution + surfactants). Removes oils, paint, dirt.
  2. **Acid pickling** — HCl bath (15-18%). Removes mill scale, rust.
  3. **Rinse** — fresh water.
  4. **Flux** — ZnCl₂ + NH₄Cl solution. Prevents oxidation between pickling + galvanizing.
  5. **Drying** — flash drying na hot oven (90-120°C).
  6. **Galvanizing bath** — immerse w molten zinc (445-465°C) for **3-10 minutes**:
     - **Zinc grade**: minimum **98% pure Zn** per ASTM B6.
     - **Additives**: Al (~0.01%) for brightness, Ni dla high-Si steel.
  7. **Withdrawal** — slow lift, allowing excess zinc to drain.
  8. **Quench** — typowo water bath dla cooling + passivation.
  9. **Inspection** — coating thickness measurement, visual appearance.
- **Coating thickness** (ASTM A123 minimum):

| Steel Thickness | Min coating thickness | Min coating mass |
|-----------------|----------------------:|-----------------:|
| < 1.6 mm | 45 µm | 320 g/m² |
| 1.6-3.2 mm | 65 µm | 470 g/m² |
| 3.2-4.8 mm | 75 µm | 540 g/m² |
| 4.8-6.4 mm | 85 µm | 610 g/m² |
| > 6.4 mm | 100 µm | 715 g/m² |

- **Service life** (atmospheric):
  - **Rural environment**: 75+ years dla 100 µm coating.
  - **Suburban**: 40-60 years.
  - **Urban / industrial**: 25-40 years.
  - **Marine**: 15-25 years.
- **Pipe-specific applications**:
  - **Galvanized pipe**: gas distribution (low pressure), water supply (deprecated dla potable due to Zn leaching), structural pipe (fence posts, scaffolding tube), sprinkler systems (NFPA 13).
  - **Galvanized fittings**: NPT-threaded malleable iron fittings.
  - **NIE używać** dla:
    - **Hot water** (Zn dissolves > 60°C).
    - **High-pressure steam** (galvanized layer fails at high T).
    - **Stainless service** (galvanic corrosion z stainless components).
- **Process advantages**:
  - **Inside + outside coated** simultaneously (full immersion).
  - **Corners + edges** covered uniformly (no thin spots).
  - **Sacrificial protection** — Zn corrodes preferentially over steel, protecting bare spots from scratches.
  - **Robust mechanical** — abrasion-resistant.
  - **Self-healing** small scratches (Zn migrates).
- **Process limitations**:
  - **Hydrogen embrittlement risk** — high-strength steels (> 1000 MPa yield) susceptible during pickling. Special procedures required.
  - **Distortion** — large thin-walled parts may warp from thermal cycling.
  - **Threaded ends** — galvanized layer may interfere z thread engagement. Tap threads after galvanizing.
  - **Welding HDG pipe** — Zn fumes toxic (metal fume fever). MUST remove HDG coating from weld area (5 cm minimum) before welding.
- **Welding HDG-coated pipe** (specialty):
  - **Grind off galvanizing** at weld area (5 cm each side).
  - **Weld normally**.
  - **Touch-up** weld area z **cold galvanizing compound** (zinc-rich paint, 95% Zn min).
  - **Local hot galvanizing** post-welding — rarely possible (whole assembly must re-galvanize).
- **Quality control** (inspection):
  - **Magnetic thickness gauge** — non-destructive.
  - **Microscope cross-section** — destructive, used dla certification samples.
  - **Adhesion test** — hammer test, knife test.
  - **Uniformity** — visible no bare spots, no excessive runs.
- **Vendors / processors**: regional galvanizers — typowo certified by AGA (American Galvanizers Association) lub equivalent national bodies.

### LLLLLLL. Welder Ergonomics — Workstation Design + Fatigue Prevention
- **Ergonomic challenges welder**:
  - **Repetitive motions** dla long welds.
  - **Awkward postures** — overhead, 6G, confined spaces.
  - **Static load** — holding helmet + torch w one position.
  - **Heat stress** — proximity to welding arc + ambient industrial heat.
  - **Cable + hose drag** — pulling welding cables.
  - **Heavy PPE** — leather jacket, helmet, boots add 5-10 kg of equipment.
- **Health consequences (MSDs — Musculoskeletal Disorders)**:
  - **Lower back pain** — bending over work, lifting.
  - **Neck strain** — forward head posture w helmet.
  - **Shoulder injuries** — overhead welding, repetitive arm motion.
  - **Wrist + forearm** — torch handling, fine motor control.
  - **Knee + hip** — kneeling, squatting w confined spaces.
- **Workstation design principles**:
  - **Work piece at WAIST-to-SHOULDER height**:
    - **Pipe positioner** rotates pipe so weld zone at optimal height (1.0-1.4 m).
    - **Height-adjustable benches** — raise / lower per welder height + task.
    - **Eliminate stooping** dla long welds.
  - **Operator can SIT or STAND** options:
    - **Welding stools** — height-adjustable, with back support.
    - **Sit-stand desks** w workshop.
    - **Foot rests** — relieve standing fatigue.
  - **Reachable storage**:
    - **Filler rod / wire** within arm's reach (no stretching).
    - **Helmet on hook** at chest height.
    - **Tools on belt / nearby tray**.
  - **Lighting**:
    - **Task lighting** > 1000 lux at workstation.
    - **No glare** sources behind welder (helmet darkens automatically, reflection problems).
    - **Color temperature** 4000-5000K (daylight).
- **Equipment selection** dla reducing strain:
  - **Auto-darkening helmet** — eliminates **head nodding** (saves 1000+ nods per shift dla repetitive welding).
  - **Lightweight helmet** — modern auto-darkening: 500-700 g (vs older fixed-shade 1000-1500 g).
  - **PAPR (Powered Air-Purifying Respirator)** — eliminates respirator straps, cools head, integrates z helmet.
  - **Lightweight welding gun** — modern MIG guns 0.5-1 kg.
  - **Spool gun** — eliminates long umbilical drag dla aluminum welding.
  - **Wireless remote control** — adjustment w hand without going to power supply.
  - **Counterbalance arms** — support gun + cable weight.
- **Positioning equipment**:
  - **Pipe positioner / rotator** (covered iter 27) — pipe rotates while welder stays stationary.
  - **Welding fixture / jig** — holds part w optimal orientation.
  - **Magnetic clamps** — eliminate manual holding.
  - **Auxiliary supports** — pipe weight not on welder's arms.
- **Schedule + breaks**:
  - **Task rotation** — switch between welding, fit-up, inspection w shift.
  - **Micro-breaks** — 30-second stretches every 30-60 min.
  - **Major breaks** — 15 min every 2 hours w intense welding.
  - **Hydration** — heat stress prevention.
- **Cable management**:
  - **Overhead reels** — cables drop from above, eliminate floor drag.
  - **Cable hooks / clips** on workstation.
  - **Strain relief** at gun connection.
  - **NIE depcać** kabli (insulation damage).
- **Statistics**:
  - **Welder MSD claims**: ~30-40% of all welding workforce reports back/neck issues over career.
  - **Productivity loss** from poor ergonomics: 5-20% per shift.
  - **Workers' compensation costs** w US: average claim $35K dla MSD (median).
- **Ergonomic improvements ROI**:
  - **Modest investment** (height-adjustable bench, lighter helmet, positioner): $5,000-15,000.
  - **Productivity gain**: 10-30%.
  - **MSD reduction**: 50-70% with comprehensive ergonomic program.
  - **Payback**: 6-18 months typowo.
- **Standards / guidelines**:
  - **ANSI/AIHA Z10** — Occupational Health & Safety Management Systems.
  - **CCOHS Welding Ergonomics** (Canada) — comprehensive guide.
  - **NIOSH Lifting Equation** — workplace lifting limits.
  - **OSHA ergonomics enforcement** — case-by-case, no specific standard (rule withdrawn 2001).
- **Self-assessment** dla welder (warning signs):
  - **Pain persisting** beyond rest period = consult MD.
  - **Numbness / tingling** = compression of nerve.
  - **Reduced grip strength** = repetitive strain.
  - **Fatigue out of proportion** do work intensity = ergonomic issue.

## Iteration 56 — 2026-05-25

### MMMMMMM. TEMA Heat Exchanger Designations — AES / BEM / BES (3-letter code)
- **TEMA (Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association)** — industry standard dla **shell-and-tube heat exchanger designations** + design + fabrication.
- **TEMA designation = 3-letter code**:
  - **1st letter** = **front head type** (stationary head, where tubes start).
  - **2nd letter** = **shell type** (cylindrical body containing tubes).
  - **3rd letter** = **rear head type** (where tubes end, opposite from front).
- **Front head types** (1st letter):
  - **A** — channel + removable cover. Most accessible dla tube cleaning + inspection.
  - **B** — bonnet (integral cover). Cheaper than A. Less access.
  - **C** — channel integral z tubesheet + removable cover. Welded tubesheet, removable channel.
  - **N** — channel integral z tubesheet, integral cover. Welded to shell.
  - **D** — special high-pressure closure (breech-lock, hydroclave) dla > 1000 psi.
- **Shell types** (2nd letter):
  - **E** — one-pass shell. **MOST COMMON**, simplest.
  - **F** — two-pass shell z longitudinal baffle. Better dla equal flow distribution.
  - **G** — split flow shell.
  - **H** — double-split flow shell.
  - **J** — divided flow shell (kettle-style w some variants).
  - **K** — kettle reboiler shell. Vapor space at top dla evaporation.
  - **X** — cross flow shell (no baffles).
- **Rear head types** (3rd letter):
  - **L** — fixed tubesheet, A-type cover (matches A-front).
  - **M** — fixed tubesheet, B-type bonnet (matches B-front).
  - **N** — fixed tubesheet, N-type integral cover.
  - **P** — outside-packed floating head.
  - **S** — internal split-ring floating head.
  - **T** — pull-through floating head.
  - **U** — U-tubes (bent at rear, no separate rear tubesheet).
  - **W** — packed externally tubesheet floating head.
- **Common combinations + their meanings**:
  - **AES** — A front (removable channel), E shell (one-pass), S rear (internal split-ring floating head):
    - **Removable bundle** dla cleaning both sides.
    - **Fouling service** (refinery crude preheaters, hot oil services).
    - **Standard dla refineries**.
  - **BEM** — B front (bonnet), E shell, M rear (fixed tubesheet, B-cover):
    - **Fixed tubesheet** — bundle NIE removable (welded).
    - **CHEAPEST** design.
    - **Clean service** only (steam condenser, water cooling clean side).
    - **Low pressure, low T**.
  - **BES** — B front, E shell, S rear (split-ring floating head):
    - Bundle removable.
    - **High thermal differential** OK (floating head accommodates expansion).
    - Less expensive than AES (B-front cheaper niż A).
  - **AEU** — A front, E shell, U-tubes:
    - **U-tube bundle** — single tubesheet, bent tubes at far end.
    - **Bundle removable**.
    - **Limited cleaning** (cannot mechanically clean inside U-bends).
    - **Lower cost** than AES.
  - **AKU** — A front, K shell (kettle), U-tubes:
    - **Kettle reboiler** — vaporizes shell-side fluid.
    - **Common dla distillation tower reboilers**.
  - **BEU** — B front, E shell, U-tubes — economical, U-tube benefits.
- **Selection guidance**:
  - **Clean service + low cost**: BEM (fixed).
  - **Standard refinery fouling service**: AES.
  - **Large temperature differential**: AEU lub BES (floating head / U-tube).
  - **High pressure**: AES with thick tubesheet + special seals.
  - **Vaporization (reboiler)**: AKU, BKU.
- **Construction notes**:
  - **Tube layouts**: triangular (more tubes per shell area, fouling-prone), square (less tubes, easier mechanical cleaning).
  - **Tube pitch**: tubeOD × 1.25-1.5 (triangular), tubeOD × 1.25 (square).
  - **Pass arrangement**: 1, 2, 4, 6, 8 tube passes (more passes = higher heat transfer + pressure drop).
- **TEMA class** (durability classification):
  - **Class R** — Refinery service. Most stringent, high pressure/temperature + cyclic.
  - **Class C** — Commercial / general service. Moderate.
  - **Class B** — Chemical processing.
- **Standards**:
  - **TEMA 10th Edition (2019)** — current.
  - **ASME Section VIII Div 1** — pressure vessel rules apply.
  - **ASME B16.5** — flange dimensions.
  - **ASTM A179, A213, A249** — tube materials.

### NNNNNNN. Process Analyzer Shelter / Sample Conditioning House
- **Process analyzer shelter (PAS / analyzer house)** = dedicated enclosure containing **online process analyzers** + **sample conditioning system (SCS)** + utilities. Standard dla:
  - **Continuous gas chromatograph (GC)** — composition analysis dla custody transfer, process control.
  - **Mass spectrometer** — specialized chemical detection.
  - **Moisture analyzer** — gas dryers, refrigeration loops.
  - **Sulfur analyzer (UV fluorescence, X-ray)** — fuel quality.
  - **pH / conductivity** — water treatment.
  - **Vibration / FTIR** — multi-stream monitoring.
- **Why shelter needed**:
  - **Protect sensitive analyzers** od weather (rain, sun, humidity), dust.
  - **Temperature control** — analyzers need stable 20-25°C dla accuracy.
  - **Safety** — flammable gas presence requires Ex-rated environment (Class 1 Div 2 typowo).
  - **Sample integrity** — minimize sample line length (analyzer close to tap).
- **Shelter types**:
  - **Walk-in analyzer house** — full-room (2.4 × 2.4 m typowo, do 6 × 3 m large).
  - **Walk-up open-air shelter** — three walls + roof, sides exposed.
  - **SFE (Small Footprint Enclosure)** — wall-mounted cabinet, no walk-in space.
  - **Double-door cabinet** — outdoor mounted, weather-protected analyzer w cabinet.
- **Design standards**:
  - **NFPA 496** — purged + pressurized enclosures dla hazardous locations.
  - **IEC 61285** — analyzer cabinet design.
  - **ATEX directive** (EU) — explosion-proof equipment.
  - **API RP 555** — process analyzers in refining.
- **Internal layout** dla walk-in shelter:
  - **Unobstructed headroom** ≥ 2 m.
  - **Floor space**: workbench + analyzer racks + utility panels.
  - **Sample lines** routed along walls / ceiling.
  - **Air conditioning** — split system lub package unit.
  - **Lighting** — emergency battery backup.
  - **Exhaust ventilation** — purge of any leakage gases.
- **Materials**:
  - **Galvanized steel** — most common, economical.
  - **Stainless steel** — refinery / corrosive areas.
  - **GRP (Glass Reinforced Plastic)** — chemical industry.
  - **Concrete** — fire-resistant, permanent installations.
- **Sample conditioning system (SCS)** integrated z shelter:
  - **Pre-filter** — particulate removal (5-50 µm).
  - **Pressure regulator** — sample pressure reduction (typowo 50-100 psig → 10-30 psig).
  - **Temperature conditioning** — heater (avoid liquid condensation w gas service) lub cooler.
  - **Liquid knockout** — drain trapped liquids.
  - **Flow regulator** — constant flow rate dla analyzer.
  - **Vent / drain** — sample disposal safely.
- **Sample lines**:
  - **Material**: 316 SS most common, special alloys dla aggressive.
  - **Diameter**: small (1/8" to 1/2") dla quick response time.
  - **Heated tracing** if needed (prevent freezing, condensation).
  - **Cabinet penetrations** sealed (NFPA 496 compliance).
- **Utility connections**:
  - **Electrical**: 110V / 220V main + uninterruptible power supply (UPS).
  - **Instrument air**: dla pneumatic operations + purge.
  - **Carrier gas** (helium, hydrogen, nitrogen) dla GC.
  - **Calibration gas cylinders** — typowo housed OUTSIDE shelter (safety) z fume cabinet.
  - **Communication** — fiber optic / Ethernet / 4-20 mA do DCS.
- **Hazardous area classification**:
  - **Inside shelter**: pressurized + purged → **non-hazardous** internally despite external environment.
  - **Outside shelter** (vent stack, sample relief): may release combustibles → Class 1 Div 1 Zone 0/1.
- **HSE features**:
  - **Gas detectors** — LEL + toxic.
  - **Emergency stop** — kill power + close sample valves.
  - **Fire suppression** — sometimes Inergen / FM-200 dla critical equipment protection.
  - **Eyewash + safety shower** w large shelters.
- **Maintenance**:
  - **Walk-in access** dla calibration + repair without weather exposure.
  - **Sample taps** valved + accessible.
  - **Spare parts** stored w cabinets w shelter.
- **Vendors**: Modcon Systems, Autocontrol, Cherokee Measurement, ASaP, Petrointech, Tricore IMS.

### OOOOOOO. Pipe Whip Restraints — Nuclear LOCA Safety Equipment
- **Pipe whip** = **violent motion of ruptured pipe** caused by **blowdown thrust force** (escaping fluid reaction). **NIE problem normalnego pipingu** — strictly nuclear + high-energy pressure piping w specific aplikacje.
- **Critical scenario**: **LOCA (Loss of Coolant Accident)** w nuclear power plant — large coolant pipe ruptures, hot pressurized water/steam escapes, ruptured pipe end **whips violently** w arc-trajectory, potentially impacting:
  - **Other safety-related piping** (cascading failure).
  - **Containment structure** (penetration breach risk).
  - **Reactor vessel** (catastrophic).
  - **Electrical cables**, instrumentation (loss of monitoring).
- **Pipe whip restraint (PWR)** = mechanical device designed do **absorb kinetic energy** of whipping pipe + **limit motion** within acceptable envelope. Located strategically along high-energy piping.
- **Design philosophy**:
  - **Engineered plastic deformation** — restraint material yields w plastic range, absorbs kinetic energy without breaking.
  - **NOT elastic deformation** — elastic strain doesn't absorb enough energy.
  - **Final position predictable** — pipe end stops in known location.
- **Types of restraints**:
  - **U-bolt restraint (tension-based)**:
    - **U-bolt** wraps around pipe, anchored to structural backing.
    - Pipe motion → U-bolt stretches → plastic deformation → energy absorbed.
    - **Multiple U-bolts** in series dla higher energy absorption.
    - **Initial clearance** between pipe + restraint typowo 0.5-3 mm — allows normal thermal expansion movement without engaging restraint.
  - **H-type restraint (pressure-based)**:
    - **H-shape steel frame** wraps around pipe.
    - **Compression deformation** of frame absorbs energy via crushing.
    - **High energy absorption per device**.
    - **Less precise** — pipe final position less predictable.
  - **Energy-absorbing strut** — cylindrical tube z plastically-deforming core (honeycomb, foam).
  - **Snubber-based** — hydraulic / mechanical snubbers (also dla seismic + thermal motion control).
- **Design parameters**:
  - **Blowdown thrust force** = ρ × V² × A + (P − P_atm) × A:
    - Initial blowdown = jet of fluid out broken end.
    - Force may reach **2-5 MN dla large pipes** (DN 800+ steam).
  - **Pipe end velocity** at full rupture: ~10-50 m/s dla high-pressure steam.
  - **Restraint strength** > blowdown force.
  - **Restraint stiffness** controls displacement (typowo 50-200 mm allowed motion).
- **Material selection**:
  - **Carbon steel** — typowo ASTM A36 lub A516 dla restraint frames.
  - **High-yield steel** dla U-bolts (ductile failure mode).
  - **Energy absorber inserts** — honeycomb aluminum lub steel.
- **Placement strategy**:
  - **Both sides of postulated break locations** (downstream + upstream of guillotine break).
  - **Tees, elbows, reducers** — high-stress locations more break-prone.
  - **Near sensitive equipment** — restrict motion away from safety systems.
  - **Containment penetrations** — protect penetration boundary.
- **Postulated break locations**:
  - **Guillotine break** (full circumferential rupture) — worst case dla pipe whip.
  - **Longitudinal split** — less whipping, more focused jet impingement.
  - **Branch connections** — common stress concentration sites.
- **Design code references**:
  - **ASME Section III, Subsection NB** — Class 1 Nuclear piping.
  - **NRC NUREG-0800** — Standard Review Plan (regulatory).
  - **MEB 3-1** — Mechanical Engineering Branch position on pipe break.
- **Other nuclear-specific piping considerations**:
  - **Snubbers** — restrain piping during seismic events, allow thermal expansion in normal operation.
  - **Jet impingement shields** — barriers against escaping fluid jet (separate from whip restraint).
  - **Leak-before-break (LBB)** — design philosophy demonstrating crack leaks detectably before catastrophic rupture, eliminating need dla some PWRs.
- **Inspection + testing**:
  - **Periodic visual inspection** — confirm no premature damage (excessive thermal motion).
  - **Clearance verification** — between pipe + restraint maintained.
  - **Material certification** — restraint components z documented properties.
  - **Computational modeling** (FEA — Finite Element Analysis) dla verification.
- **Aplikacje (limited beyond nuclear)**:
  - **Aerospace** — fuel + hydraulic line restraints w jets.
  - **High-pressure steam plants** — niektóre power plants z elevated risk.
  - **Subsea oil & gas** — niektóre critical lines.

## Iteration 57 — 2026-05-25

### PPPPPPP. Pipe Nipples — Close / Short / Long / Hex (TBE / TOE)
- **Pipe nipple** = **short length of pipe** z **NPT threads** at one or both ends. Standard fittings dla:
  - Connecting two pipe fittings.
  - Extending fitting reach (e.g., flange to valve).
  - Tank / vessel connections.
  - Branch take-offs.
- **Standard pipe nipple sizes**: NPS 1/8" do 4" (DN 6 do DN 100). Larger sizes use full pipe joints + couplings instead.
- **Materials**:
  - **Black iron / CS** — standard utility (gas, oil, water).
  - **Galvanized steel** — water service, mild environments.
  - **316 / 304 SS** — corrosion resistance.
  - **Brass** — water service plumbing.
  - **PVC / CPVC** — non-pressure / low-pressure water.
- **Manufacturing**:
  - **From standard pipe stock** (Schedule 40, 80, 160).
  - **Threads cut** per ASME B1.20.1 (NPT tapered threads).
  - **NPT thread**: 1°47'24" taper (3°35' included angle).
- **Nipple types by length + thread configuration**:
  - **CLOSE nipple** — **shortest possible**:
    - Threads almost meet w middle.
    - **NO unthreaded section** — entire length threaded.
    - **Aplikacja**: tight spaces, direct fitting-to-fitting connection.
    - **Standard length**: DN 25 close nipple = ~25 mm long total.
    - **Hex section** — short close has hex midsection dla wrench grip.
  - **SHORT (shoulder) nipple**:
    - Slightly longer than close.
    - **Short unthreaded band** w middle (~10-15 mm).
    - **Wrench grip** on unthreaded section.
    - **More structurally robust** than close.
  - **LONG nipple**:
    - **Specified by total length** — e.g., "1/2" × 4" long nipple".
    - **Threaded ends** + **long unthreaded middle**.
    - **Standard lengths**: 1.5", 2", 2.5", 3", 4", 5", 6", 8", 10", 12".
  - **Cut-to-length**: dla custom lengths, cut and thread from pipe stock.
  - **HEX nipple**:
    - **Hexagonal section** w middle dla wrench grip.
    - **Threaded ends only**.
    - **Long hex nipple**: longer between threads + hex.
  - **REDUCING nipple**:
    - **Different thread sizes** on each end (e.g., 1/2" × 3/4" reducer nipple).
    - **Aplikacja**: connecting different size fittings without separate reducer.
  - **SWAGE nipple** (NOT threaded — different concept):
    - **Reducer pipe** swaged at one end.
    - **One end Plain (PE)** lub **Beveled (BE)** dla welding.
    - **Other end same OD** as pipe.
    - **NPT-threaded** versions also available.
- **End preparation abbreviations**:
  - **TBE (Threaded Both Ends)** — male NPT on both ends. Standard nipple.
  - **TOE (Threaded One End)** — only one end threaded; other end **plain** lub **beveled**.
  - **PE (Plain Ends)** — no threads on either end (rare).
  - **BE (Beveled End)** — prepared for welding (37.5° bevel typowo).
- **Pressure ratings**:
  - **Schedule 40** — standard pressure (Class 150-300).
  - **Schedule 80** — higher pressure (Class 600-1500).
  - **Schedule 160** — extreme pressure (Class 2500+).
  - **Threaded joints** w general weaker niż butt-welded.
- **Service limitations**:
  - **Maximum temperature**: typowo 250°C dla CS NPT joints (above = thread sealing degrades).
  - **NIE dla cyclic / vibration** services — threaded joints loosen.
  - **NIE dla sour service** above ~5 ppm H₂S (NACE concerns).
- **Sealing**: Teflon tape lub pipe dope (per iter 11).
- **Standards**:
  - **ASME B16.11** — Forged Fittings, Socket-Welding and Threaded.
  - **ASME B1.20.1** — Pipe Threads, General Purpose (NPT).
  - **ASTM A733** — Welded and Seamless Carbon Steel and Austenitic Stainless Steel Pipe Nipples.
- **Common quantity per project**:
  - **Refinery / chem plant**: 1000-5000+ nipples (instrument connections, vents, drains, drainage).
  - **Most are**: 1/2" - 1" w schedule 80 dla critical services.
- **Storage**: keep dry (rust prevention dla CS), threaded end caps prevent thread damage.

### QQQQQQQ. Pipe Vibration Analysis — FFT + Resonance Troubleshooting
- **Pipe vibration** = oscillatory motion of piping system caused by:
  - **Pump-induced** — rotating + reciprocating equipment.
  - **Flow-induced** — turbulence, cavitation, slug flow.
  - **Acoustic** — pulsations (covered iter 44).
  - **Mechanical resonance** — pipe natural frequency match with forcing frequency.
  - **Wind-induced** — vortex shedding na exposed long unsupported runs.
- **Consequences**:
  - **Fatigue cracking** at flanges, welds, fittings.
  - **Equipment damage** — pump bearings, valve seats.
  - **Loose connections** — flanges, threaded fittings.
  - **Noise** — exposure issue dla personnel.
  - **Foundation damage** — vibrations transmitted to building / supports.
- **Measurement equipment**:
  - **Triaxial accelerometer** — measures vibration in 3 axes (X, Y, Z) simultaneously.
  - **Magnetic mount** — sticks to ferromagnetic pipe.
  - **Glue mount** dla non-magnetic (SS).
  - **Stud mount** — most accurate dla high-frequency.
  - **Hand-held data collector** — Bently Nevada Snapshot, Emerson AMS, SKF Microlog.
- **Measurement units**:
  - **Displacement [µm peak-to-peak]** — low frequencies (< 10 Hz).
  - **Velocity [mm/s RMS]** — most common dla industrial pipe (10-1000 Hz).
  - **Acceleration [g, m/s²]** — high frequencies (> 1000 Hz).
- **FFT (Fast Fourier Transform) analysis**:
  - **Time domain signal** (raw vibration vs time) → frequency domain spectrum.
  - **Identifies discrete frequencies** of vibration.
  - **Spectrum peaks** correlate to specific causes:
    - **1× running speed** — imbalance.
    - **2× running speed** — misalignment.
    - **Vane pass frequency** (RPM × # vanes) — pump hydraulic.
    - **Bearing frequencies** (BPFO, BPFI, BSF, FTF) — bearing defects.
    - **Tooth meshing** (RPM × # teeth) — gearbox.
    - **Slot pass** — electrical motor issues.
- **Resonance analysis**:
  - **Natural frequency** of pipe section — function of mass, stiffness, length, supports.
  - **Common formula** (simply supported pipe): f_n = (π/2L²) × √(EI/ρA) gdzie L=length between supports, EI=stiffness, ρA=mass per length.
  - **Forced frequency** = source frequency (pump speed × harmonics, pulsation frequencies).
  - **Resonance** = natural freq = forced freq → amplification 10-100×.
- **Vibration limits** (ISO 10816 reference dla rotating equipment, adapted dla piping):
  - **Class A (good)**: < 2.3 mm/s RMS.
  - **Class B (acceptable)**: 2.3-4.5 mm/s.
  - **Class C (alarm)**: 4.5-7.1 mm/s.
  - **Class D (trip)**: > 7.1 mm/s.
  - **API 618 (pulsation)**: 0.4 mm/s velocity peak limit dla pipe.
- **Troubleshooting workflow**:
  1. **Walk-down** — visual inspection identifying high-amplitude points.
  2. **Quick measurement** at multiple points — identify worst location.
  3. **Detailed FFT analysis** at worst point — identify frequencies.
  4. **Forcing function check** — speed, valve cycling, pulsation.
  5. **Natural frequency test** — bump test (impact pipe, measure decay) — modal analysis.
  6. **Diagnosis**: source of vibration + whether resonance.
  7. **Remediation** designed based on cause.
- **Remediation methods**:
  - **Add support** — change natural frequency.
  - **Remove support** — change natural frequency (sometimes).
  - **Add mass / clamp** — change natural frequency.
  - **Pulsation dampener** (recip pump source).
  - **Orifice / restriction** — damping at specific resonance.
  - **Pipe rerouting** — change path, eliminate resonant sections.
  - **VFD on pump** — change operating speed, avoid resonance band.
  - **Equipment isolation** — flexible coupling, expansion joint between pump + pipe.
- **CAESAR II vibration analysis** (covered iter 42) — predicts vibration during design.
- **Software**:
  - **AMS Suite** (Emerson).
  - **SKF @ptitude Analyst**.
  - **Bently Nevada System 1**.
  - **National Instruments LabVIEW** (custom solutions).
- **Personnel**: Vibration Analyst certifications **ISO 18436-2 Cat I-IV** (similar do NDE levels).

### RRRRRRR. Welding Cable Connectors + Crimp Lugs — Lenco/Tweco Style
- **Welding cable termination** = connecting welding cable z power source + electrode holder / ground clamp / accessory. **Quality of termination critical** dla:
  - **Current flow** — poor connection = voltage drop, heat at connection.
  - **Cable life** — strain relief, no insulation damage.
  - **Operator safety** — touch protection, secure connections.
- **Standard connector types**:
  - **Lenco connectors** (E.G. Lenco / Sirius brand) — original cam-lock design.
  - **Tweco connectors** — popular USA brand z slightly different cam profile.
  - **Lenco + Tweco** sometimes interchangeable (most common style globally).
  - **Cam-Lok (Marechal / Cooper Crouse-Hinds)** — larger industrial cam connectors.
  - **Dinse connectors** (Germany) — European standard, small diameter.
  - **NEMA W series**.
- **Connector parts**:
  - **Cable lug** (crimp end) — copper barrel z hex / square profile dla crimping onto cable strands.
  - **Cable connector body** — brass cam-action mechanism.
  - **Insulating boot** — rubber sleeve protecting connection.
  - **Stud** (other side) — connects do welder lug, electrode holder, ground clamp.
- **Wire size compatibility**:
  - **#4 to 1/0 AWG** — small connectors.
  - **2/0 to 4/0 AWG** — large industrial connectors.
  - **Sized match** required dla cable diameter (too small = squeeze damage strands; too large = loose, poor electrical contact).
- **Crimping installation procedure**:
  1. **Strip cable**: remove insulation (typowo 25-50 mm length), don't nick copper strands.
  2. **Inspect strands**: all intact, twisted slightly tight.
  3. **Insert do lug barrel**: full insertion, no strands sticking out.
  4. **Crimp tool**: matched dla lug size (hex, indent, hydraulic):
     - **Hex crimp** — 6 indentations around barrel (standard).
     - **Indent crimp** — single deep indentation.
     - **Hydraulic crimper** dla larger sizes (2/0+).
  5. **Verify crimp**: pull test (cable should not slip), visual (uniform compression).
  6. **Heat shrink** over connection — covers crimp lug, prevents corrosion + shock.
  7. **Quality check**: continuity test (ohmmeter), low resistance < 0.1 Ω total path.
- **Soldering alternative** (less common, more time):
  - Tin cable strands.
  - Insert into lug.
  - Heat lug + cable, apply solder.
  - **Brittleness** issue (soldered joint vibration cracks).
  - **Crimping preferred** dla industrial.
- **Cable connector** (in-line cable splice):
  - **Male + female** halves twist + lock together (cam action).
  - **High amperage capacity** — 200-600 A typowo.
  - **Quick connect / disconnect** without tools.
  - **Allow extending cable** lub changing accessories quickly.
- **Common failure modes**:
  - **Poor crimp** — high resistance heats up under load, melts insulation, fire risk.
  - **Loose connector** — high current, arcing, damaged contacts.
  - **Damaged insulation** — exposed copper, shock hazard, short circuit.
  - **Corrosion** at lug-cable interface — increases resistance over time.
- **Maintenance**:
  - **Visual inspection** weekly — look dla burns, damaged insulation, loose mounts.
  - **Continuity test** quarterly — verify low resistance.
  - **Heat scan** while welding — IR camera detects hot connections (failures imminent).
  - **Re-terminate** if any concerns — relatively quick fix.
- **Cable length effects** (per iter 30):
  - **Voltage drop** increases z length — cable size up dla longer leads.
  - **Connector resistance** adds to total — multiple connectors w series compound issue.
- **Standards**:
  - **NEC** (National Electrical Code) — electrical safety.
  - **UL listings** dla welding cable + connectors.
  - **AWS / ANSI** dla welding-specific products.

## Iteration 58 — 2026-05-25

### SSSSSSS. MIG Wire Feed Troubleshooting — Bird's Nest + Kink Prevention
- **Wire feed mechanism** = krytyczny system w GMAW/FCAW. Drive rolls + liner + contact tip muszą działać razem.
- **Symptom: "bird's nest"** = wire tangles between drive rolls + gun, looks like tangled bird's nest of wire. **STOP welding immediately** — risk of damage.
- **Causes + fixes**:
  - **Excessive drive roll tension**:
    - **Symptom**: wire shaving, copper coating peeling, drive rolls grooved.
    - **Fix**: back off tension, set to minimum that prevents slipping.
    - **Test**: pinch wire with thumb between rolls + gun trigger pulled — wire should NOT push back (just stop). If wire shaves under thumb pressure → too tight.
  - **Insufficient drive roll tension**:
    - **Symptom**: wire feed inconsistent, arc sputters.
    - **Fix**: increase tension slightly.
  - **Wrong drive roll groove**:
    - **U-groove (smooth)** dla solid wire (steel, SS, aluminum).
    - **V-groove (sharp)** dla hard wire (steel).
    - **Knurled** dla flux-cored wire (softer surface, needs traction).
    - **Wrong size** — 0.035" roll forcing 0.024" wire = tangles; 0.035" wire na 0.024" roll = overflow.
  - **Liner issues**:
    - **Kinks** w gun cable (rolled up tight, dropped on floor).
    - **Plugged liner** — accumulated copper shavings, dust.
    - **Wear/old liner** — internal scoring, polish from years of use.
    - **Fix**: blow compressed air through, replace if damaged.
  - **Contact tip problems**:
    - **Spatter blockage** — molten metal solidified w tip ID.
    - **Wrong tip size** — 0.040" tip dla 0.035" wire = poor electrical contact + birdnest above tip.
    - **Worn tip** — oversize ID, arc wandering.
    - **Fix**: replace contact tip frequently (consumable, $1-3 each).
  - **Wire spool problems**:
    - **Tangled spool** — wire crossed over itself on spool.
    - **Bent wire end** (from kink).
    - **Spool brake too tight** — wire can't release smoothly.
  - **Wire chemistry / quality**:
    - **Rusty wire** — surface corrosion w humid storage.
    - **Old wire** — work-hardened, brittle.
    - **Quality issues** — cheap wire may have inconsistent diameter.
- **Diagnostic workflow**:
  1. **Stop welding immediately** if bird's nest seen.
  2. **Cut tangled wire** at drive rolls — clear mess.
  3. **Inspect drive rolls** — visible damage, contamination.
  4. **Check contact tip** — clear?
  5. **Run wire** without arc (purge) — verify smooth feed.
  6. **Restart welding** at low parameters, verify normal feed.
- **Prevention practices**:
  - **Daily**: visual inspection of liner + tip, clean spatter.
  - **Weekly**: blow out liner z compressed air.
  - **Monthly**: replace contact tip (regardless of wear) dla heavy use.
  - **Quarterly**: replace liner.
  - **Use proper drive roll** dla wire type.
  - **Tension setting per manufacturer** spec.
  - **Storage**: keep wire spools dry (humidity-controlled if possible).
- **Drive roll types**:
  - **Two-roll system**: simpler, lighter feeding.
  - **Four-roll system**: better grip, used dla aluminum + flux-cored wires (avoid wire crush).
- **Aluminum-specific feed challenges**:
  - **Soft wire** — easily kinks, deforms.
  - **Use 4-roll drive** + **U-groove plastic rolls**.
  - **Teflon liner** (NOT steel monocoil) — slick, low friction.
  - **Push-pull gun** — feed motor IN gun + at welder = synchronized force.
- **Spool gun option** (aluminum + Al-MIG):
  - **Wire spool MOUNTED on gun** — only 10 cm of wire travel between spool + tip.
  - **Eliminates liner issues** — wire travels nowhere.
  - **Bulky gun**, harder dla tight spaces.

### TTTTTTT. HVAC Sheet Metal Ductwork — Different from Pipe Joining
- **HVAC ductwork** = sheet metal channels conveying **air** (heated, cooled, ventilation). Different from pipe (carries liquids, gases under pressure). **Same fabricator workforce** often handles both.
- **Construction materials**:
  - **Galvanized steel** — STANDARD, low corrosion. Sheet gauges: 16-30 (heavier = larger duct + higher pressure).
  - **Stainless steel 304** — kitchen exhaust (grease), corrosive environments, hospital / pharma.
  - **Aluminum** — lighter weight, marine + corrosive.
  - **Pre-insulated panel (ductboard)** — flexible insulated panel ducts.
  - **Flex duct** — wire helix + fabric, dla short runs.
- **Duct shapes**:
  - **Rectangular** — most common dla high air flow.
  - **Round (spiral seam)** — better static pressure, easier sealing.
  - **Oval (flat-oval)** — compromise between round + rectangular.
  - **Square** — niche aplikacje.
- **Common seam types** (joining sheet metal panels):
  - **Pittsburgh lock** — STANDARD dla longitudinal seams w rectangular duct. **Heavy gauge** application. **Female pocket fold** locks over **male edge** when hammered + pressed.
  - **Snap lock** — **smaller duct, lighter gauge**. Male button snaps into female pocket when pressed together. **Audible snap** confirms locked seam.
  - **Standing seam** — rectangular duct, edges folded up + crimped (raised seam).
  - **Drive cleat** — alternative seam, slide-on assembly.
- **Transverse joints** (connecting duct sections):
  - **TDC (Transverse Duct Connector)** — slip-on rolled flanges with corner pieces.
  - **TDF (Tail-on-Flange)** — flanges rolled directly into duct ends.
  - **Slip + Drive** — older method, slip joint + drive cleat seal.
  - **Snap lock joints** dla round duct.
- **Fabrication tools**:
  - **Pittsburgh lock former** — bench-mounted machine forms seam profile.
  - **Snap lock roll former** — small lock formed.
  - **Brake press** — bends sheet to angle.
  - **Slip roll** — round forming.
  - **Plasma / laser cutter** — modern CNC sheet metal cutting.
  - **Shear** — straight cuts.
- **Sealing**:
  - **Duct sealant (mastic)** — flexible silicone-like compound applied to seams.
  - **Foil tape** — covers seam, waterproof.
  - **Gaskets** — between transverse joints (TDC/TDF).
- **Pressure classes (SMACNA standards)**:
  - **Low pressure**: ≤ 2" w.g. (water gauge) ≈ 500 Pa.
  - **Medium**: 2-6" w.g.
  - **High**: > 6" w.g. (rare dla typical HVAC).
- **Standards**:
  - **SMACNA (Sheet Metal And Air Conditioning Contractors National Association)** — definitive HVAC sheet metal manuals.
  - **ANSI/ASHRAE** dla design.
  - **NFPA 90A/90B** dla fire safety.
- **Hangers + supports**:
  - **All-thread rod** + clip dla rectangular hanging.
  - **Strap iron** dla round (lighter loads).
  - **Spacing**: 8-12 ft typowo, closer dla larger duct.
- **Insulation** (commonly applied dla cooling ducts):
  - **External wrap** — fiberglass blanket z foil-faced jacket.
  - **Internal lining** — duct liner glued to internal surface (acoustic + thermal).
- **HVAC vs piping** key differences:
  - **HVAC sheet metal**: low pressure, gas (air), large diameters.
  - **Piping**: high pressure, liquid + dense gas, smaller diameters.
  - **Sheet metal worker (SMW)** trade vs **pipefitter (PFT)** trade — different unions, different skills.
- **Crossover**:
  - **Pipefitter** sometimes fabricates large round HVAC duct.
  - **SMW** sometimes installs small steam piping.
  - **Industrial sheet metal**: large-scale HVAC duct + dust collection systems.

### UUUUUUU. Tank Insulation Jacketing — Aluminum / Stainless Cladding
- **Jacketing (cladding)** = **outer protective metallic sleeve** wrapped around insulation on tanks, vessels, pipes. **Differs from insulation itself** — purely protective layer.
- **Functions**:
  - **Physical protection** — abrasion, impact damage.
  - **UV protection** — prevents insulation degradation in sunlight.
  - **Weather seal** — sheds rain / snow, keeps insulation dry.
  - **Personnel safety** — sharp insulation edges contained.
  - **Aesthetic** — clean industrial appearance.
- **Materials**:
  - **Aluminum jacketing** (most common):
    - **Alloys**: 1100 (pure Al), 3003 (Mn-alloy, slightly stronger), 5005.
    - **Thicknesses**: 0.016", 0.020", 0.024" (0.4-0.6 mm).
    - **Finishes**:
      - **Smooth** — economy, simple appearance.
      - **Stucco embossed** — slight texture, hides minor dents.
      - **3/16" corrugated (cross-crimped)** — wavy pattern, looks decorative + provides slight rigidity.
    - **Light weight** — ~5-10% mass of steel.
    - **Service life**: 20-40 years atmospheric.
  - **Stainless steel jacketing**:
    - **Grades**: **T-304** (standard), **T-316** (chloride / coastal / chemical).
    - **Per ASTM A240** — sheet specification.
    - **Thicker than Al** — 0.020-0.040" typical.
    - **Higher cost** (~3-5× aluminum) but better dla harsh chemistry.
    - **Service life**: 40-80 years.
  - **Galvanized steel** — economy option, indoor / mild environments.
  - **PVC jacket** — small diameter pipe + accessories, low budget.
  - **GRP / FRP jacket** — chemical environments.
- **Moisture barrier**:
  - **Standard aluminum** comes z **3-mil polyfilm laminate** on **inside surface**.
  - **Purpose**: prevent corrosion of jacketing inside surface + underlying pipe/vessel.
  - **Without moisture barrier**: aluminum + condensation = pitting corrosion + galvanic with steel substrate.
- **Installation procedure**:
  1. **Install insulation** (mineral wool, calcium silicate, etc. per iter 12).
  2. **Wrap insulation z waterproof outer wrap** (kraft paper lub vapor barrier film).
  3. **Cut jacketing sheet** to size (slightly longer than circumference).
  4. **Wrap around insulation**:
     - **Horizontal pipe**: align lap DOWNWARD (water sheds).
     - **Vertical pipe**: higher section overlaps lower (water can't enter).
     - **Tank shell**: top sheet overlaps bottom sheet w each ring.
  5. **Secure z bands** (stainless steel banding, 1/2" wide typowo, ratchet tensioned).
  6. **Caulk all seams** with **aluminum-pigmented silicone** (matches color, weather seal).
  7. **Tab dressings** w corners + at fittings.
- **Fittings + accessories**:
  - **Pre-formed elbow covers**, **tee covers**, **valve covers**.
  - **Manhole covers** dla tank manways.
  - **Removable insulation pillows / blankets** — w access locations (flanges, valves) dla maintenance.
  - **Vapor barriers** dla cryogenic — special multi-layer construction.
- **Banding**:
  - **Stainless steel** — most common, 1/2"-3/4" wide.
  - **Spacing**: 9-12" typowo, closer at seams.
  - **Tension**: hand-tight z banding tool, NOT excessive (deforms insulation).
- **Tank-specific jacketing**:
  - **Vertical tank shells**: rings of jacketing, top overlap bottom.
  - **Tank roofs**: sloped jacketing, water shed away from manways.
  - **Tank insulation thickness**: typowo 75-150 mm (3-6"), depends on T differential.
- **Maintenance**:
  - **Visual inspection** annually.
  - **Caulk replacement** every 5-10 years (silicone weather degrades).
  - **Repair localized damage** — patch / replace sections.
- **CUI risk** (Corrosion Under Insulation — covered iter 12):
  - **Jacketing alone NIE protects** od CUI — moisture ingress + temperature trap = corrosion behind jacket.
  - **CUI primer** wymagana on pipe/tank surface UNDER insulation (TSA — Thermal Spray Aluminum, lub epoxy heat-resistant).
- **Standards**:
  - **ASTM C1729** — aluminum jacketing specification.
  - **ASTM A240** — stainless sheet.
  - **NIA (National Insulation Association)** — best practices.
- **Vendors**:
  - **Johns Manville** — premier aluminum + SS jacketing.
  - **ITW Insulation Systems** — full range.
  - **Pittsburgh Corning** — Foamglas + jacketing.
  - **Owens Corning** — fiberglass + jacketing.
  - **General Insulation** — distribution.

## Iteration 59 — 2026-05-25

### VVVVVVV. Subsea Pipeline Installation — S-Lay / J-Lay / Reel Lay
- **Subsea (offshore) pipeline construction** = installation of pipeline **on seabed** od onshore landfall do offshore platform / FPSO / wellhead. Different from onshore (HDD/trenched) — pipe **laid from ship-mounted vessel**.
- **Three primary methods**:
  - **S-Lay** — pipe assembled horizontally na vessel deck, exits w S-curve do seabed.
  - **J-Lay** — pipe assembled near-vertically, exits straight down do seabed.
  - **Reel Lay** — pre-fabricated pipe on huge reel, unspooled at site.
- **S-Lay method** (most common):
  - **Construction**:
    - **Pipe joints** (typowo 12-24 m lengths) welded **end-to-end horizontally** na firing line.
    - **Multiple welding stations** along firing line (root, hot pass, fill, cap stations).
    - **Inspection stations** (RT lub AUT) między welding stations.
    - **Coating stations** (FBE field joint coating per iter 13).
    - **Tensioner cranes** — caterpillar-track devices grip pipe + control pay-out tension.
    - **Stinger** — steel guide structure extending od stern z roller supports, controls **overbend** as pipe exits ship.
    - **Sagbend** — curvature near seabed touchdown point, controlled by tension.
  - **S-curve geometry**:
    - **Overbend** at top (controlled by stinger).
    - **Straight transition** w deep water.
    - **Sagbend** at touchdown.
  - **Productivity**: 3-5 km/day pipe laid w typical conditions.
  - **Water depth**: typowo **suitable do ~600 m** (deeper requires too much tension dla S-curve geometry).
  - **Vessels**: Allseas Solitaire, Lorelay, Saipem Castorone, McDermott Lay Vessel 105.
- **J-Lay method** (deepwater specialty):
  - **Construction**:
    - **Pipe assembled w nearly-vertical mast** (60-90° from horizontal).
    - **Single welding station** at top of mast.
    - **Pipe exits straight down** — almost no overbend (only J-shape near seabed).
    - **Much lower tension** required vs S-Lay (pipe own weight, no overbend bending stress).
  - **Water depth**: typowo **> 500 m**, perfect dla **deepwater (1000-3000 m)**.
  - **Lower productivity** vs S-Lay (only one welding station, sequential).
  - **Productivity**: 1-2 km/day.
  - **Vessels**: Saipem 7000, Heerema Balder, McDermott DLV 2000.
- **Reel Lay method** (smaller diameters, fastest):
  - **Construction**:
    - **Pipeline assembled at onshore spool-base** (welded, coated, tested in shore facility).
    - **Spooled onto huge reel** (5-30 m diameter, holds 10-50 km pipe).
    - **Reel mounted on lay barge** + transported do field.
    - **Unspooled at site** — pipe passes through straightener (eliminates curvature memory) + over chute do seabed.
  - **Pipe diameter limit**: typowo **DN 100-400** (4"-16") — larger pipe too stiff dla reeling.
  - **Productivity**: VERY HIGH — **10-30 km/day** during unspooling (no field welding required).
  - **Limitation**: pipe needs to be flexible enough dla reeling (smaller diameters, lower wall thickness).
  - **Vessels**: Subsea 7 vessels (Apache, Skandi Acergy), Technip Apache II, Saipem FDS2.
- **Vessel + crew**:
  - **150-250 personnel** w 12-hour shifts.
  - **DP (Dynamic Positioning)** — vessels stay on station bez anchors (GPS + thrusters).
  - **Helicopter / supply boats** dla crew change, parts.
  - **Cost**: $300K-$1M per day operating costs (dla large lay vessels).
- **Pipeline construction quality**:
  - **Each weld**: automated UT (AUT) — phased array + TOFD per API 1104 + DNV-OS-F101.
  - **Defect repair**: cut out + re-weld → SLOW (5-15 min downtime każdy).
  - **Coating integrity** — holiday detection po field joint coating.
- **Standards**:
  - **DNV-OS-F101** — Submarine Pipeline Systems (most common dla offshore).
  - **API 1111** — Design, Construction, Operation of Offshore Hydrocarbon Pipelines.
  - **ISO 13623** — Pipeline Transportation Systems.
  - **API 17J/K** — Flexible riser specifications.
- **Other subsea features**:
  - **Anchor blocks / weight coating** — concrete coating dla negative buoyancy (pipe stays on seabed).
  - **Trenching + burial** — buried 1-3 m below seabed dla protection w shallow water.
  - **Riser** — vertical section from seabed do platform.
  - **PLET (Pipeline End Termination)** — manifold structure at end of pipeline.
  - **Tie-in spool** — short pipe section connecting pipelines lub manifolds.

### WWWWWWW. Slurry Pipeline — Mining + Mineral Processing
- **Slurry pipeline** = transports **mixture of solid particles + carrier liquid** (usually water). Different from clean fluid piping — **abrasion + sedimentation issues**.
- **Service examples**:
  - **Ore concentrates** — copper, iron, gold, lead-zinc concentrates from mine to port lub processing.
  - **Tailings** — waste material from milling, disposed do tailings pond.
  - **Coal slurry** — Coal Water Mixture transport.
  - **Cement slurry** — pre-mixed cement od plant.
  - **Sand / aggregate** — dredging operations.
  - **Wastewater w solids** — sewage transport.
- **Critical design challenges**:
  - **Erosion-corrosion** — solid particles wear away pipe wall + corrosive water medium.
  - **Sedimentation** — particles settle if velocity too low → clogging.
  - **Pump wear** — slurry abrades pump internals.
  - **Plugging risk** — solid concentration spikes can block pipe.
- **Pipe materials** (selection priorities w order):
  - **Carbon steel** — economical, ALE wymaga rapid wear:
    - **Service life**: months dla heavy abrasion (iron ore, sand).
    - **Internal lining** często wymagana.
  - **HDPE** — flexible, **smooth ID** (low friction), good dla mild abrasion:
    - **Service life**: 5-20 years dla coal slurry, mining tailings.
    - **NIE dla extreme abrasion** (iron ore, sand at high velocity).
    - **Limit**: pressure ~25 bar.
  - **UHMWPE (Ultra-High Molecular Weight Polyethylene)** — premium plastic:
    - **15× more abrasion-resistant than steel**.
    - **8× lighter than steel**.
    - **5× lower friction coefficient**.
    - Limit pressure: ~16 bar.
    - **Aplikacje**: mine tailings, ore slurry premium.
  - **Ceramic-lined steel** — best abrasion resistance:
    - **Alumina (Al₂O₃) tiles** lub **fused cast basalt** lining inside steel pipe.
    - **Service life**: 20-40 years dla extreme abrasion.
    - **Higher cost** (5-10× plain CS).
    - **Stosowane**: iron ore concentrate, copper concentrate, large mining operations.
  - **Rubber-lined steel** — moderate abrasion + corrosion:
    - **Natural rubber** lub neoprene liner.
    - **Resilient** — absorbs particle impact.
    - **Cost-effective** dla moderate-severity service.
  - **Wear-back / Trickle-fed slurry pipe** — special design with replaceable wear plate w bottom:
    - **Bottom half wears first** (particles settle).
    - **Rotate pipe periodically** dla even wear.
- **Critical velocity**:
  - **Below critical**: particles settle, pipe blocks.
  - **Above critical**: too much abrasion + pump energy.
  - **Optimum**: typowo **2-3 m/s** dla most slurries.
  - **Function of**: particle size, concentration, fluid viscosity, pipe diameter.
- **Slurry concentration**:
  - **Volumetric concentration** (Cv) — % volume solids w total.
  - **Mass concentration** (Cw) — % mass solids w total.
  - **Typical**: 30-50% solids by mass.
- **Pumps dla slurry**:
  - **Centrifugal slurry pump** — most common (Warman, Goulds, KSB Slurry).
  - **Positive displacement (piston / diaphragm)** — high pressure mining mainlines.
  - **Lined w hardened materials** — high-Cr iron, rubber, ceramic.
- **Long-distance slurry pipelines** (notable examples):
  - **Black Mesa coal slurry** (USA, 437 km, decommissioned).
  - **Samarco iron ore** (Brazil, 396 km — failed catastrophically 2015 dam breach).
  - **Antamina copper-zinc** (Peru, 302 km).
  - **Karakum Pipeline** — copper concentrate.
- **Maintenance**:
  - **Regular flushing** z clean water — between cycles.
  - **Pigging** — clean fluid pushes residual slurry, gauges thickness.
  - **UT thickness mapping** — frequent (3-6 months) dla wear monitoring.
  - **Replace worn sections** preemptively (less than full failure).
- **Hazards**:
  - **Pipeline leaks** — toxic process chemicals (cyanide w gold mining).
  - **Tailings dam failures** — catastrophic (Brumadinho 2019, Mariana 2015).
  - **High-pressure operations** — burst risk increased z abrasion thinning.

### XXXXXXX. Pre-Fab Paint Shop — Blast + Coating Operations
- **Pre-fab paint shop** = dedicated facility within (lub adjacent to) pipe spool fabrication where **completed welded spools are surface-prepared + coated**. **Different from field painting** — controlled environment, repeatable quality.
- **Process flow**:
  1. **Spool arrives** od welding bay z all welds completed + NDE passed.
  2. **Hot stamping** identification stamps verified.
  3. **Blast preparation** — typowo abrasive blasting dla profile.
  4. **First coat** — primer.
  5. **Second coat** — intermediate (optional).
  6. **Top coat** — finish color, weather/chemical resistant.
  7. **Cure** — ambient lub elevated T.
  8. **Inspection** — DFT (Dry Film Thickness), holidays, visual.
  9. **Tagging + shipping**.
- **Surface preparation** (KRYTYCZNE dla coating adhesion):
  - **SSPC (Society for Protective Coatings) cleanliness standards**:
    - **SSPC-SP1**: solvent cleaning (oil/dirt removal).
    - **SSPC-SP3**: power tool cleaning.
    - **SSPC-SP5 (Sa3)**: WHITE METAL blast — 100% clean, no rust, no residue. Premium.
    - **SSPC-SP10 (Sa2.5)**: NEAR-WHITE blast — slight staining OK. **STANDARD dla most industrial coatings**.
    - **SSPC-SP6 (Sa2)**: COMMERCIAL blast — moderate cleanliness.
    - **SSPC-SP7 (Sa1)**: BRUSH-OFF — minimum cleanliness.
  - **Surface profile** (anchor pattern):
    - **2-3 mil (50-75 µm)** depth — typowy dla industrial coatings.
    - **3-5 mil** dla high-build coatings.
    - **Measured z replica tape** (Testex Press-O-Film) → micrometer.
- **Blasting media**:
  - **Coal slag (Black Beauty)** — common, economical.
  - **Garnet** — durable, recyclable, mineral natural product.
  - **Steel grit** — high productivity, recyclable many times.
  - **Aluminum oxide** — premium, dla SS surfaces (NIE Fe contamination).
  - **Glass bead** — light cleaning, gentle.
  - **Plastic media** — paint removal without metal damage.
- **Blast room / booth**:
  - **Enclosed space** dla containment of media + dust.
  - **Recovery system** — media falls do floor grates, recovered for cleaning + reuse.
  - **Dust collector** — bag filter lub cartridge, prevents air pollution.
  - **PPE dla operator**: dust mask, hearing protection, helmet z fresh air supply.
  - **Sizes**: small booths 3×3×6 m, large 6×6×20 m dla full pipe spools.
- **Coating application** (paint booth):
  - **Spray methods**:
    - **Conventional air spray** — atomizes paint, air-propelled. Standard.
    - **Airless spray** — high-pressure pump (1500-3500 psi), pure paint atomization. Heavy industrial.
    - **HVLP (High Volume Low Pressure)** — high transfer efficiency, less overspray.
    - **Electrostatic spray** — charged paint particles attracted do grounded part. Premium efficiency.
    - **Powder coating** — electrostatic dry powder + oven cure (FBE — covered iter 13).
  - **Booth ventilation**: cross-draft lub down-draft. Filters capture overspray.
  - **Lighting**: explosion-proof (paint vapors ignite).
- **Coating systems** (typical industrial paint):
  - **Primer (1-2 coat)**:
    - **Zinc-rich epoxy** — sacrificial Zn protects steel even after damage. Standard offshore.
    - **Inorganic zinc** — similar but inorganic binder. Premium for highly corrosive.
    - **Red oxide primer** — economy.
  - **Intermediate coat (optional)**:
    - **High-build epoxy** — barrier coat, thick (5-15 mil).
  - **Top coat**:
    - **Polyurethane** — UV-resistant, color-stable, popular outdoor.
    - **Aliphatic polyurethane** — premium UV resistance.
    - **Epoxy** — chemical resistance, less UV stability.
    - **Acrylic** — economy, indoor / mild outdoor.
- **DFT (Dry Film Thickness) measurement**:
  - **Magnetic gauge** (PosiTector, Elcometer) — Fe substrate.
  - **Eddy current** dla non-magnetic (SS, Al).
  - **Spec**: typowo 8-16 mil total system thickness.
  - **Per coat**: measure between coats (wet + dry).
- **Holiday detection** (per iter 13) — UV-cured + epoxy systems for buried lines.
- **Personnel certifications**:
  - **NACE CIP (Coating Inspector Program)** Level 1-3.
  - **SSPC PCS (Protective Coatings Specialist)**.
  - **Frosio** (European certification).
- **Quality issues common**:
  - **Inadequate surface prep** — most common cause of coating failure (premature peel, blister).
  - **Excessive DFT** — sagging, cracking dla certain coatings.
  - **Insufficient DFT** — undercoat exposed.
  - **Overcoating intervals** — too long lub too short between coats (specific manufacturer recommended).
- **Productivity**:
  - **Small spool** (≤ DN 200): 30-60 min total prep + coat.
  - **Medium spool** (DN 200-500): 1-3 hours.
  - **Large spool** (≥ DN 600): half-day to full day.
- **Vendors / equipment**:
  - **Graco** — spray equipment.
  - **Wagner** — airless sprayers.
  - **Nordson** — powder coating.
  - **Global Finishing Solutions** — booths.
  - **Blastone** — blast equipment.
