## Iteration 60 — 2026-05-25

### YYYYYYY. Annubar / Averaging Pitot Tube — Insertion Flow Meter
- **Annubar / Averaging Pitot Tube (APT)** = **insertion flow meter** — measures flow via differential pressure across **multi-port sensor extending across pipe diameter**. Different from orifice plate (insert across pipe, NOT inline restriction).
- **Annubar** is brand name (Emerson Rosemount). Generic name: **averaging Pitot tube**.
- **Working principle**:
  - **Multiple sensing ports** on upstream side of sensor (5-7 typically).
  - **Each port samples velocity profile** at different radial position.
  - **All ports lead to single pressure tap** — effectively averages pressure across diameter.
  - **Downstream port** at low-pressure side.
  - **Differential pressure (ΔP)** correlates to flow rate via Bernoulli.
- **Single Pitot tube** by contrast:
  - **Single port** measures velocity at single point.
  - **Inadequate dla developed flow profile** w pipe — biased reading.
  - **Annubar averaging** corrects this.
- **Construction**:
  - **Sensor body** — extends radially across pipe diameter (orthogonal to flow).
  - **Mounting flange / fitting** — installed through pipe wall via tap.
  - **Sensor cross-sections**: T-shape, diamond, hourglass — different brands.
  - **Materials**: SS 316L most common, alloys dla harsh service.
- **Installation requirements (Annubar / APT)** — **straight pipe lengths**:

| Upstream disturbance | Min upstream (D) | Min downstream (D) |
|----------------------|-----------------:|-------------------:|
| **Pipe bend** (90° single) | 8 | 4 |
| **Pipe elbow (sharp)** | 23 | 4 |
| **Reducer / expander** | 12 / 18 | 4 |
| **Upstream valve** | 30 | 4 |
| **Two elbows in series** | 25-50 | 4 |

- **Installation procedure**:
  1. **Tap hole** wycina się w pipe wall — typowo gate valve mounted dla insertion under pressure.
  2. **Annubar assembly** wsuwany through tap.
  3. **Sensor tip** extends do OPPOSITE wall (full diameter spanning).
  4. **Pressure connections** to differential pressure transmitter.
  5. **Manifold (3-valve)** dla transmitter isolation + zero.
- **Aplikacje**:
  - **Liquids, gases, steam** — versatile media.
  - **Cooling water flow** — large pipe sizes.
  - **Steam line flow measurement**.
  - **Stack flow** dla emissions monitoring.
  - **HVAC duct flow** — large rectangular ducts.
- **Advantages**:
  - **Low pressure drop** — minimal flow restriction (vs orifice).
  - **Low cost installation** — no spool replacement, just tap.
  - **Retrofit-friendly** — installs on existing pipe.
  - **Large diameter friendly** — works well DN 100 do DN 6000+.
  - **Insertion under pressure** possible (hot tap fittings).
- **Limitations**:
  - **Lower accuracy** vs orifice (±1-3% vs ±0.5-1%).
  - **Wear** — sensor exposed do flow, erodes over time.
  - **Clogging** — particulates can plug sensor ports.
  - **Limited Re range** — turbulent flow required (Re > 10,000).
  - **Straight pipe** still required.
- **Calibration**:
  - **Theoretical** w clean conditions per ASME MFC standards.
  - **Wet calibration** w water flow lab dla critical service.
  - **In-field** vs reference meter checks recommended.
- **Maintenance**:
  - **Sensor cleaning** — port flushing.
  - **Inspection** — wear, deposition.
  - **Replacement** — sensor head every 5-15 years zależnie od service.
- **Vendors**:
  - **Emerson Rosemount Annubar** — premier (brand origin).
  - **ABB Torbar** — averaging Pitot.
  - **Veris Accelabar**.
  - **Krohne Pitot tubes**.

### ZZZZZZZ. Drift Mandrel + Pipe ID Verification — API 5L / 5CT
- **Drift mandrel** = **calibrated cylindrical rod** smaller than pipe nominal ID. Passed through pipe to verify **minimum ID clearance** without restriction.
- **Cel testing**:
  - **Verify no internal restriction** (kink, dent, ovality).
  - **Confirm pipe accepts string + workover tools** (downhole).
  - **Confirm pipe accepts pigs** (pipeline transmission).
  - **API monogram requirement** — pipe must drift to carry API certification.
- **API 5L (line pipe)** drift requirements:
  - **Drift mandrel diameter** = nominal pipe ID − specific deduction.
  - **Length** typically 6"-12" (152-305 mm).
  - **Pulled through** entire pipe length without binding.
- **API 5CT (casing + tubing)** drift requirements:
  - **More stringent** than 5L — downhole tools must pass through.
  - **Drift size table** specific dla each casing OD + weight per foot.
  - **Drift OD = casing ID − 1/4" minimum** (varies by casing class).
- **Drift mandrel materials**:
  - **Steel** — standard.
  - **Aluminum** — lighter (large diameter).
  - **Teflon / nylon** — softer, prevents scratching internal coating.
- **Drift test procedure**:
  1. **Verify drift size** matches pipe spec (per API table).
  2. **Pipe end inspection** — clean, no debris.
  3. **Drift attached do rod** lub pulled w rope.
  4. **Pulled through pipe** at controlled speed.
  5. **No binding allowed** — must pass freely.
  6. **Mark + repeat** dla other end.
  7. **Tag pipe** as "drifted" with date + size.
- **Failure modes**:
  - **Drift won't pass** = internal restriction:
    - **Dent** (impact damage).
    - **Ovality** out of spec.
    - **Weld root protrusion** w welded pipe.
    - **Internal scale / coating** uneven.
  - **Reject pipe** lub re-round / repair.
- **Drift table excerpt** (API 5CT, partial):

| Casing OD | Weight (lb/ft) | Drift Diameter |
|-----------|---------------:|---------------:|
| 4 1/2" | 9.5 | 3.795" |
| 4 1/2" | 13.5 | 3.795" |
| 5 1/2" | 14.0 | 4.767" |
| 5 1/2" | 20.0 | 4.653" |
| 7" | 23.0 | 6.151" |
| 7" | 32.0 | 5.969" |
| 9 5/8" | 36.0 | 8.765" |
| 9 5/8" | 47.0 | 8.625" |
| 13 3/8" | 54.5 | 12.459" |

- **Specialty drifts** (modified standard):
  - **Special drift** (SP-DR) — larger than API drift (less stringent).
  - **Custom drift** — engineered dla specific tool clearance.
- **Drift gauge vs go/no-go gauge**:
  - **Drift gauge** = single OD specification, verify passes.
  - **Go/no-go** = pair (go = should pass + no-go = should NOT pass through restriction).
- **Pipe inspection role**:
  - **Mill inspector** drifts each pipe before shipping (manufacturer responsibility).
  - **Field inspection** sometimes drifts again before installation (verify no transport damage).
  - **Internal damage from transport** detected by repeat drift.
- **Beyond API** — informal drift checks:
  - **Process piping pre-installation**: confirm pipe ID clear.
  - **Heat exchanger tubes**: drift verifies before re-installation in tubesheet.
  - **Buried pipeline post-construction**: drift verifies no excavator damage.
- **Vendors**: Pan O Tools, BJ Services (now Schlumberger), HillTOP Technologies.

### AAAAAAAA. Crowfoot Wrench + Specialty Wrenches — Tight Access Bolting
- **Crowfoot wrench** = **open-end wrench head WITHOUT handle**, designed do attach na **square drive** (1/4", 3/8", 1/2") of ratchet, extension, lub torque wrench. **Adapter** dla reaching bolts w confined spaces.
- **Cel**: torque flange bolts where standard wrench cannot fit:
  - **Behind obstructions** — pipe, valves, insulation.
  - **Tight clearance** between bolt + adjacent component.
  - **Hydraulic / sensor fittings** w congested skids.
  - **Pump / motor** flanges close to wall.
- **Common designs**:
  - **Standard crowfoot** — fixed-angle head, aligns with extension axis.
  - **Flex / pivoting crowfoot** — head pivots 0-180° (flexible angle).
  - **Flare nut crowfoot** — opened wider than standard, used dla flare fittings.
  - **Allen / hex** crowfoot — for socket-head cap screws.
- **Sizes**:
  - **Drive sizes**: 1/4", 3/8", 1/2", 3/4", 1" — match torque wrench drive.
  - **Wrench sizes**: 1/4" to 2-1/4" SAE; 6 mm do 50 mm metric.
- **Aplikacje pipefitter**:
  - **Flange bolting** w congested piping (most common):
    - Bolt heads obscured by adjacent flange.
    - Behind pipe lub equipment.
  - **Pump suction / discharge flanges** close to wall.
  - **Valve body bolts** w congested areas.
  - **Hydraulic tubing fittings** (especially small flare nut connections).
  - **Boiler / heat exchanger** internal flange access.
- **Torque correction factor** (KRYTYCZNE!):
  - **Crowfoot extends effective lever** length of torque wrench.
  - **Increases applied torque** beyond wrench dial setting.
  - **Correction needed** unless crowfoot perpendicular do torque wrench.
- **Formula**:
  - **T_actual = T_wrench × (L_wrench + L_offset) / L_wrench**
  - L_wrench = torque wrench arm length.
  - L_offset = crowfoot offset (distance from drive center to bolt center).
  - **Example**: Torque wrench L=18", crowfoot offset 2", set wrench to 50 ft-lb → actual bolt torque = 50 × 20/18 = 55.6 ft-lb.
- **90° configuration** (zero correction):
  - **Mount crowfoot perpendicular do torque wrench handle**.
  - **Effective lever unchanged**.
  - **No torque correction needed**.
  - **Preferred dla precision torquing**.
- **Standard configurations** (in line):
  - **180° (extension)** — crowfoot in line with handle. Correction factor = (L+offset)/L. Need calculation.
  - **0° (back)** — crowfoot opposite handle (rare). Correction factor = (L-offset)/L. Reduce setting.
- **Quality considerations**:
  - **12-point head** — more wrench position options vs 6-point.
  - **Material**: chrome vanadium steel hardened.
  - **Industrial grade** dla heavy torque.
- **Brands**:
  - **Snap-on** — premium quality.
  - **Williams (Snap-on industrial brand)** — heavy industrial.
  - **Wright Tool** — USA industrial.
  - **Proto** — premium.
  - **GearWrench, ARES, JB Tools** — economical professional.
- **Other specialty pipefitter wrenches**:
  - **Pipe wrench (Stillson)** — adjustable, gripping pipe OD.
  - **Strap wrench / chain wrench** — alternative dla finished surfaces (NO scratching).
  - **Hammer wrench (slugging wrench)** — heavy short wrench struck with hammer dla extreme torque (large flanges).
  - **Spanner wrench** — pin / hook design dla circular nuts.
  - **Pipe vise wrench** — Yost / Ridgid for holding pipe during work.
  - **Hex key (Allen wrench)** — set screws, internal hex bolts.
  - **Torx / Star** — newer fastener designs.
  - **Box-end ratcheting** — reduced clearance vs combination wrench.
- **Specialty sockets**:
  - **Impact sockets** — z impact wrench (black finish, thicker walls).
  - **Crowfoot sockets** — z drive flat dla crowfoot adapter.
  - **Universal joint extensions** — flexible drive train dla angled access.
- **Pipefitter's essential wrench kit**:
  - Combination wrenches 8-32 mm (or SAE equivalent).
  - Crowfoot set (most common sizes).
  - Pipe wrenches (10", 14", 18", 24").
  - Ratchet + extension set.
  - Allen + Torx sets.
  - Torque wrench (calibrated, primary tool).
  - Hammer wrench / slugger dla heavy flange.

## Iteration 61 — 2026-05-25

### BBBBBBBB. ASTM A105 — Forged Carbon Steel dla Pipe Fittings + Flanges
- **ASTM A105 / A105M** ("Standard Specification for Carbon Steel Forgings for Piping Applications") — **base material** dla **forged carbon steel components**:
  - Weld neck flanges (WN).
  - Slip-on flanges (SO).
  - Blind flanges (BL).
  - Lap joint stub ends.
  - Reducing flanges.
  - Forged tees, elbows, reducers (B16.11 forged fittings).
  - Forged valves (gate, globe, ball, check w body).
  - Long weld neck (LWN) — extended hub flanges.
- **Forging vs casting**:
  - **Forging** — metal deformed plastically pod compressive force (press / hammer). **Refined grain structure**, **no porosity**, **higher strength**.
  - **Casting** — molten metal poured into mold. **Cheaper** dla complex shapes ale **internal porosity** + **weaker grain**.
  - **Pressure containment + critical service**: forging preferred ZAWSZE.
- **Composition** (typical):
  - **Carbon (C)**: max 0.35%.
  - **Manganese (Mn)**: max 1.05%.
  - **Phosphorus (P)**: max 0.035%.
  - **Sulfur (S)**: max 0.040%.
  - **Silicon (Si)**: 0.10-0.35%.
- **Mechanical properties** (minimum):
  - **Tensile strength**: 70 ksi (485 MPa).
  - **Yield strength** (0.2% offset): 36 ksi (250 MPa).
  - **Elongation**: 22% (50 mm gauge length).
  - **Reduction of area**: 30%.
  - **Hardness**: 187 HBW max.
- **Forging process**:
  - **Heating**: 1700-2200°F (925-1200°C) — austenitizing range.
  - **Deformation**: hammer lub press, multi-step shaping.
  - **Cooling**: typowo air cool lub controlled cooling.
- **Heat treatment options** (per A105):
  - **As-forged** — only allowed dla low pressure / small sizes.
  - **Annealed** — heat to 900°C, slow furnace cool.
  - **Normalized** — heat to 900°C, air cool (most common).
  - **Normalized + Tempered** — preferred dla critical applications.
  - **Quenched + Tempered** — high strength applications.
  - **Mandatory heat treatment when**:
    - Flanges **above Class 300**.
    - Items **over 4" NPS (DN 100) above Class 300**.
    - Flanges of special design.
- **Forging repair** — welding allowed pod warunkiem:
  - **Repair welds** allowed within specified limits (not on critical surfaces).
  - **Post-weld heat treatment (PWHT)**: 1100°F (593°C) minimum dla **0.5 h/inch** (12.7 mm) of section thickness.
  - **Alternative**: full anneal, normalize + temper, lub quench + temper.
- **Size limits** (A105):
  - **Maximum forging weight**: **10,000 lb (4,540 kg)**.
  - **Larger forgings**: ordered to **A266/A266M** (or A350/A350M dla low-temp).
- **Inspection requirements**:
  - **Heat number traceability** — each forging marked.
  - **Charpy V-notch** dla low-temperature service.
  - **Hardness test** — 1 per heat treatment lot.
  - **Mechanical testing** — tensile per heat.
  - **NDE** — UT mandatory dla high-criticality.
- **Common service**:
  - **Process piping** ASME B31.3 (refinery, chemical).
  - **Power piping** B31.1.
  - **Pressure vessels** ASME VIII Div 1.
  - **Temperature range**: ambient to ~425°C (above = use A182 alloy steel like F11/F22).
- **Related materials**:
  - **A350 LF2** — low-temperature CS forgings (down to −50°C).
  - **A182 F11/F22** — Cr-Mo low-alloy (high-T).
  - **A182 F304/F316** — stainless forgings.
- **Identification on forging**:
  - **Heat number** stamped.
  - **Manufacturer logo**.
  - **Material grade** (e.g., "A105").
  - **Size + class**.
- **Common quantity production**:
  - **Standard flanges** (DN ≤ 200, Class ≤ 600) — mass-produced, off-the-shelf.
  - **Specialty items** (large diameter, high pressure) — custom forge orders, lead time 6-12 weeks.

### CCCCCCCC. Spray-Applied Fireproofing — SFRM + Intumescent dla Refineries
- **Fireproofing piping + structural steel** = passive fire protection. Steel **loses 50% strength at ~550°C** — w fire conditions structure can collapse w minutes. Fireproofing **insulates** steel, delays heat transfer.
- **Driver**: petroleum + chemical plants → hydrocarbon pool fires can reach 1000°C+ w 5 min.
- **Two main types**:
  - **SFRM (Spray-applied Fire Resistive Materials) / Cementitious fireproofing**:
    - **Binder**: Portland cement lub gypsum.
    - **Lightweight aggregates**: vermiculite, perlite, mineral wool, polystyrene beads.
    - **Result**: insulating "concrete-like" layer.
    - **Thickness**: 25-100 mm typowo (dla 2-4 hour fire ratings).
    - **Density**: low (300-500 kg/m³) — light, doesn't load structure.
    - **Adhesion**: primer required na steel surface.
    - **Standard dla structural steel** — beams, columns, decks.
  - **Intumescent fireproofing (IFRM)**:
    - **Coating** that **expands when heated** — typically 30-100× original thickness.
    - **Composition**: epoxy resin + intumescent additives + fillers.
    - **Reaction at ~200°C** — chemical reaction releases gases, expands w foam-like char layer.
    - **Thickness applied**: 1-15 mm (thin compared to SFRM!).
    - **Aesthetic**: looks like paint, paintable.
    - **Outdoor use**: weather-resistant, UV-stable variants.
    - **Standard dla exposed steel** w architectural + industrial applications.
- **Fire rating**:
  - **1 hour** — minimum dla many applications.
  - **2 hour** — typical structural steel.
  - **3 hour** — critical fire walls.
  - **4 hour** — extreme (refinery hot zones).
- **Fire types** dla refinery design:
  - **Cellulose fire** — normal building (wood, paper) — slower temperature rise.
  - **Hydrocarbon fire** — refinery (oil, gas) — rapid temperature rise (1100°C w 30 min). **More demanding fireproofing**.
  - **Jet fire** — pressurized release, very intense, localized.
- **Refinery applications**:
  - **Structural steel** — pipe rack columns, supports.
  - **Pipe supports + hangers** w fire-prone zones.
  - **Critical equipment skirts** — column bases, vessel skirts.
  - **Cable trays** — emergency power cables protected.
  - **Valves** — emergency isolation valves (fire-safe certified separately).
- **NOT typically applied do**:
  - **Process piping itself** — pipe heated externally same as internally (heat transfer through wall).
  - **Coatings** — fire-resistant paints exist ale typowo dla shorter duration / architectural.
- **Application method**:
  - **SFRM**:
    - **Surface prep**: power tool clean SP-3, primer applied.
    - **Wet spray** lub **dry spray** — mixed with water at gun lub tank.
    - **Multiple coats** required dla full thickness.
    - **Cure**: 24-72 hours per coat.
    - **Finish**: rough texture (can be smoothed if needed).
    - **Mesh reinforcement** — galvanized welded mesh embedded dla added integrity.
  - **Intumescent**:
    - **Sandblast surface prep** SP-6 / SP-10.
    - **Primer**: zinc-rich epoxy.
    - **Apply intumescent** — typowo 2K epoxy, sprayed (cross-spray rolled / brushed).
    - **Multiple coats** dla required DFT.
    - **Top coat** — UV-resistant urethane optional (outdoor).
    - **Cure**: 24-72 hours per coat ambient, faster z heated booth.
- **Verification**:
  - **DFT measurement** — wet + dry film thickness.
  - **Adhesion testing** — pull-off test per ASTM D4541.
  - **Coupon test fire** — heat coupon sample, verify rating.
- **Standards**:
  - **UL 263 / ULC S101** — Fire test of building construction.
  - **UL 1709 / ASTM E1529** — Hydrocarbon fire test.
  - **ASTM E84** — Surface burning characteristics.
  - **NFPA 251** — Standard methods of fire tests.
- **Service environment limits**:
  - **SFRM**: indoor lub protected outdoor only (waterproofing needed dla exposure).
  - **Intumescent**: outdoor durable, refinery proven.
- **Maintenance**:
  - **Annual visual** — cracks, gaps, mechanical damage.
  - **Repair** small areas immediately (preserves fire rating).
  - **Re-coating** every 10-20 years dla intumescent (UV degradation).
  - **SFRM** lasts 30-50+ years if protected.
- **Vendors**:
  - **SFRM**: Cafco (Carboline), Pyrocrete, Pyrok, Monokote (Grace).
  - **Intumescent**: Sherwin-Williams FIRETEX, Carboline Pyroclad, AkzoNobel Interchar.

### DDDDDDDD. PTFE Slide Bearing Plates — Pipe Support Friction Reduction
- **PTFE slide plate** = **low-friction sliding interface** between pipe support shoe + steel beam, allowing pipe **thermal expansion movement** without transmitting friction loads do structure.
- **Cel** (per iter 13 thermal expansion overview):
  - Hot pipe rośnie wzdłuż osi (od ambient do operating T).
  - **Friction between pipe shoe + steel beam** would transmit force to anchor + structure.
  - **PTFE slide reduces friction** dramatically → minimum loads na supports.
- **PTFE properties**:
  - **Lowest friction** material w common engineering use.
  - **Coefficient of friction** μ = **0.06-0.10** (PTFE-on-stainless smooth).
  - **For comparison**: steel-on-steel μ = 0.3-0.4 (5× higher!).
  - **Self-lubricating** — no grease / oil needed.
  - **Chemical resistance** — virtually all chemicals.
  - **Temperature range**: **−320°F do +500°F** (−195°C do +260°C).
- **Slide plate construction** (typical):
  - **Top plate** — typowo **PTFE bonded to backing plate** (steel lub stainless).
  - **Bottom plate** — stainless steel mirror finish (typowo 304L lub 316L).
  - **PTFE thickness**: 1.5-6 mm.
  - **Bonding**: adhesive lub mechanical fastening.
- **Material variations**:
  - **Virgin PTFE** — pure, soft, lowest friction. Limited compressive strength.
  - **Glass-filled PTFE (25% glass)** — most common, **higher compressive strength** + reduced **cold flow (creep)**. Best dla pipe supports.
  - **Graphite-filled PTFE** — improved wear resistance, slightly higher friction.
  - **Carbon-filled PTFE** — high wear, slightly conductive (anti-static).
  - **Bronze-filled PTFE** — high load + temperature.
  - **Coal filled PTFE** — high load capacity, dimensional stability.
- **Installation** (typowy):
  - **Bottom slide plate** welded to top of structural beam.
  - **Top slide plate** bonded to pipe shoe.
  - **Plates contact** when pipe at operating position.
  - **Vertical clearance** between pipe + shoe NOT required (shoe = integral pipe attachment).
- **Slide direction limits**:
  - **Horizontal directions** (X + Y) — unrestricted slide.
  - **Vertical** — supports pipe weight (compression load).
  - **Tilt** — limited (slide plate doesn't rotate well).
- **Load capacity**:
  - **Virgin PTFE**: 5-10 MPa allowable bearing pressure.
  - **Glass-filled PTFE**: 15-25 MPa.
  - **Other filled grades**: 20-40 MPa.
- **Movement allowance**:
  - **Slide travel** = function of pipe expansion (per iter 7 thermal expansion):
    - Carbon steel: 0.012 mm/m/°C.
    - Stainless steel: 0.017 mm/m/°C.
  - **Plate sized** dla expected travel + safety margin.
- **Aplikacje**:
  - **Long horizontal pipe runs** at varying temperature — refinery process piping, district heating.
  - **Power plant** main steam, boiler feed water.
  - **Cryogenic pipelines** LNG terminals.
  - **Industrial chemical plants** — hot service.
- **Pipe shoe + slide plate assembly**:
  - **Insulated shoe** — pipe shoe extends through insulation, slide plate sits on structural beam.
  - **Saddle support** with slide plate dla curved bottom of large pipe.
  - **Anchor block** — REVERSE concept: pipe pinned to structure (no slide allowed) at one point.
- **Common issues / failures**:
  - **PTFE creep (cold flow)** — material deforms over time pod sustained load:
    - **Glass-filled grades** resist this much better than virgin.
    - **Long-term load** w fixed position causes flow.
  - **Wear**: surface scratches, abrasion z dirt + grit.
  - **Contamination**: solvents (acetone) attack PTFE.
- **Vendors**:
  - **Piping Technology & Products (PT&P)** — premier USA.
  - **Pipe Shields Inc**.
  - **Bergen Power Pipe Support (BPPS)**.
  - **Dannenbaum LLC**.
  - **Witzenmann (Germany)** — European supplier.

## Iteration 62 — 2026-05-25

### EEEEEEEE. OSHA 1926 Subpart P — Trench Shoring + Excavation Safety
- **OSHA 1926 Subpart P** ("Excavations") = primary USA regulation dla trenching + excavation safety. **Trench collapses kill ~25 workers/year w USA** — most preventable workplace deaths in construction. Pipeline construction is high-trenching trade.
- **Trench definition** (per OSHA):
  - **Width ≤ 15 ft (4.6 m)**.
  - **Depth > 5 ft (1.5 m)** triggers protection requirements.
  - **Excavation** = broader term (anything dug — pits, trenches, basements).
- **Protection requirements** (mandatory):
  - **Trenches ≥ 5 ft deep**: protective system **REQUIRED** unless excavated entirely w stable rock.
  - **Trenches < 5 ft**: competent person decides if protection needed.
  - **All > 20 ft**: registered professional engineer (PE) design.
- **3 main protection methods**:
  - **Sloping (benching)** — angle of trench walls cut back do safe angle:
    - **Type A soil** (clay, hard cohesive): max **1.5:1** vertical:horizontal slope (33.7° from vertical).
    - **Type B soil** (cohesive less stable): max **1:1** (45°).
    - **Type C soil** (sand, gravel, submerged): max **1:1.5** (33.7° from horizontal — flatter cut).
    - **Soil classification** by competent person.
  - **Shoring** — supports walls vertically:
    - **Timber shoring** (Subpart P App C) — wooden uprights + horizontal stringers + cross braces (whalers + struts).
    - **Aluminum hydraulic shoring** (App D) — vertical rams jacked between trench walls.
    - **Speed shores** — vertical struts inflated z hydraulic pressure.
  - **Shielding (trench box)** — separate steel box dropped into trench:
    - **Trench box** lowered z crane / excavator.
    - **Workers work inside box** — protected if walls collapse.
    - **Box not structural** — does NOT prevent collapse, but protects within.
    - **Box ratings** — depth class A, B, C dla different soils.
- **Soil classification** (per Subpart P):
  - **Type A** — most cohesive, undisturbed clay, silty clay. Stable.
  - **Type B** — moderately cohesive — silty clay loams, sandy loams.
  - **Type C** — granular, low cohesion — sand, gravel, submerged soils, soft clay.
  - **Stable rock** — natural solid mineral, NOT loose / blasted rock.
  - **Tests**: visual + manual (pocket penetrometer, thumb test, plasticity).
- **Egress requirements**:
  - **Ladder** within **25 ft (7.6 m)** of every worker — in trench > 4 ft deep.
  - **Multiple egress points** dla long trenches.
  - **Ramp** dla heavy equipment access.
- **Hazardous atmosphere testing**:
  - **Trenches > 4 ft deep** w possible atmospheric hazard (near landfill, gas line, sewers).
  - **Test**: O₂ (19.5-23.5%), LEL (< 10%), toxic gases.
  - **Continuous monitoring** during work.
  - **Ventilation** if needed (fans).
- **Vibration / loads near trench**:
  - **Spoil pile** (excavated dirt) — **minimum 2 ft (0.6 m) back from trench edge**.
  - **Equipment** (trucks, excavators) — no closer than equipment-recommended distance.
  - **Vibration** z passing vehicles can cause cave-ins — distance kept.
- **Water accumulation**:
  - **Pumping** required jeśli water accumulates.
  - **Continuous monitoring**.
  - **Diversion ditches** od surface runoff.
- **Underground utilities**:
  - **Call 811 (USA "Call Before You Dig")** — minimum 48-72 h before digging.
  - **Locate + mark** all underground utilities (gas, electric, communications, water).
  - **Hand digging** within 2 ft of marked utilities.
- **Competent person** requirements:
  - **Training**: soil analysis, shoring systems, OSHA Subpart P.
  - **Authority**: can stop work if unsafe conditions.
  - **Daily inspection** of trench before entry + after any event (rain, vibration).
- **Pipeline construction specific**:
  - **Long open trenches** (1000+ ft) — sequential shoring as pipe laid.
  - **Trench widening at joints** — adequate space dla welder access.
  - **Spoil management** — staging area away from trench, restoration after pipe laid.
- **Common hazards**:
  - **Cave-in** — most fatal, sudden.
  - **Engulfment** w sand / saturated soil.
  - **Falls** from edge.
  - **Hit by equipment** (excavator swing, dumped spoil).
  - **Hazardous atmosphere** — particularly w sewer / utility crossings.
- **Trench rescue**:
  - **Specialized rescue team** required — confined space + heavy equipment skills.
  - **DO NOT enter unprotected trench** for "rescue" — secondary casualties common.
  - **Local fire department** trench rescue capability varies — some not equipped.

### FFFFFFFF. Boiler / Steam Plant Layup — Wet vs Dry Preservation
- **Boiler layup** = preservation of boiler + steam piping during **idle periods** (turnaround, seasonal shutdown, long outage). **Prevents corrosion** by water-side stagnation.
- **Why layup matters**:
  - **Stagnant water** + oxygen + iron = **pitting corrosion**.
  - **Single short-term layup mistake** can cause **decades of cumulative damage**.
  - **Boiler tube failure** = $50K-$500K repair + days/weeks downtime.
- **Two main methods**:
  - **Wet layup** — boiler **FILLED with treated water**:
    - **Water chemistry controlled** — pH 9.5-10.5, oxygen scavenger added (hydrazine lub carbohydrazide).
    - **Steam space** protected:
      - **Nitrogen blanket** (cover gas) — displaces O₂ above water line.
      - **Desiccant** — silica gel, calcium chloride.
      - **Dehumidified air circulation**.
    - **Pros**: fast restart (already filled).
    - **Cons**: requires monitoring, freeze risk, water expense if dumped + refilled.
  - **Dry layup** — boiler **DRAINED + DRIED**:
    - **All water removed**.
    - **Drying** — heat application (steam coils, fans).
    - **Preservation** — nitrogen blanket, desiccant, lub dehumidified air.
    - **Pros**: no freeze risk, no water chemistry monitoring.
    - **Cons**: longer restart time (fill, chemical condition).
- **When to use** wet vs dry:
  - **Wet layup** dla:
    - **Short outages** (< 1 month).
    - **Emergency standby** — quick start required.
    - **Boilers without easy drain access**.
  - **Dry layup** dla:
    - **Long outages** (> 1 month).
    - **Winter freeze risk areas**.
    - **Major maintenance** requiring internal access.
- **Wet layup procedure**:
  1. **Cool boiler** to 80°C or below.
  2. **Fill completely** z deaerated boiler feedwater.
  3. **Treat water**:
     - **Hydrazine** ~200 mg/L (volatile oxygen scavenger).
     - **Caustic NaOH** dla pH 9.5-11.
     - **OR** ammonia + morpholine dla volatile chemistry.
  4. **Vent air** through steam outlet.
  5. **Top up vent** with nitrogen (5-10 psi) — exclude air ingress.
  6. **Daily monitoring** — pressure, water level, leak check.
  7. **Weekly chemistry** — pH, hydrazine residual.
  8. **Monthly chemistry** — full water analysis.
- **Dry layup procedure**:
  1. **Cool, drain completely**.
  2. **Inspect interior** — manholes opened, scale removed if needed.
  3. **Dry** — heat 50-60°C z fans, lub vacuum dry, lub fill z dry air.
  4. **Internal inspection** — vide próbnie.
  5. **Apply desiccant**:
     - **Silica gel** trays inside drum.
     - **Hydrogen scavenger** dla H₂ generation prevention.
  6. **Or apply** nitrogen blanket (5-10 psi, monitored).
  7. **Seal all openings** — gasketed blind flanges.
  8. **Monthly check** — pressure / humidity verification.
- **Nitrogen blanket** specifically:
  - **Inert atmosphere** — no oxygen reaction.
  - **Pressure 5-10 psi** above atmospheric (prevents air ingress).
  - **Source**: nitrogen cylinders + auto-fill regulator OR onsite N₂ generator.
  - **NIE używać** dla manned-confined-space — asphyxiation risk.
- **Special considerations** (specific equipment):
  - **Superheaters / reheaters** — drainage difficult, may require special drying procedures.
  - **HRSG (Heat Recovery Steam Generator)** — multiple pressure circuits, each layup'd separately.
  - **Economizers** — typowo dry layup (small water volume).
  - **Condensers (steam-side)** — wet layup z deaerated water lub dry.
- **Standards**:
  - **EPRI** (Electric Power Research Institute) guidelines.
  - **ASME PTC 4** — boiler performance test code (peripheral guidance).
  - **NACE Layup Guidelines** dla water-side.

### GGGGGGGG. Pipe Bursting — Trenchless Pipeline Replacement
- **Pipe bursting** = **trenchless pipeline replacement** method. **Old pipe destroyed in-place + new pipe pulled into space simultaneously**. Different from **CIPP** (covered iter 50) which lines old pipe — bursting **REPLACES** old pipe.
- **Pieces**:
  - **Bursting head (cone)** — front end z **larger diameter than old pipe**, breaks/expands ground.
  - **New HDPE pipe** — fused continuous length (per iter 20 butt fusion), attached behind bursting head.
  - **Pulling rod** lub cable — attaches do bursting head from pulling pit.
  - **Pulling machine** — hydraulic, located w **pulling pit** (downstream).
  - **Entry pit** — small excavation at start (upstream side).
- **Process**:
  1. **Inspect old pipe** — CCTV, identify obstructions.
  2. **Excavate 2 pits** — entry + exit (each ~1.5 × 1.5 × pipe depth m).
  3. **Insert pulling rod** through old pipe od pulling pit → entry pit.
  4. **Attach bursting head + new HDPE** at entry pit.
  5. **Hydraulic pulling** z pulling pit:
     - **Bursting head** advances through old pipe.
     - **Old pipe fractures + displaces outward** (radial).
     - **New HDPE** drawn behind bursting head.
  6. **Continuous installation** without trenching middle of run.
  7. **Reconnect laterals** (sewer service connections):
     - **Pre-locate laterals** before bursting.
     - **Robot lateral reinstatement** lub manual excavation.
- **Bursting methods**:
  - **Pneumatic (dynamic) pipe bursting**:
    - **Impact hammer** — pneumatic-powered repeated impact.
    - **Cable + winch** guides hammer through pipe.
    - **Old method**, still used dla some applications.
    - **Drawbacks**: ground vibration may damage adjacent utilities.
  - **Hydraulic pipe bursting**:
    - **Hydraulic pulling rams** w pulling pit.
    - **Bursting head** has **expanding leaves** (radial cutters).
    - **For brittle pipe** (cast iron, vitrified clay, asbestos cement).
  - **Static pipe bursting** (MOST COMMON modern method):
    - **Continuous static pull** (no shock impact).
    - **Less ground vibration** vs pneumatic.
    - **GRUNDOBURST (TT Technologies)** lub **HammerHead Hydroburst** premier systems.
- **Pipe materials** (old pipe being burst):
  - **Vitrified clay** (sewer) — brittle, ideal.
  - **Cast iron** — splits well.
  - **Asbestos cement (AC)** — bursts ale **asbestos handling regulations** — special protocols.
  - **PVC / HDPE** (existing) — different burst technique (splitting head).
  - **Steel** — difficult, requires special heavy equipment.
  - **Concrete** — possible z heavy hydraulic bursters.
- **New pipe (replacement)**:
  - **HDPE** — most common (smooth ID, no corrosion, butt-fused continuous).
  - **PE100** typowo (modern grade).
  - **Diameter same as old** OR **upsized** (typowo 25-50% larger).
- **Aplikacje**:
  - **Sewer mains** — most common (gravity flow, vitrified clay → HDPE).
  - **Water mains** — water utility renewal.
  - **Gas distribution** — old steel → HDPE z service line bursting.
  - **Industrial** — chemical, process water.
- **Advantages**:
  - **Replaces** old pipe — better than CIPP if old pipe structurally degraded.
  - **Upsizes diameter** possible (capacity increase).
  - **Minimal surface disruption** (only 2 pits dla long runs).
  - **Faster** vs open-cut replacement.
- **Limitations**:
  - **Cannot handle bends > 22.5°** — pipe must be straight.
  - **Service connections** must be re-excavated + reconnected.
  - **Adjacent utility damage** risk od ground heave.
  - **Pipe must be in fragmentable condition** — heavily reinforced concrete pipe nie possible.
- **CIPP vs Pipe Bursting decision**:
  - **CIPP**: existing pipe structurally OK (only corrosion / pitting). Smaller capacity than new pipe.
  - **Bursting**: existing pipe failing structurally. Same / larger capacity.
- **Cost**:
  - **Pipe bursting**: $80-200 per ft (depends on diameter, depth).
  - **Open-cut replacement**: $150-500 per ft.
  - **CIPP**: $100-300 per ft.
  - **Cost-effectiveness** zależy od soil + utility congestion.
- **Vendors**:
  - **TT Technologies** — GRUNDOBURST (German).
  - **HammerHead Trenchless** — USA premier.
  - **TRIC Tools** — heads + tools.
  - **Pow-r-Mole** — pneumatic systems.

## Iteration 63 — 2026-05-25

### HHHHHHHH. API 579 / ASME FFS-1 — Fitness-For-Service Assessment
- **API 579-1 / ASME FFS-1** ("Fitness-For-Service") = standardized **engineering evaluation methodology** dla determining whether **damaged equipment can continue safe operation**.
- **Different from API 570** (inspection) — **API 570 detects** damage, **API 579 evaluates** what to do z found damage.
- **Cel**: avoid premature repair / replacement gdy equipment still safe.
- **Equipment coverage**:
  - **Piping** (process + power).
  - **Pressure vessels**.
  - **Storage tanks**.
  - **Heat exchangers**.
  - **Reactors**.
- **Damage mechanisms covered**:
  - **General metal loss** (Part 4) — uniform corrosion thinning.
  - **Local metal loss** (Part 5) — localized pitting, gouges, mechanical damage.
  - **Pitting corrosion** (Part 6) — pit depth + density evaluation.
  - **Blisters + laminations** (Part 7) — HIC, hydrogen blistering.
  - **Weld misalignment + shell distortion** (Part 8) — out-of-round, hi-lo, dent.
  - **Crack-like flaws** (Part 9) — using fracture mechanics.
  - **Creep damage** (Part 10) — high-T components.
  - **Fire damage** (Part 11) — post-incident assessment.
  - **Dent, gouge, dent-gouge combinations** (Part 12) — mechanical damage.
  - **Laminations** (Part 13).
- **3 assessment levels**:
  - **Level 1** — **conservative simple screening**:
    - **Quick check** based on simple parameters (thickness, defect size).
    - **Conservative** — likely fails if borderline.
    - **Pass = fit for continued service**.
    - **Fail = upgrade do Level 2 lub repair**.
    - **Performed by**: pipe inspector lub field engineer.
  - **Level 2** — **detailed analytical assessment**:
    - **Engineering calculations** z FEA lub closed-form equations.
    - **Considers more variables** (stress + strain, geometric complexity).
    - **Less conservative** — more accurate.
    - **Performed by**: experienced engineer.
  - **Level 3** — **advanced numerical analysis**:
    - **Full FEA (Finite Element Analysis)**, possibly z plasticity + creep + nonlinear material models.
    - **Most accurate** — least conservative.
    - **Most expensive + time-consuming**.
    - **Performed by**: specialized engineering firm, peer-reviewed.
- **Assessment workflow**:
  1. **Identify damage type** + mechanism.
  2. **Collect data** — wall thickness measurements, geometry, history.
  3. **Material properties** — design code allowables, actual MTRs jeśli available.
  4. **Operating conditions** — pressure, temperature, cycles.
  5. **Run Level 1 assessment** — typowo z online calculator lub spreadsheet.
  6. **If passes Level 1** = continue operation z monitoring.
  7. **If fails Level 1** = run Level 2 (detailed).
  8. **If fails Level 2** = run Level 3 (FEA) lub repair / replace.
- **Common outcomes**:
  - **Continue operation as-is** — pass Level 1 lub 2.
  - **Continue z increased inspection frequency** — borderline pass.
  - **De-rate operating pressure / temperature** — extend life w reduced service.
  - **Repair** — patch, weld overlay, sleeve.
  - **Replace** — beyond economical repair.
- **Example: general metal loss** (Part 4) Level 1:
  - **Input**: current minimum thickness (t_min), original thickness, design pressure, allowable stress.
  - **Calculation**: code-based pressure rating dla actual t_min.
  - **If allowable pressure > design pressure**: **PASS** — fit for service.
  - **If not**: Level 2 with FEA, lub repair.
- **Crack-like flaws (Part 9)** — specialized:
  - **Fracture mechanics** — calculate K (stress intensity factor) at crack tip.
  - **Compare to K_IC** (material toughness).
  - **If K < K_IC** — crack stable, won't propagate catastrophically.
  - **Inputs**: crack length, depth, orientation, location, stress field.
- **Documentation requirements**:
  - **Assessment report** — detailed calculation, assumptions, conclusions.
  - **Engineering certification** — PE seal often required.
  - **Operator approval** — accepted risk.
  - **Periodic re-evaluation** — long-term monitoring.
- **Related standards / coupling**:
  - **API 510** — Pressure vessel inspection.
  - **API 570** — Piping inspection (per iter 25).
  - **API 653** — Storage tank inspection.
  - **API 580/581** — Risk-Based Inspection (RBI) coordinates z FFS.
  - **ASME B31G** — older standard dla pipeline corrosion (now incorporated w API 579).

### IIIIIIII. Pipe Deflection / Sag Calculation — Beam Formulas
- **Pipe deflection** = mid-span sag of pipe spanning between supports under own weight + contents. **Critical dla**:
  - **Aesthetic concern** w visible piping.
  - **Drainage** — sag creates low points where condensate / sediment accumulates.
  - **Flow** — sagging pipe affects velocity profile.
  - **Stress** — excessive deflection induces stress on flanges, weld toes.
- **Deflection formula** (simply supported beam, uniformly distributed load):
  - **δ = (5 × w × L⁴) / (384 × E × I)**
  - **δ** = deflection at center [mm lub in]
  - **w** = weight per unit length [N/m lub lb/in]
  - **L** = span between supports [mm lub in]
  - **E** = modulus of elasticity [N/mm² lub psi]
  - **I** = moment of inertia of pipe cross-section [mm⁴ lub in⁴]
- **Moment of inertia** dla pipe:
  - **I = π/64 × (D⁴ − d⁴)** gdzie D = OD, d = ID.
  - **For DN50 Sch 40 (OD 60.3 mm, t=3.9 mm)**: I ≈ 32 cm⁴.
  - **For DN100 Sch 40 (OD 114 mm, t=6.0 mm)**: I ≈ 313 cm⁴.
- **Modulus of elasticity (E)** dla common materials:
  - **Carbon steel**: 200 GPa (29 × 10⁶ psi) at room temp, decreases at high T.
  - **Stainless steel (304/316)**: 193 GPa.
  - **Aluminum**: 70 GPa.
  - **HDPE**: 0.8-1.0 GPa (much lower — bigger sag!).
- **Pipe weight per unit length** (total = pipe + insulation + fluid + fittings):
  - **CS pipe DN50 Sch 40**: 5.4 kg/m empty, ~8 kg/m with water.
  - **CS pipe DN100 Sch 40**: 16.1 kg/m empty, ~24 kg/m with water.
  - **CS pipe DN200 Sch 40**: 42.6 kg/m empty, ~67 kg/m with water.
  - **CS pipe DN300 Sch 40**: 79.7 kg/m empty, ~135 kg/m with water.
- **Allowable deflection**:
  - **Typical industry standard**: **≤ 12.5 mm (1/2")** at mid-span.
  - **Higher precision applications** (instrument tubing, BPE): ≤ 6 mm (1/4").
  - **Cosmetic / aesthetic**: visible sag bothering operators (≥ 25 mm visible).
- **Span calculation formula** (rearranged dla maximum span):
  - **L_max = [(δ_allow × E × I) / (17.1 × W)]^(1/4)** (imperial units, L w ft, W w lb/ft, δ w inch)
  - **L_max = [(384 × δ_allow × E × I) / (5 × w)]^(1/4)** (SI units)
- **Practical example**:
  - **DN100 Sch 40 CS pipe** filled with water (24 kg/m).
  - **E = 200 GPa, I = 313 cm⁴**.
  - **Allowable deflection 12.5 mm**.
  - **L_max** ≈ **5.2 m** between supports.
  - **Matches MSS SP-69 Table 3** (covered iter 14) — 5.2 m water service DN100.
- **MSS SP-58 / SP-69 tables** — pre-calculated max spans dla common pipes (per iter 14).
- **Non-uniform loading**:
  - **Concentrated load** (valve, flange, fitting) — adds local stress + deflection at mid-span.
  - **Formula adjustment**: superpose distributed + concentrated load contributions.
- **Slope** (deliberately designed):
  - **Process piping** often **sloped 1:100 to 1:50** dla drainage.
  - **Slope > deflection** — net positive drainage.
  - **Deflection > slope** = puddle (water trap).
- **Real-world adjustments**:
  - **Multi-span** vs simply supported — more complex formula (uses moments transferred at supports).
  - **Fixed ends** (welded to anchor) — less deflection but more stress.
  - **Insulated pipe** — weight of insulation + jacketing adds 5-15% to total weight.
- **Software tools**:
  - **CAESAR II** (covered iter 42) — full pipe stress analysis.
  - **AutoPIPE** (Bentley).
  - **Online calculators**: Engineers Edge, Omni, Piping-World.

### JJJJJJJJ. Mechanical Pipe Couplings — Hymax / Romac / Repair Clamps
- **Mechanical pipe coupling** = **bolted or compression-style coupling** joining pipe ends bez welding lub threading. **Standard dla water + wastewater pipeline repair + connection**.
- **Different from**:
  - **Flange + bolt** — requires flanged pipe ends.
  - **Push-on (Tyton)** — gasket only, no mechanical restraint.
  - **Welded** — permanent, requires hot work permit.
  - **Threaded (NPT)** — small diameters, low pressure.
- **Mechanical coupling types**:
  - **Compression coupling** (Hymax HYMAX, Romac 501, Dresser Style 38):
    - **Two end glands** + central **compression sleeve**.
    - **Bolts pull glands together** → squeeze elastomer ring around pipe OD.
    - **Joins different pipe materials + diameters** (within range).
    - **Standard service**: water mains.
  - **Restrained coupling** (HYMAX GRIP, Romac MCR, Dresser MJ):
    - **Compression seal + mechanical restraint** (grip ring lub teeth).
    - **Withstands axial pull** (pipe pressure thrust).
    - **Dla in-line repair under pressure**.
  - **Sleeve coupling** (Dresser Style 38 long sleeve):
    - **Longer central section** dla bridging gap between pipes.
    - **Used dla**: pipe ends not aligned, slight length adjustment, replacement section connect.
  - **Wide-range coupling**:
    - **Single coupling handles range of pipe ODs** (e.g., 4"-4.5" range).
    - **HYMAX series** — premier wide-range product.
    - **Aplikacja**: connecting different pipe materials z slightly different OD (CS to ductile iron, DI to HDPE).
  - **Repair clamp** (full-circumferential):
    - **Wraps around damaged pipe section** completely.
    - **Multiple bolts** dla even sealing.
    - **Inner gasket** seals around pipe OD + over defect.
    - **Standard service**: emergency leak repair.
    - **Romac SS1** — pioneer all-stainless steel repair clamp (1969).
  - **Tapping sleeve + valve**:
    - **Compression sleeve** wrapped around main + service tap valve.
    - **Hot tap** (under pressure) — service connection bez pipeline shutdown.
- **Materials**:
  - **Body**: ductile iron lub stainless steel (T-304 lub T-316).
  - **Bolts**: 304 SS lub 316 SS dla water service.
  - **Gaskets**: NBR (Nitrile) dla water + petroleum, EPDM dla potable water, Viton dla aggressive chemicals.
- **Aplikacje**:
  - **Water utility pipe repair** — primary use.
  - **Wastewater + sewer** mains.
  - **Industrial cooling water** loops.
  - **Fire main** repairs (limited — must be NFPA / FM listed).
  - **HVAC water systems**.
  - **NOT dla pressurized hazardous** (gas, hydrocarbon) — mechanical couplings typowo do max 16 bar water.
- **Pressure ratings**:
  - **Standard repair couplings**: 150-250 psi (10-16 bar).
  - **Wide-range restrained**: do 350 psi (24 bar).
  - **Specialty high-pressure**: 600+ psi available.
- **Installation**:
  1. **Pipe surface preparation** — clean, smooth, no scale, no welds.
  2. **Lubricate gasket** z compatible water-based lubricant (NEVER oil dla EPDM).
  3. **Slide coupling onto pipe** ends.
  4. **Center coupling** over joint location.
  5. **Tighten bolts** **alternating diagonal pattern** (jak flange — covered iter 2):
     - 1/3 torque first pass.
     - 2/3 torque second pass.
     - Full torque third pass.
  6. **Verify centering** + parallel alignment of glands.
  7. **Pressure test** at design pressure dla 30 min minimum.
- **Limitations**:
  - **Pipe surface tolerance** — out-of-round > 5% may prevent good seal.
  - **Pipe coating** — gasket grips coating, not pipe; coating may flow over time.
  - **Operating temperature**: typowo ≤ 65°C (gasket limit).
  - **Vibration** — over time loosens bolts (re-torque inspection required).
- **Vendors**:
  - **Hymax (Krausz Industries / Mueller)** — premier wide-range couplings.
  - **Romac Industries** — comprehensive water industry products.
  - **Dresser (now Mueller)** — historic, Style 38 classic.
  - **Smith-Blair (now Mueller)** — repair clamps.
  - **Ford Meter Box** — utility products.
  - **JCM Industries** — repair couplings.
- **Standards**:
  - **AWWA C219** — Bolted Sleeve-Type Couplings.
  - **AWWA C800** — Underground Service Line Valves and Fittings.
  - **NSF/ANSI 61** — potable water certification.

## Iteration 64 — 2026-05-25

### KKKKKKKK. Tank Cleaning — Robotic Sludge Removal (Crude Oil + Storage)
- **Tank cleaning** = periodic process removing **sediment, sludge, scale, residual product** from storage tank floor + walls. Historically done by manual confined-space entry (HIGH RISK).
- **Why tanks accumulate sludge**:
  - **Heavy hydrocarbons** settle z bottom (waxes, asphaltenes, carbon w residual oil).
  - **Water-oil emulsion** breaks → water + sediment layer w bottom.
  - **Inorganic deposits** — corrosion products, sand, FeS (iron sulfide).
  - **Bacterial mats** — biofilms w water-contacted areas.
- **Sludge layer thickness**: typowo 50-500 mm w 10-30 year-old tanks before cleaning.
- **Risks of MANUAL tank entry** (traditional method):
  - **Confined space deaths** — top cause of worker fatalities w oil + gas.
  - **Toxic gases**: H₂S, mercaptans, benzene, hydrocarbon vapor.
  - **Explosive atmosphere**: hydrocarbon w air = LEL flammable.
  - **Pyrophoric iron sulfide** (FeS) — spontaneously combusts w oxygen exposure.
  - **Heat stress**, falls, equipment hazards.
  - **Long downtime** dla cleaning (weeks to months).
- **ROBOTIC TANK CLEANING** (modern method):
  - **Tank robot** (ROV — Remote Operated Vehicle) operates **inside tank**, remotely controlled z outside.
  - **NO HUMAN ENTRY** dla initial cleaning phase.
  - **Eliminates confined space risk**.
- **Common robots**:
  - **Magnetic crawlers** — magnetic wheels grip floor + walls.
  - **Wheeled crawlers** — drive across sludge layer.
  - **TankROV STRIKER** — submersible-style ROV for floating-roof tanks.
  - **Re-Gen Robotics**, **Arham Oil**, **Chartwell Energy Services** — service providers.
- **Robotic cleaning process**:
  1. **Tank emptied** of product (pump out + drain).
  2. **Tank isolated** (block valves, spectacle blinds per iter 15).
  3. **Nitrogen purge** — displaces explosive hydrocarbon vapors.
  4. **Tank atmosphere tested** — O₂, LEL, H₂S — until safe.
  5. **Robot inserted** through manway (smaller than human-sized entry needed).
  6. **Cleaning operation**:
     - **Robot agitates sludge** z mechanical arms + high-pressure water jets.
     - **Vacuum extraction** — sludge pumped out through extraction hose.
     - **ATEX-rated cameras** allow operator monitoring outside tank.
     - **Lighting** built-in dla tank interior visibility.
  7. **Robot removed** + visual inspection (still no human entry yet).
  8. **Final cleaning** — manual confined entry (if needed) — much shorter, safer.
  9. **Tank dried + inspected** dla floor integrity (per iter 48 — MFL scanning).
- **Productivity benefits**:
  - **40% time reduction** vs manual cleaning.
  - **Zero confined space entry** dla majority of work.
  - **Less waste** (cleaner extraction).
  - **Lower personnel exposure** + insurance costs.
- **Sludge disposal**:
  - **Centrifuge separation** — water, oil, solids separated.
  - **Oil recovered** + recycled.
  - **Solids dried** dla disposal (hazardous waste typically).
  - **Water treated** w API separator + discharged lub re-injected.
- **Tank inspection po cleaning**:
  - **MFL scan** floor (per iter 48).
  - **UT thickness mapping** of plates.
  - **Visual + PT** of welds.
  - **Settlement readings** if hydrotest follows (per iter 52).

### LLLLLLLL. Concrete Wall Sleeve — Pipe Penetration Sealing
- **Wall sleeve** = cylindrical insert **embedded w concrete wall** during pour, dla future pipe passage. Allows pipe replacement without breaking concrete + provides annular space for sealing.
- **Cel**:
  - **Pre-formed pipe penetration** through cast concrete walls.
  - **Flexibility** dla pipe replacement / modifications.
  - **Sealing surface** dla water/gas tight closure (with Link-Seal — covered iter 36, lub similar).
  - **Fire-rated penetration** dla code compliance.
- **Sleeve materials**:
  - **Schedule 40 black steel pipe** — most common dla industrial.
  - **Galvanized steel** — water-resistant.
  - **PVC** — light-duty, indoor.
  - **Fiberglass / FRP** — corrosive environments.
  - **Stainless steel** — premium, marine + chemical.
  - **HDPE** — flexible, modern.
- **Standard features**:
  - **Length** = wall thickness + 25-50 mm extension each side (dla flange / seal access).
  - **Diameter** = pipe OD + 25-50 mm annular gap dla seal.
  - **Water stop** — flange / collar w middle of sleeve, prevents water migration along sleeve OD.
  - **Smooth ID** — facilitates pipe insertion.
- **Water stop** designs:
  - **Integral steel collar** (welded) — most common dla steel sleeves.
  - **Rubber waterstop** — molded onto sleeve, 50-150 mm wide.
  - **Hydrophilic strip** — swells w water contact, additional seal.
  - **Bentonite rope** — site-installed dla unsealed sleeves.
- **Installation sequence** (cast-in-place concrete):
  1. **Layout** — exact position w concrete wall.
  2. **Anchor sleeve** to formwork (typowo welded to rebar lub strapped).
  3. **Center sleeve** w wall thickness.
  4. **Cap ends** during pour (prevent concrete entering).
  5. **Pour concrete** + cure.
  6. **Strip forms**, remove caps.
  7. **Inspect sleeve interior** — clean, no concrete inside.
  8. **Install pipe** through sleeve.
  9. **Install seal** w annular space (Link-Seal, mechanical seal, putty + caulk).
- **Retrofit installation** (existing wall):
  - **Core drill** through wall — diamond core drill creates clean hole.
  - **Slide sleeve** into hole.
  - **Grout annular space** (between sleeve OD + concrete) z hydraulic cement lub epoxy.
  - **Install pipe + seal** w sleeve.
- **External mount split wall sleeve**:
  - **Wraps around** existing pipe externally.
  - **Bolts together** at split joint.
  - **Used dla retrofit** without disconnecting pipe.
  - **Pressure capable** — withstands up to **40 psig** (92 ft head) w modular seals.
- **Sleeve sizing rules**:
  - **Annular gap** dla Link-Seal: 5-25 mm (specific per Link-Seal model).
  - **Annular gap dla mechanical seal**: 12-50 mm.
  - **NIE oversize** excessively — wastes material, harder to seal.
- **Fire-rated penetrations**:
  - **2-hour rating** dla critical fire walls.
  - **UL Listed** assemblies (sleeve + seal + caulk combinations).
  - **Tested with** specific pipe materials + diameters.
- **Pressure / hydrostatic ratings**:
  - **Standard Link-Seal** w sleeve: 20 psig (40 ft head).
  - **High-pressure variants**: 100+ psig.
  - **Burrowed thru-wall**: typowo low-pressure water (not high-pressure gas).
- **Standards**:
  - **CDB 805-030-019** dla plumbing piping (commercial buildings).
  - **NFPA / IBC** dla fire-rated penetrations.
  - **PHMSA** dla pipeline penetrations w containment dikes.
- **Common errors**:
  - **Oversized sleeve** — gap too large dla available seal.
  - **Off-center** w concrete — uneven annular gap.
  - **No water stop** — water migrates along sleeve OD into building.
  - **Damage during pour** — caps fall off, concrete enters.
- **Vendors**:
  - **Garlock / Link-Seal** — premier sealing system + sleeves.
  - **Metraflex** — sleeves + accessories.
  - **PSI Products (Germany)** — PipeX wall sleeve.
  - **Flexicraft** — penetration sleeves.

### MMMMMMMM. ANSI/ISEA 107 — High-Visibility Safety Apparel (Hi-Vis)
- **ANSI/ISEA 107-2020** ("High-Visibility Safety Apparel + Accessories") = standard dla **reflective + fluorescent garments**. Protects workers w **low-light, traffic, congested industrial environments**. **Workplace standard dla many trades**, w tym pipefitters / welders.
- **Why needed**:
  - **Low visibility** = top cause of worker accidents (struck-by hits w construction).
  - **Reflective material** returns light od headlights, lighting → worker visible.
  - **Fluorescent fabric** enhances daytime visibility (UV → visible light shift).
- **Classification system** (Type + Class):
- **Types** (intended use):
  - **Type O (Off-road)** — workers NOT exposed do roadway traffic:
    - Industrial parking lots, warehouses, oilfields away from public roads.
    - **Class 1 only** typowo.
  - **Type R (Roadway)** — exposed do **roadway traffic + temporary traffic control zones**:
    - Highway construction.
    - Utility work near streets.
    - **Class 2 lub 3**.
  - **Type P (Public safety)** — first responders, emergency personnel.
    - **Class 2 lub 3**.
- **Classes** (amount of high-vis material):
  - **Class 1** — minimum background + reflective:
    - **Background**: 217 sq in (1400 cm²).
    - **Reflective**: 155 sq in (1000 cm²).
    - **Aplikacja**: low-risk environments, daytime.
  - **Class 2** — moderate coverage:
    - **Background**: 775 sq in (5000 cm²).
    - **Reflective**: 201 sq in (1300 cm²).
    - **Aplikacja**: moderate traffic exposure.
  - **Class 3** — maximum coverage:
    - **Background**: 1240 sq in (8000 cm²).
    - **Reflective**: 310 sq in (2000 cm²).
    - **Sleeves required** dla full Class 3.
    - **Aplikacja**: high-traffic, low-light, distance recognition.
- **Allowed background colors**:
  - **Fluorescent yellow-green** — most visible w daytime (bright sunlight).
  - **Fluorescent orange-red** — high visibility, also dla forestry / hunting.
  - **Fluorescent red** — emergency / public safety.
- **Reflective stripe materials**:
  - **Silver retroreflective** — most common, balanced day/night.
  - **Silver-yellow-silver** — enhanced visibility.
  - **Microprism film** — superior reflectance vs glass-bead.
- **Garment types covered**:
  - **Safety vests** — most common.
  - **Shirts + jackets**.
  - **Coveralls + rain gear**.
  - **Pants** (Class 1 only — half-body coverage).
  - **Headwear** — caps + hard hat strips.
  - **Footwear stripes**.
- **Garment care**:
  - **Reflective material degrades** z UV exposure + washing.
  - **Replacement**: typowo 1-3 years depending on use.
  - **Visible damage** (cracks, fading, missing reflective tape) — replace immediately.
- **Pipefitter / welder-specific concerns**:
  - **Flame resistance** (FR) — typical hi-vis polyester melts. **Welder needs FR + Hi-Vis combo**:
    - **FR cotton + reflective tape**.
    - **Modacrylic blends** — FR + Hi-Vis fluorescent.
    - **NFPA 2112 + ANSI 107 dual certification** required dla hot work near traffic.
  - **Comfort** — breathable, lightweight crucial dla long shifts.
  - **Pockets + tool loops** — practical features.
- **OSHA requirements**:
  - **23 CFR 634** — Federal Highway Administration: hi-vis required dla workers w right-of-way.
  - **OSHA 1926.201** — Signaling.
  - **Construction site rules** vary by employer + jurisdiction.
- **Common errors**:
  - **Wrong Class** dla traffic exposure.
  - **Faded / damaged** apparel still used.
  - **NIE FR** dla welders (fire hazard).
- **Vendors**:
  - **Ergodyne GloWear**.
  - **PIP (Protective Industrial Products)**.
  - **3M Reflective Material Division**.
  - **Bulwark FR** (FR + hi-vis combo).
  - **DragonWear** (FR + hi-vis).

## Iteration 65 — 2026-05-25

### NNNNNNNN. Rupture Disk / Burst Disc — Pressure Safety Device
- **Rupture disk (burst disc)** = **single-use pressure relief device** — thin metal diaphragm that **bursts at predetermined pressure**, releasing contents do safe area. **Faster + tighter sealing** than spring-loaded relief valves (PRV/PSV).
- **Different from PRV (Pressure Relief Valve)**:
  - **PRV** opens at set pressure, **reseats** when pressure drops, **reusable**.
  - **Rupture disk** opens (bursts) at set pressure, **stays open** → **system depressurizes completely** → disc REPLACED before restart.
- **Aplikacje**:
  - **Primary relief** dla critical / fast-acting service.
  - **Secondary safety** behind PRV (back-up if PRV fails).
  - **In-series with PRV** — disc protects PRV from corrosion + leak, both must fail dla disc burst.
  - **Vacuum protection** — special vacuum discs prevent tank collapse.
  - **Fire safety** — emergency vent dla pool fire scenarios.
- **Two main types** (orientation matters!):
  - **Forward-acting disc** (FAD, tension-loaded):
    - **Pressure on CONCAVE side** (process side).
    - **Disc dome shape** opens outward as pressure tensile stresses metal.
    - **Older technology**, simpler manufacturing.
    - **Limitation**: prone to **fatigue z pressure cycling**, may burst PREMATURELY below rated pressure after many cycles.
    - **Maximum operating pressure (MOP)**: typowo **70% of marked burst pressure**.
  - **Reverse-buckling disc** (RBD, compression-loaded):
    - **Pressure on CONVEX side** (process side).
    - **Disc buckles inward** when burst pressure reached.
    - **Compression loading** — resists fatigue better.
    - **Maximum operating pressure (MOP)**: up to **90% of marked burst pressure** (better dla cyclic).
    - **Cleaner break + lower fragmentation** vs forward-acting.
    - **STANDARD modern choice**.
- **Burst initiation mechanism**:
  - **Score line** (groove) on disc surface — controlled burst location.
  - **Knife blades** w holder — slice disc when buckled (reverse-acting).
  - **Buckling pin** — non-fragmenting design (pin holds disc, deforms at burst pressure).
- **Disc holder + safety head**:
  - **Two-piece body** — clamps disc between flanges.
  - **Often mounted in 90° from process pipe** (vent direction).
  - **Drain hole** dla detecting leakage.
- **Materials**:
  - **Inconel** — premium, corrosion-resistant.
  - **Hastelloy C276** — extreme chemistry.
  - **316L SS** — standard chemicals.
  - **Tantalum** — HCl service.
  - **Monel** — HF service.
  - **Aluminum, copper, gold** — specialty applications.
- **Specifications**:
  - **Burst pressure** — set point (e.g., 150 psig).
  - **Burst pressure tolerance** — typowo **±5%** (ASME Section VIII requirement).
  - **Maximum operating pressure (MOP)** vs burst rating.
  - **Temperature rating** — affects burst pressure (typowo specified at 22°C; adjustment factor needed dla operating T).
  - **Differential pressure (vacuum support)** — disc must withstand vacuum without inverting.
- **Standards**:
  - **ASME Section VIII Div 1, UG-127** — Pressure relief device requirements.
  - **API 520/521** — Sizing + selection (similar to PRV).
  - **API 526** — Flanged steel relief valves (some applicable).
  - **ASME PTC 25** — Performance test code for pressure relief devices.
- **Installation**:
  - **Specific orientation** — convex side towards process dla reverse-acting.
  - **Wrong orientation** = burst pressure WAY above rated (catastrophic failure of vessel before disc bursts).
  - **Disc holder bolted** between standard pipe flanges.
  - **Leak detection** — pressure gauge between disc + downstream valve (if used) detects disc damage.
  - **Pre-installation inspection** — visual, no dents, no surface damage.
- **Replacement procedure**:
  1. **Isolate** system + depressurize.
  2. **LOTO** safety procedures.
  3. **Remove damaged disc** from holder.
  4. **Inspect holder** — no scoring, gaskets fresh.
  5. **Install new disc** — verify ORIENTATION + burst pressure tag matches spec.
  6. **Torque bolts** per holder specification.
  7. **Update LOG** — date, serial #, burst pressure.
- **Common errors**:
  - **Wrong orientation** — disc inverted (most catastrophic).
  - **Used disc reused** (single-use only).
  - **Damaged disc installed** — dent / pinhole reduces burst pressure.
  - **Wrong size / pressure** dla service.
  - **Burst delayed** — flow path obstructed (block valve closed downstream).
- **Vendors**:
  - **BS&B Safety Systems** — premier (ASME).
  - **ZOOK Disk + Holder**.
  - **Fike Corporation**.
  - **OseCo (OPW Engineered Systems)**.
  - **Continental Disc Corporation**.

### OOOOOOOO. Buried Pipeline Hydrostatic Test — Procedure + Verification
- **Buried pipeline hydrotest** = **pressure testing buried pipeline** before commissioning. Different from above-ground (more challenging — no visual leak inspection from outside).
- **Differences vs above-ground hydrotest** (covered iter 5):
  - **Cannot visually inspect joints** (buried).
  - **Backfill in place** during test typowo.
  - **Soil temperature** affects water temperature (potential air pocket issues).
  - **Longer hold times** required dla leak detection.
- **Test sequence**:
  1. **Pipeline installed + welded** completely.
  2. **Coating + cathodic protection** installed.
  3. **NDE complete** — RT/AUT all welds.
  4. **Backfill** (typowo to operating depth).
  5. **Cleaning / pigging** — flush debris.
  6. **Filling z water** dla hydrotest.
  7. **Pressurization**.
  8. **Hold + verification**.
  9. **Depressurization + draining**.
- **Filling procedure** (KRYTYCZNE):
  - **Treated water** — chlorinated, deoxygenated dla corrosion prevention.
  - **Fill from LOWEST elevation point** upward.
  - **Vents open** at highest points dla air escape.
  - **NO air pockets** — air compresses, makes pressure unreliable + dangerous.
  - **Fill rate**: 1-2 m³/min typowy dla DN 600 pipeline.
  - **Inlet temperature monitored** — large temperature swings cause pressure spikes.
- **Pressurization**:
  - **Gradual ramp** — 10% per 30 min typowo, do test pressure.
  - **Hold at 25% test pressure** dla preliminary check (vents, fittings sealed).
  - **Continue do 50%, 75%, 100% test pressure** w stages.
- **Test pressure** (API 1110, ASME B31.4, B31.8):
  - **Oil pipeline (B31.4)**: **1.25 × MAWP** minimum.
  - **Gas pipeline (B31.8)**: **1.25-1.4 × MAOP** (varies by class location).
  - **High-pressure pipeline (oil & gas)**: typowo **125% of MAWP** at any point along length.
  - **Process piping (B31.3)**: 1.5× MAWP.
- **Hold time**:
  - **Strength test**: **4 hours minimum** at full test pressure.
  - **Leak tightness test**: **24 hours** at slightly reduced pressure dla leak monitoring.
  - **Critical pipeline**: 72+ hours hold.
- **Leak detection methods** (without visual inspection):
  - **Pressure decay monitoring**:
    - **Pressure recorder** (chart recorder lub digital) — continuous P + T tracking.
    - **Allowable pressure drop** based on thermal correction.
    - **Temperature compensation**: ΔP_thermal = α × E × ΔT (water + steel both expand).
    - **Beyond allowable decay** = leak detected.
  - **Volume measurement**:
    - **Make-up water added** during hold = leak volume.
    - **Tracks via metering**.
  - **Tracer testing** (post-completion):
    - **Helium tracer** added to water → sniffer survey above ground (specialty).
- **Settlement verification** (for buried pipeline new construction):
  - **Post-test pipeline alignment survey** — verify no significant settlement.
  - **Top-of-pipe elevation** measured at intervals.
  - **Beyond tolerance** = foundation issue, may require remediation.
- **Acceptance criteria**:
  - **PHMSA 49 CFR 192/195** (USA) — pipeline safety regulations.
  - **API 1110** — Pressure Testing of Steel Pipelines for the Transportation of Gas, Petroleum Gas, Hazardous Liquids, Highly Volatile Liquids, or Carbon Dioxide.
  - **Pass criterion**: stable pressure z thermal correction within tolerance.
  - **Re-test** if anomalous decay (re-locate + repair leak).
- **Post-test**:
  - **Drain water** through low-point drains.
  - **Treat / dispose** water (per environmental permit).
  - **Dry pipe** dla gas service (dew point < operating dew point).
  - **Internal coating** dla long-term storage if delayed startup.
- **Special concerns**:
  - **Cold weather**: water freezes — antifreeze (glycol) added.
  - **Wide diameter pipeline**: very large water volumes (megaliters).
  - **Long distance**: thermal gradients along route cause varying expansion.
- **Failure scenarios**:
  - **Rupture** at test pressure — design issue, re-design + replace.
  - **Welding defect leak** — identify by pressure decay + acoustic monitoring, repair specific joint.
  - **Backfill damage** during test — soil pressure on coating defect → corrosion path activated.
- **Vendors / contractors**: specialized pipeline testing service providers (NCS Fluid Handling, NiGen, Furmanite, T.D. Williamson testing services).

### PPPPPPPP. Welding Wire Spool Storage — Humidity Control + Reconditioning
- **Welding wire moisture** = silent enemy of weld quality. **Moisture → hydrogen w arc → hydrogen embrittlement + porosity**.
- **Why moisture critical**:
  - **Solid wire** (GMAW): surface absorption (moderate impact).
  - **Flux-cored wire** (FCAW): flux hygroscopic — significantly worse.
  - **SMAW electrodes**: most hygroscopic (covered iter 26 rod oven).
- **Storage conditions** (KRYTYCZNE):
  - **Temperature**: **10-32°C (50-90°F)** — stable.
  - **Relative humidity**: **≤ 50%** (ideal), absolute maximum **70%**.
  - **No direct contact z floor** (concrete absorbs moisture).
  - **Sealed packaging maintained** until use.
- **Dew point principle**:
  - **Storage temperature MUST be above dew point** dla given humidity.
  - **Lower → condensation forms ON wire** — saturated.
  - **Heated storage room** lub climate-controlled warehouse.
- **Exposure limits** (after package opened):
  - **Hydrogen-controlled wires (H4, H8, H16 designation)**:
    - **H4**: max **72 hours** ambient exposure.
  - **Standard wires** (non-hydrogen-designated):
    - **< 2 weeks** typowo allowed.
  - **Beyond limit** = wire MUST be reconditioned (baked) lub discarded.
- **Reconditioning** (rebaking):
  - **Wire on metal spools / Masonite / wire baskets** — can be rebaked:
    - **Temperature**: **230-300°F (110-150°C)**.
    - **Duration**: **6-12 hours minimum**.
    - **Drives moisture out** of flux + surface.
  - **Plastic spools** — CANNOT rebake (plastic melts).
    - **Discard** if moisture-exposed beyond limit.
    - **Only buy wire on metal spools** dla critical applications.
- **By wire type**:
  - **Solid wire (ER70S-6)**:
    - **Copper-coated** — rust-resistant.
    - **Cover when not in use** — dust prevention.
    - **Long shelf life** w sealed package (years).
  - **Flux-cored arc welding (FCAW)**:
    - **Most moisture-sensitive** of wire types.
    - **Vacuum-packed** preferred.
    - **Sealed packaging** must remain intact.
    - **Seamless cored wire** (some brands) — less moisture absorption than seamed.
  - **Stainless steel (304L, 316L, 309L)**:
    - **Less prone to rust** ale moisture still absorbed.
    - **Worm tracking** w weld (visible track marks) — sign of moisture contamination.
  - **Aluminum (ER4043, ER5356)**:
    - **Oxidizes rapidly** even w dry conditions.
    - **Sealed in original packaging** until use.
    - **Wrap remaining spool** w plastic + desiccant.
- **Spool condition issues**:
  - **Rust on solid wire** — wire shaving w drive rolls, contact tip contamination, arc instability.
  - **Damp flux core** — porosity, erratic arc, "worm tracking" patterns w weld.
  - **Wire stuck on spool** — humidity caused wire layers to bond.
- **Best practices**:
  - **FIFO inventory** (First In, First Out) — oldest wire used first.
  - **Climate-controlled warehouse** — temperature + humidity.
  - **Cover spool when not in use** — plastic bag with desiccant.
  - **Welder's wire feed cabinet** — heated storage at workstation.
  - **Limit exposure time** — return wire to climate-controlled storage at shift end.
- **Specialty conditioning equipment**:
  - **Wire conditioning ovens** (Phoenix DryRod, Lincoln Electric) — heat large spools dla reconditioning.
  - **Dehumidified wire dispensers** — climate-controlled wire feed cabinets.
- **Hydrogen testing** dla critical service:
  - **Diffusible hydrogen content** measured per AWS A4.3.
  - **Sampling**: wire bead deposited, gas extracted, measured.
  - **Typical limits**: H4 ≤ 4 mL/100g, H8 ≤ 8 mL/100g, H16 ≤ 16 mL/100g.
