- **Welding requirements**:
  - **WPS specific dla sour service** (PWHT, low-H electrodes, hardness post-weld).
  - **PWHT temperature**: typically 595-650°C dla CS, time = 1 h per inch.
  - **Hardness mapping post-PWHT**: 100% welds verified.
  - **Low-S, low-P steel**: S ≤0.005%, P ≤0.020% — reduce inclusions (HIC initiation).
- **Inclusion control**:
  - **HIC-tested plate** — NACE TM0284 test (4-week immersion in H₂S solution, cross-section examination dla cracks).
  - **CLR (Crack Length Ratio)**: max 15%.
  - **CTR (Crack Thickness Ratio)**: max 5%.
  - **CSR (Crack Sensitivity Ratio)**: max 2%.
  - **Plate orientation**: rolling direction known + controlled.
- **Inspection requirements**:
  - **Post-installation**:
    - **WFMT (Wet Fluorescent MT)** all welds + HAZ.
    - **UT scanning** dla HIC (look dla internal blisters/cracks).
    - **Hardness check** field-portable tester.
  - **In-service** (every 2-3 years):
    - WFMT critical welds.
    - UT thickness mapping.
    - Hydrogen permeation monitoring (probes installed).
- **Field hardness testing**:
  - **Portable hardness testers** (Brinell, Equotip, UCI):
    - **Equotip / Leeb** rebound — easy field use, ±10 HV accuracy.
    - **Ultrasonic Contact Impedance (UCI)** — accurate, requires smooth surface.
    - **Portable Rockwell C** — direct HRC, classical method.
  - **Calibration** before each use — reference block.
- **Documentation chain**:
  - **Material Test Report (MTR)** — chemistry + mechanical + hardness from mill.
  - **PMI (Positive Material ID)** — verify on every component.
  - **HIC test certificate** dla plate-derived components.
  - **WPS-NACE** linked.
  - **Hardness records** — every weld documented.
  - **PWHT chart** — proof of heat treatment.
- **Application examples**:
  - **Sour gas wells + processing** — onshore + offshore.
  - **Refinery hydroprocessing** — hydrotreater, hydrocracker (H₂S generated).
  - **Sour water stripper** — refinery vessel.
  - **Pipeline transporting sour gas/oil** — covered B31.4/B31.8 z NACE addendum.
  - **Amine treater** (covered iter 81) — H₂S in solution, full NACE compliance.
- **Common errors**:
  - **Wrong bolt grade** (B16 used instead B7M) — SSC w months.
  - **Skipped PWHT** dla sour welds — residual stress + HAZ hardness.
  - **Plate without HIC test** — blistering after months service.
  - **No hardness mapping post-weld** — out-of-spec welds w service.
  - **Field repair without NACE-compliant WPS** — failure point.
- **Practical fitter rules**:
  - **NACE-stamped components only** dla sour service — verify każdy w MTR.
  - **Dedicated tools** for sour service work — no cross-contamination z non-sour shop.
  - **Welder qualification** — separate NACE-compliant WPS.
  - **PMI 100%** components — alloy substitution discovery.
  - **Hardness testing 100%** welds — field-portable z calibration.
  - **Storage**: keep NACE components separate w warehouse — labeled, tagged.
  - **Refusal authority**: any inspector can reject suspected non-NACE component → re-verify.

### OOOOOOOOOOO. Titanium / Zirconium Welding — Reactive Metal Procedures
- **Po co dziać**: Ti + Zr są **reactive metals** — w high temperatures (>200-300°C) **rapidly absorb** O₂, N₂, H, C z atmosphere → form brittle oxides/nitrides/hydrides → weld embrittled + cracked.
- **Aplikacje typowe**:
  - **Titanium (Ti Grade 1, 2, 5, 7, 12)**:
    - Aerospace (light, strong, T resistant).
    - Chemical industry (excellent corrosion resistance dla HCl, FeCl₃, seawater).
    - Heat exchangers (chemical, desalination).
    - Pharma (biocompatible).
  - **Zirconium (Zr 702, 705)**:
    - Nuclear (fuel rod cladding — neutron transparent).
    - Highly corrosive chemicals (concentrated acids — sulfuric, nitric, hydrochloric).
- **Welding processes** (preferred):
  - **GTAW (TIG)** — primary method, controlled heat input, clean.
  - **Plasma arc welding (PAW)** — keyhole mode dla deep penetration.
  - **EBW (Electron Beam Welding)** — vacuum chamber, highest quality.
  - **Laser welding** — small parts, automated.
  - **NIE używać**: SMAW (flux contamination), GMAW (oxidation likely), FCAW (flux).
- **Color guide** dla weld inspection (Ti):
  - **Bright silver / shiny** — perfect, <100 ppm O.
  - **Light straw / gold** — minor oxidation, **REPAIRABLE** (light brush + re-weld).
  - **Dark blue / dark gold** — moderate oxidation, **MARGINAL** — engineering judgment.
  - **Purple / dark blue** — significant oxidation — **REJECT + grind out**.
  - **Grey / matte** — severe oxidation, **REJECT** (brittle alpha case).
  - **White / yellow flaky** — catastrophic — entire section discarded.
- **Color guide dla Zr** — similar progression, ale stricter tolerance (lower O limit).
- **Oxygen + impurity limits during welding**:
  - **Titanium**: <**50 ppm O₂** w purge gas during weld.
  - **Zirconium**: <**20 ppm O₂** (more strict).
  - **Hydrogen**: <10 ppm dew point in shielding gas (-76°F dew point).
  - **Argon purity**: ≥**99.995%** weld-grade.
- **Shielding gas system** — KRYTYCZNE:
  - **3 zones protection**:
    1. **Arc shielding** — primary TIG cup, covers arc + weld pool.
    2. **Trailing shield** — extends primary, covers cooling weld 20-30 mm behind torch.
    3. **Back purge** — inside pipe / behind weld (back side).
  - **Why trailing shield**: weld stays >200°C dla seconds-minutes after arc passes → cont. oxidation if exposed.
- **Trailing shield design**:
  - **Slot 50-150 mm long** behind torch.
  - **Gas distributed** via diffuser plate (sintered metal) lub flow channels.
  - **Flow rate**: 20+ CFH (10+ L/min).
  - **Critical dla all-position pipe welding** — weld cools w gravity-affected pattern.
- **Back purge** — inside pipe:
  - **Argon** flushed through pipe interior.
  - **Bladders / dams** (covered iter 83) — limit volume.
  - **Pre-purge time**: 10-15 minutes typowo (5+ pipe volumes).
  - **Flow rate**: 20-50 L/h zależnie od pipe size.
  - **O₂ monitoring**: at exhaust → continuous, alarm if rises.
- **Surface preparation**:
  - **Pre-clean** weld zone 25 mm each side.
  - **Mechanical** — stainless wire brush (dedicated Ti/Zr only).
  - **Chemical clean** — acetone wipe (no Cl⁻!) + alcohol final.
  - **NIE używać** standard cleaner (chloride contamination → embrittlement).
  - **NIE używać** Cl⁻-containing marker pens — Sharpie!
- **Filler metal**:
  - **Ti**: ER Ti grade matching base (ERTi-1 dla Grade 1, ERTi-5 dla Grade 5 Ti-6Al-4V).
  - **Zr**: ER Zr-2/4 dla Zr 702.
  - **Storage**: vacuum-packed lub argon-filled containers (oxidizes shelf-life).
  - **Handling**: clean white cotton gloves only (skin oils contaminate).
- **Pre-weld checks**:
  - **Argon analyzer** at supply — verify purity.
  - **O₂ monitor** at purge outlet — verify <50 ppm.
  - **Visual inspection** prep surfaces — discoloration = rework.
  - **Welder skin contact prevention** — gloves + tool isolation.
- **WPS specifics dla Ti**:
  - **Lower heat input** vs steel (Ti thermal conductivity 1/7 of CS).
  - **Slower travel speed** — controlled cooling.
  - **No preheat** typowo (Ti doesn't need it, may oxidize).
  - **No PWHT** standardly — Ti work-hardens, PWHT relieves stress only.
- **Post-weld inspection**:
  - **Visual color** — primary acceptance (cited above).
  - **VT 100%** (visual).
  - **PT 100%** (dye penetrant — verify no surface cracks).
  - **RT/UT** dla critical service (pressure vessels).
  - **Hardness** post-weld — verify spec.
  - **Bend test** dla qualification coupons.
- **Repair**:
  - **Light discoloration**: brush z stainless wire wheel → re-clean → re-weld.
  - **Heavy discoloration / alpha case**: **grind out + re-weld** — must remove all embrittled material.
  - **Through-wall failure**: cut out section + new piece welded.
- **Common errors**:
  - **No trailing shield** — weld bead OK but HAZ contaminated.
  - **Insufficient back purge time** — initial welds oxidized.
  - **Wrong filler grade** — mismatched Ti grades crack.
  - **Cl⁻ contamination** (marker, cleaning fluid) — embrittlement after weeks/months.
  - **Skin contact during weld prep** — oil + salt deposits → contamination at re-weld.
  - **Sharing tooling z carbon steel** — Fe contamination → SCC.
- **Practical fitter rules**:
  - **Dedicated Ti / Zr workspace** — separate od carbon steel shop.
  - **Color-coded tools** — only used Ti work.
  - **Clean gloves** every workstation — disposable nitrile preferred.
  - **No grinding sparks** in welding area — Fe contamination.
  - **Pre-fab in shop** — controlled environment, minimum field work.
  - **Argon supply dedicated** — high-purity manifold, no cross-contamination.
  - **Training mandatory** — welders certified specifically dla reactive metals.
  - **Cost**: 5-10× standard SS welding cost — justified by special service requirements.

### PPPPPPPPPPP. ASME B31 Family — Code Comparison + Selection Guide
- **Co to**: ASME B31 jest **rodzina kodów** dla różnych typów piping. **Nie jedna code** — każdy adresuje specific industry / service. Selection critical dla **legal compliance + safety**.
- **Główne kody (most relevant dla fitters)**:
  - **B31.1 — Power Piping** — power generation, **boilers + steam systems**.
  - **B31.3 — Process Piping** — chemical, petrochemical, pharmaceutical, refineries.
  - **B31.4 — Liquid Pipeline Transportation** — cross-country oil + petroleum + slurry pipelines.
  - **B31.5 — Refrigeration Piping + Heat Transfer Components** — refrigerant systems.
  - **B31.8 — Gas Transmission + Distribution** — natural gas cross-country + city distribution.
  - **B31.9 — Building Services Piping** — heating, cooling, plumbing w buildings.
  - **B31.12 — Hydrogen Piping + Pipelines** (covered iter 89) — H₂ specific.
- **Selection logic** — często overlap, hierarchical:
  - **Within process facility** (refinery, chem plant) → **B31.3**.
  - **Power plant steam-water cycle** → **B31.1**.
  - **Cross-country liquid (crude oil, products, water)** → **B31.4**.
  - **Cross-country / distribution gas** → **B31.8**.
  - **Building HVAC + plumbing** → **B31.9**.
  - **Hydrogen** → **B31.12** (regardless of location).
- **B31.1 (Power Piping) vs B31.3 (Process Piping)** — najczęściej confused:
  - **Safety factor (design margin)**:
    - **B31.1**: **4:1** (SMTS/4) — historical, public safety.
    - **B31.3**: **3:1** (SMTS/3) — economic balance dla complex facilities.
  - **Allowable stress**: B31.3 higher allowable → cheaper piping dla same conditions.
  - **Code longevity**: B31.1 piping 30-50 year design life; B31.3 process loops often replaced 10-20 years.
- **Material handling**:
  - **B31.1**: focused on **CS + alloy steels** (boiler grade, steam service). Limited non-metallic.
  - **B31.3**: extensive coverage **CS, SS, Ni alloys, plastics, exotic** (Hastelloy, titanium, FRP).
- **Pressure-temperature ratings**:
  - **B31.1**: high T (500-600°C steam), high P (200-350 bar).
  - **B31.3**: variable — cryogenic (-200°C) do **process T** (500-600°C w specific service).
- **B31.4 (Liquid Pipeline)**:
  - **Aplikacja**: crude oil, refined products, slurry, anhydrous ammonia transport — **between facilities**.
  - **Design factor F**: **0.72 constant** dla main lines (vs B31.8 location-dependent).
  - **Burial requirements**, **right-of-way**, **environmental considerations**.
  - **Long-distance flow assurance**: hydraulic calcs, drag reduction agents.
- **B31.8 (Gas Pipeline)**:
  - **Aplikacja**: **natural gas**, fuel gas, syngas — cross-country + distribution.
  - **Design factor F**: **variable** z **location class**:
    - **Class 1** (rural, <10 buildings/mile): F = 0.72.
    - **Class 2** (suburban, 10-46 buildings/mile): F = 0.60.
    - **Class 3** (urban, ≥46 buildings/mile): F = 0.50.
    - **Class 4** (high-density buildings ≥4 stories): F = 0.40.
  - **Stricter requirements w populated areas** — population density-based safety.
- **B31.5 (Refrigeration Piping)**:
  - **Refrigerants**: ammonia, HCFC/HFC, propane.
  - **Aplikacje**: HVAC large systems, food processing, cryogenic.
  - **Similar to B31.3** but refrigerant-specific.
- **B31.9 (Building Services)**:
  - **Plumbing, HVAC, low-pressure steam** w commercial/residential.
  - **Lower pressure ratings** vs B31.3.
  - **Simplified design** — copper, PVC, cast iron common.
- **Other codes** (specialized):
  - **B31.11** — Slurry Transportation (mining, tailings — covered iter 84).
  - **B31E** — seismic design of piping (companion document).
  - **B31G** — manual dla evaluation of remaining strength corroded pipelines.
- **Selection criteria** (decision tree):
  1. **Is it building plumbing/HVAC?** → B31.9.
  2. **Is it refrigerant?** → B31.5.
  3. **Is it hydrogen?** → B31.12.
  4. **Is it cross-country (between facilities)?**:
     - Liquid → B31.4.
     - Gas → B31.8.
  5. **Is it inside facility?**:
     - Power plant steam → B31.1.
     - Everything else → B31.3.
- **Jurisdictional aspects**:
  - **Boiler external piping** dla power plant — B31.1 + ASME BPV Section I overlap.
  - **Vessel external piping** dla pressure vessel — B31.3 + ASME BPV Section VIII overlap.
  - **State / country laws**: many jurisdictions require **specific code adoption** — verify legal requirement.
- **International equivalents**:
  - **B31.1** ≈ **EN 13480** dla industrial piping (Europe).
  - **B31.3** ≈ **EN 13480** + **PED (Pressure Equipment Directive)**.
  - **B31.4 / B31.8** ≈ **EN 14161** + **DNV-OS-F101** dla pipelines.
- **Mixing codes** — NOT allowed:
  - **Don't combine B31.1 + B31.3 sections** w single project — choose one, stick with it.
  - **Boundary between codes** w specific document section — clearly defined.
- **Common errors**:
  - **Wrong code applied** — e.g., B31.1 on refinery piping (overdesigned, expensive) lub B31.3 na power plant (underdesigned, safety risk).
  - **Mixed code requirements** — partial compliance one code + partial other = none compliant.
  - **Jurisdictional adoption** missed — local fire authority requires specific code.
- **Practical fitter rules**:
  - **Project P&IDs** — code reference printed (B31.3-CL150, etc.).
  - **WPS-WPQ matched do code** — qualified per applicable code.
  - **Welder certification** — typically B31.3 broad coverage (also covers B31.1 w many cases).
  - **NDT requirements** vary by code — verify w project spec.
  - **Hydrotest requirements** — different per code (B31.1 = 1.5× design, B31.3 = 1.5× design @ T compensation).
  - **PWHT requirements** — vary, B31.1 more stringent typically.
  - **Material spec** — code-specific allowed materials, verify B16.5 + B36.10 references.

---

## Iteration 92 — 2026-05-26

### QQQQQQQQQQQ. Steam Methane Reformer (SMR) — H₂ Production Piping
- **Co to**: largest commercial source of hydrogen (≈95% global production). Convert **methane (CH₄) + steam (H₂O)** → **H₂ + CO** w high-T catalyst-filled tubes.
- **Aplikacje**:
  - **Refinery hydrogen supply** — hydroprocessing units (hydrotreater, hydrocracker).
  - **Ammonia production** (NH₃ via Haber process — H₂ + N₂).
  - **Methanol production**.
  - **Fuel cell H₂ supply** (small scale).
  - **Growing**: clean H₂ z carbon capture (Blue Hydrogen).
- **Chemistry**:
  - **Reforming reaction**: CH₄ + H₂O → CO + 3H₂ (endothermic, +206 kJ/mol).
  - **Water-gas shift**: CO + H₂O → CO₂ + H₂ (exothermic, -41 kJ/mol).
  - **Net result**: 1 mole CH₄ + 2 moles H₂O → 4 moles H₂ + 1 mole CO₂.
- **Operating conditions**:
  - **Reformer T**: **815-925°C (1500-1700°F)**.
  - **Pressure**: **15-40 bar** typowo (lower P thermodynamically favored, but compression downstream costly → compromise).
  - **Steam:carbon ratio**: 2.5-3.5:1 (excess steam prevents coke formation).
- **Reformer furnace** layout:
  - **Vertical fired furnace** — refractory-lined box.
  - **200-400 catalyst tubes** hanging vertically inside.
  - **Side burners** lub **top burners** (radiant section).
  - **Convection section** dla feed preheat, steam superheat, BFW preheat.
  - **Stack** — flue gas exit, often w heat recovery.
- **Catalyst tubes** — CRITICAL component:
  - **Materials**:
    - **HK-40 (Fe-Cr-Ni cast)** — 25Cr-20Ni — historical, do 950°C wall T.
    - **HP-Mod (HP-NbTi)** — 25Cr-35Ni microalloyed with Nb + Ti — improved creep resistance, current standard.
    - **Manaurite XM** — Manoir Industries grade, similar to HP-Mod.
    - **Centralloy ET 45** — Schmidt+Clemens equivalent.
  - **Wall thickness**: 8-15 mm typically.
  - **OD**: 100-150 mm (4-6").
  - **Length**: 10-14 m vertical tubes.
  - **Service life**: design 100,000 hours (11-12 years) — z creep limit.
- **Tube failure modes**:
  - **Creep rupture** — primary lifetime limit; tubes elongate under stress, micro-voids form, coalesce → through-wall.
  - **Carburization** — z process gas (CH₄, CO) high carbon activity → Cr depletion + microstructure damage.
  - **Sigma phase precipitation** — embrittlement w 700-870°C zone.
  - **Coking** — carbon deposits inside tube → hot spots → premature failure.
  - **Flame impingement** — burner misaligned → localized overheating → rapid creep.
- **Tube inspection** w turnarounds:
  - **Visual** od inside (borescope) + outside.
  - **Diameter measurement** — laser gauging, identifies creep elongation.
  - **Eddy current** dla microstructure changes (sigma phase).
  - **Replicate metallography** — sample tubes destructively + microstructure analysis.
  - **Decision**: replace tubes z >2% diameter creep lub significant microstructure damage.
- **Pigtails (transfer piping)**:
  - **Connect catalyst tubes to outlet manifold**.
  - **Material**: **Incoloy 800H / 800HT** lub specialty **Alloy 803**.
  - **Small bore** (typically 25-50 mm).
  - **Subject to thermal cycling** + stress → fatigue critical.
  - **NDT every turnaround** — UT, MT, replicate metallography.
- **Outlet (transfer) line / collector**:
  - **Hot gas** (800-900°C) z all tubes collected w single header.
  - **Refractory-lined CS shell** — outer CS pipe, **internal castable refractory** insulation (200-300 mm).
  - **Wall T**: outer CS ~300-400°C, inner refractory 800-900°C.
  - **Why this design**: pure alloy pipe would be 50-100× more expensive at this size; refractory cheaper.
- **Process gas cooler**:
  - Cools reformer effluent od 850°C do ~350°C.
  - **Generates HP steam** (waste heat recovery boiler).
  - **Wall material**: bayonet tubes (Incoloy 825) lub specialty alloys.
- **Shift reactors**:
  - **High-T shift** (HTS): 350-400°C, Fe-Cr catalyst.
  - **Low-T shift** (LTS): 200-250°C, Cu-Zn catalyst.
  - **Material**: CS w high-T shift, CS lub SS w low-T.
  - **CO converted** to CO₂ + more H₂.
- **PSA (Pressure Swing Adsorption)**:
  - **Purification H₂** do 99.99%+ purity.
  - **Adsorbent beds** (zeolite, activated C) — adsorb CO₂, CH₄, CO, N₂.
  - **Cycle pressure** — purge low P, adsorb high P.
  - **Piping**: CS, ANSI 300-600 typowo.
- **Welding catalyst tube material**:
  - **HP-Mod welds**: specialized procedure, **filler ENi-3 (ERNiCr-3)** lub similar Ni-based.
  - **PWHT**: typically NOT done (austenitic structure, no martensite).
  - **NDT**: 100% RT + PT.
  - **Repair welds w service**: difficult, often replace tube vs repair.
- **Common errors**:
  - **Wrong tube material** — cheaper grade leads to early failure.
  - **Tube material mixed** in single furnace — creep behavior different, uneven life.
  - **Burner misalignment** — flame impingement → hot spot.
  - **Steam ratio drop** — coke formation → tube hot spot.
  - **Skipped tube inspection** — failure during service = unplanned shutdown 4-8 weeks.
  - **Pigtail weld defects** — small bore, NDT 100% critical.
- **Practical fitter rules**:
  - **Pre-startup pigtail weld inspection** — 100% RT + PT.
  - **Tube replacement procedure** — controlled cool-down (steam-only purge) before tube removal.
  - **Welder qualification** — specific WPS dla HP-Mod / Incoloy 800.
  - **Material traceability** — every tube MTR, batch tracked, position recorded.
  - **Temperature monitoring** — pyrometers + thermocouples on tube walls.
  - **Long lead times** — tube replacement requires 6-12 months ordering w manufacturer.

### RRRRRRRRRRR. Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR) + Management of Change (MOC) — OSHA PSM 1910.119
- **Co to PSSR**: **formal verification process** before commissioning OR restart **new lub modified facility / process**. Wymaga **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119** (Process Safety Management) dla **highly hazardous chemicals** (refinery, chemical plant).
- **OSHA PSM** zakres (1910.119):
  - **Covered processes**:
    - **Threshold quantities** specific chemicals (Appendix A — H₂S 1500 lb, methane 10000 lb, HF 1000 lb, etc.).
    - **Flammable liquids/gases >10,000 lb**.
  - **14 PSM elements**:
    1. Employee participation.
    2. Process safety information (PSI).
    3. Process hazard analysis (PHA).
    4. Operating procedures.
    5. Training.
    6. Contractors.
    7. **PSSR (Pre-Startup Safety Review)**.
    8. Mechanical integrity.
    9. Hot work permit.
    10. **Management of Change (MOC)**.
    11. Incident investigation.
    12. Emergency planning.
    13. Compliance audits.
    14. Trade secrets.
- **PSSR triggers**:
  - **New facility commissioning** (greenfield).
  - **Modified facility** — significant enough to require PSI update.
  - **Restart after major maintenance** (turnaround).
  - **Restart after extended shutdown** (>some time threshold).
- **PSSR scope** — verify everything ready before introducing chemicals:
  - **Construction completed** per design specifications.
  - **Equipment certified** — pressure vessels stamped, instruments calibrated.
  - **Piping pressure tested + cleaned**.
  - **Instrumentation + control systems functional** — alarms, interlocks, SIL functions tested.
  - **Safety systems** verified:
    - PSVs sized + tested + installed.
    - Emergency isolation valves + ESD logic functional.
    - Fire detection + suppression operational.
    - Gas detection working.
  - **Operating procedures** written + approved + available.
  - **Training completed** — operators qualified.
  - **MOC closure** — all changes documented + approved.
  - **PHA recommendations** addressed.
  - **Permits + approvals** — air, water, fire department.
  - **Spare parts inventory** stocked.
- **PSSR checklist** typical items:
  - ✓ All construction punch list items closed.
  - ✓ All hydrotests completed.
  - ✓ All NDT completed + accepted.
  - ✓ All instrumentation calibrated.
  - ✓ Process safety information (P&IDs, vessel design data, MOC records) up-to-date.
  - ✓ PHA findings addressed.
  - ✓ Operating procedures written + reviewed.
  - ✓ Operators trained + qualified.
  - ✓ Mechanical integrity inspection programs in place.
  - ✓ Emergency response procedures + drills completed.
  - ✓ Safety equipment functional (eyewash, showers, fire extinguishers).
  - ✓ Permits secured (air, water, fire).
- **MOC (Management of Change)** workflow:
  - **Trigger**: ANY change w:
    - Process chemistry (new reagent, concentration change).
    - Technology (catalyst, new process step).
    - Equipment (replace pump, add vessel).
    - Procedures (operating, maintenance).
    - Facility (location, layout, plot plan).
  - **Steps**:
    1. **MOC initiation** — form completed by requestor (engineer, operator).
    2. **Technical review** — engineering, process safety, operations.
    3. **Risk assessment** — PHA-like analysis (what-if, HAZOP).
    4. **Approval chain** — engineering manager, operations manager, sometimes plant manager.
    5. **Implementation** — work executed per approved scope.
    6. **PSSR (small change) lub commissioning review (large change)**.
    7. **PSI update** — drawings, manuals, training materials updated.
    8. **Close-out + audit** — final documentation, post-implementation review.
- **MOC categories**:
  - **Replacement-in-kind** — exact replacement, no MOC needed.
  - **Temporary change** — limited duration, time-bounded (e.g., bypass during repair).
  - **Permanent change** — full MOC procedure.
  - **Emergency change** — expedited, post-implementation MOC documentation.
- **Common errors**:
  - **Skipped MOC** dla "small" change — incidents traced back.
  - **Replacement-in-kind misclassification** — different grade material assumed "same".
  - **Procedure changes** not documented — operator practices diverge.
  - **PSSR rushed** — startup pressure → skipped checks.
  - **PHA findings not closed** — known hazards proceed to startup.
- **Notable incidents** linked to PSSR/MOC failures:
  - **BP Texas City 2005** — refinery isomerization unit explosion, 15 deaths.
  - **MOC failure**: tower restart, vapor cloud ignited.
  - **PSSR failure**: alarm + level gauge not verified pre-startup.
- **PSSR documentation**:
  - **Written checklist** — signed by reviewer + accepter.
  - **Punch list** — open items tracked to closure.
  - **Permission to proceed** — formal authorization, often plant manager signature.
  - **Audit trail** — retained dla life of facility.
- **MOC documentation**:
  - **MOC form** — change description, reason, risk assessment.
  - **Approvals** — signatures, dates.
  - **Implementation records** — work orders, procurement, drawings.
  - **Closing documents** — PSI updates, training records, PSSR.
- **Software tools** (modern):
  - **TECH EHS, Sphera, Enablon, Process Safety Inc.** — MOC + PSSR digital platforms.
  - **Automatic workflow** — assignment, escalation, audit logs.
  - **Integrated z plant systems** — drawings, training records.
- **Practical fitter rules**:
  - **No work without MOC** — even small piping change.
  - **PSSR checklist available** at construction site — engineers verify items during build.
  - **Cross-functional review** — operations, maintenance, engineering — all sign off.
  - **Test runs + simulations** before final commissioning.
  - **Incremental startup** — section-by-section, not whole plant at once.
  - **Stop-work authority** — anyone can halt if safety concerns arise.

### SSSSSSSSSSS. Water Hammer + Surge Analysis — Joukowsky Equation, Pump Trip
- **Co to "water hammer"**: rapid **pressure surge** w pipeline gdy **moving liquid suddenly decelerates** (valve closure, pump trip). Stored kinetic energy → pressure wave traveling at acoustic velocity. Spectacular **>10× operating pressure** spikes possible.
- **Konsekwencje**:
  - **Pipe rupture** — wall stress exceeds yield.
  - **Pump damage** — reverse flow + reverse rotation.
  - **Joint failure** — flanges, fittings, gaskets blown.
  - **Anchor failure** — pipe supports broken od dynamic loads.
  - **Cavitation collapse** — vapor pockets implode w low-pressure zones, erosion.
- **Joukowsky equation** (fundamental):
  - **ΔP = ρ · a · ΔV**:
    - ρ = fluid density (kg/m³).
    - a = wave speed w pipe (m/s) — depends on fluid + pipe material + wall thickness.
    - ΔV = velocity change (m/s).
  - **Example**: water (ρ=1000), CS pipe (a≈1200 m/s), 3 m/s flow stopped instantly:
    - **ΔP = 1000 × 1200 × 3 = 3,600,000 Pa = 36 bar** — added to operating pressure.
  - **Typical operating P** 10-50 bar → surge peak **46-86 bar** → may exceed pipe rating!
- **Wave speed (a)** factors:
  - **Pure water**: ~1480 m/s.
  - **Water in CS pipe**: ~1200-1300 m/s (pipe wall flexibility lowers).
  - **Water in HDPE pipe**: ~300-400 m/s (much more flexible).
  - **Trapped air**: dramatically lowers wave speed (1% air → wave speed halves) → but cavity collapse risk.
- **Surge mechanisms**:
  - **Valve closure**:
    - **Fast closure** (time < 2L/a) → full Joukowsky pressure.
    - **Slow closure** (time > 2L/a) → partial surge, attenuated.
    - **L** = pipe length, **a** = wave speed.
  - **Pump trip** (sudden power loss):
    - **Initial deceleration** by pump check valve OR flow reversal.
    - **Most common cause** w water/oil pipelines.
    - **Downstream**: rarefaction wave (negative pressure) → cavitation possible.
    - **Upstream**: pressure wave → potential rupture.
  - **Slug flow** — gas/liquid two-phase z liquid slug accelerating into closed section.
  - **Column separation** — vapor cavity forms (low P) → collapses → secondary surge spike.
- **Prevention methods**:
  - **Slow valve closure**:
    - **Closure time > 2L/a** (full closure exceeds wave round-trip time).
    - **Motor operators with timer** — controlled rate.
    - **Two-stage closure** — fast initial, slow final stage.
  - **VFD pump control**:
    - **Soft start/stop** — gradual ramp.
    - **Anti-coast control** — controlled deceleration after trip.
  - **Surge tank** (open standpipe):
    - Tall column connected do pipeline.
    - Surge wave dissipates upward, no peak.
    - **Cost-effective** dla water supply systems.
  - **Surge accumulator** (closed pressure vessel z gas):
    - Pressurized vessel, gas bladder pre-charged.
    - **Absorbs surge** — bladder compresses gas during pressure spike.
    - **Industrial standard** — Charlatte, BAE Tanks.
  - **Pressure relief valve (PRV) / surge relief**:
    - **Spring-loaded** valve opens at set pressure.
    - **Dumps fluid** od pipeline → tank, sewer, lower-pressure system.
    - **Fast response** — required ≤1 sec.
  - **Air valves** (vacuum breakers):
    - **Inlet air** during low-pressure transients (prevent column separation).
    - **Exhaust air** during normal operation.
  - **Anti-cavitation devices** — controls negative pressure waves.
- **Engineering analysis**:
  - **Software**: AFT Impulse, Bentley HAMMER, FlowMaster, KYPipe Surge.
  - **Inputs**: pipeline topology, valve characteristics, pump curves, fluid properties.
  - **Outputs**: time-history pressure at each node, peak surge magnitude, recommended mitigation.
  - **Required**: PHA findings, code requirement (B31.4 Appendix V).
- **Velocity limits** dla water hammer mitigation:
  - **Industrial water lines**: keep <3 m/s.
  - **Slurry lines**: 1-2 m/s (also for wear).
  - **Cooling water**: 2-3 m/s.
  - **Fire water**: 4-7 m/s OK (short-duration use).
- **Pump-specific protection**:
  - **Check valve** at pump discharge — prevents reverse flow.
  - **Slow-closing check valve** — soft seating, prevents slam.
  - **Snubber** between pump + check — absorbs shock.
  - **Bypass dla pump trip** — keeps flow moving briefly during deceleration.
- **Pipe support reinforcement**:
  - **Anchors at bends** — surge loads concentrate at direction changes.
  - **Strut supports** dla axial restraint.
  - **Cantilever spans** minimized — long unsupported pipe magnifies dynamic loads.
  - **Concrete thrust blocks** (covered iter 81) — fire water mains specifically.
- **Common errors**:
  - **Fast-closing valves** without surge analysis — code violation w many cases.
  - **No check valve** at pump discharge — reverse rotation damages.
  - **Brak surge tank** w long pipeline — peak pressure exceeds rating.
  - **Underestimated velocity** w sizing — wave speed wrong → wrong surge calc.
  - **Air pockets** w high points — column separation cavitation.
  - **No PHA dla surge scenarios** — design accepts inadequate protection.
- **Surge incidents** (historical):
  - **Water main breaks** — common in city systems z fast valve operation.
  - **Pipeline ruptures** — crude oil lines after pump trip (Calgary 2013).
  - **Fire suppression line failures** — startup of stationary fire pumps.
- **Practical fitter rules**:
  - **Slow valve operation** standard — operator training.
  - **Pressure gauge w surge tank** — visual indication mitigation working.
  - **Periodic inspection** — surge accumulator pre-charge (annual N₂ check).
  - **Test slam scenarios** during commissioning — verify mitigation effective.
  - **Operating procedures** — pump start/stop sequencing.
  - **Surge analysis** for every new pipeline >200 m length — engineering requirement.
  - **Surge protection redundancy** — multiple devices (relief valve + surge tank + slow valve).

---

## Iteration 93 — 2026-05-26

### TTTTTTTTTTT. SIL (Safety Integrity Level) + ESD Valves — IEC 61511 / IEC 61508
- **Co to**: **SIL** = quantitative measure reliability **Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** w process industry. Im wyższy SIL = mniejsze prawdopodobieństwo failure on demand = bardziej niezawodny system. Critical dla **ESD (Emergency Shutdown)** valves, fire/gas detection, pressure overprotection.
- **Standardy**:
  - **IEC 61508** — general functional safety standard.
  - **IEC 61511** — **process industry specific** (chemical, refinery, oil&gas), derived od 61508.
  - **ISA 84.00.01** — US equivalent.
- **Hierarchy**:
  - **SIS (Safety Instrumented System)** — physical system: sensors + logic solver + final elements (valves).
  - **SIF (Safety Instrumented Function)** — single function performed by SIS (e.g., "close ESD valve when level exceeds high-high alarm").
  - **SIL** — integrity level of SIF (1, 2, 3, or 4).
- **SIL levels** (low-demand mode, typical process):
  - **SIL 1**: PFD = 10⁻¹ to 10⁻², RRF = 10-100.
  - **SIL 2**: PFD = 10⁻² to 10⁻³, RRF = 100-1000.
  - **SIL 3**: PFD = 10⁻³ to 10⁻⁴, RRF = 1000-10,000.
  - **SIL 4**: PFD = 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁵, RRF = 10,000-100,000 (very rare w process industry; nuclear, aerospace).
- **PFD (Probability of Failure on Demand)**:
  - **Average PFD over service period** dla ability funkcji do execute when demanded.
  - **PFD = 0.001** → 1 z 1000 demands fails → SIL 3.
- **RRF (Risk Reduction Factor)** = 1/PFD:
  - **Inverse measure** — easier intuition.
  - **RRF 100** = SIL 2.
- **Typical SIL distribution** w process industry:
  - **SIL 1** — najczęstsze, większość basic process protections.
  - **SIL 2** — common dla ESD valves, fire/gas detection.
  - **SIL 3** — high-consequence scenarios (toxic release, BLEVE potential).
  - **SIL 4** — rare, typically achieved via multiple independent SIF z lower SILs.
- **SIL determination** (per IEC 61511):
  - **LOPA (Layer of Protection Analysis)** — most common method.
  - **Risk matrix** — alternative simpler approach.
  - **Fault tree analysis** — quantitative dla complex systems.
  - **Inputs**: consequence severity, tolerable risk, existing protection layers.
- **LOPA approach**:
  1. **Initiating event** identified (z PHA/HAZOP).
  2. **Frequency** of initiating event (per year).
  3. **Independent Protection Layers (IPLs)** identified (BPCS, alarms, mechanical relief, SIS, mitigation systems).
  4. **PFD of each IPL** — alarm 0.1, BPCS 0.1, relief 0.01.
  5. **Calculate residual risk** = initiating frequency × Π(PFD of IPLs).
  6. **Compare to tolerable risk** — if exceeds, add SIF z SIL meeting gap.
- **SIS components**:
  - **Sensors** (T, P, level, flow, gas detectors) — w field, detect process abnormality.
  - **Logic solver** (Safety PLC — TÜV-certified, e.g., Triconex, HIMA, Allen-Bradley GuardLogix) — processes inputs, decides action.
  - **Final elements** (ESD valves, motor trips, pump shutdowns) — execute action.
- **ESD valve** (Emergency Shutdown valve) specifics:
  - **Quick closing** — typically <5 sec depending on application.
  - **Fail-safe**: spring-return (fail-closed lub fail-open depending on safe state).
  - **High-integrity actuator** — pneumatic z accumulator dla power-loss closing.
  - **Quarter-turn ball lub butterfly** typically (fast).
  - **Body materials** — same jako pipeline rating, sour service NACE compliant if needed.
  - **Stem extension** dla manual override — accessible od operator.
- **SIL-rated ESD valve features**:
  - **TÜV / FMEDA certified** — Failure Modes, Effects + Diagnostic Analysis report.
  - **PFD specified** dla typical PTI (Periodic Test Interval, e.g., 1 year).
  - **Partial Stroke Testing (PST)** — valve travels 10-15% to verify operability without process upset; reduces blind testing requirement.
  - **Position feedback** — proximity switches confirm full stroke.
- **Architecture** dla higher SIL:
  - **1oo1** (one-out-of-one) — single sensor, single valve. SIL 1.
  - **1oo2** (one-out-of-two) — 2 sensors voting, **either** trips → SIL 2 typically. Conservative — false trips possible.
  - **2oo2** — both must trip → less false trips, but lower SIL (both must work).
  - **2oo3** (two-out-of-three) — 3 sensors, 2 must agree → SIL 3 typical. Best balance reliability + false trip prevention.
- **Proof testing (PTI)**:
  - **Periodic test** — verify SIF functions correctly.
  - **Frequency**: per **SIL achievement requirement** (e.g., annually dla SIL 2).
  - **Methods**:
    - **Full functional test** — actually close ESD valve, verify all components.
    - **Partial stroke test** — partial actuation (less disruptive).
    - **Sensor test** — apply known signal, verify response.
- **Documentation requirements** (IEC 61511):
  - **SRS (Safety Requirements Specification)** — defines each SIF, SIL target.
  - **Hardware design + verification reports**.
  - **Software verification** dla Safety PLC programs.
  - **Operations + maintenance procedures**.
  - **Functional safety audit** — independent assessment.
- **Common errors**:
  - **Incorrect SIL assignment** — over- lub under-specified.
  - **Same instruments dla BPCS + SIS** — IPL counted twice (not independent).
  - **Skipped PTI** — actual PFD higher than designed.
  - **Modified SIS without MOC** — invalidates SIL certification.
  - **Wrong fail-safe direction** — valve fails open when should fail closed (process fluid escapes).
- **Practical fitter rules**:
  - **SIS components tagged differently** — separate od BPCS (different color, designation).
  - **No shared instruments** — dedicated dla each SIF.
  - **Cable separation** — SIS cables w separate trays od BPCS.
  - **Test access** — bypass switches w secure cabinets dla maintenance testing (controlled by-pass).
  - **Override management** — overrides logged + time-limited.
  - **Spare parts**: SIL-rated parts dedicated, not interchanged z BPCS.

### UUUUUUUUUUU. Tank PV Vent + Emergency Vent + Flame Arrester — API 2000
- **Po co tank breathing**: atmospheric storage tank (API 650) — liquid level zmienia się + thermal expansion → tank musi **breathe** (vent + intake air). Bez breathing:
  - **Overpressure** podczas filling → tank roof lifts, joints rupture.
  - **Vacuum** podczas emptying lub cooling → tank crumples (suction collapse).
- **API 2000 standard** — "Venting Atmospheric + Low-Pressure Storage Tanks":
  - **Coverage**: tanks operating at pressures **0-15 psig** (atmospheric + low pressure).
  - **Latest edition**: 7th (2014), 8th in development.
- **Types of breathing**:
  - **Normal breathing**:
    - **Out-breathing** (vapor exit) — during filling, thermal heating.
    - **In-breathing** (air entry) — during emptying, thermal cooling.
  - **Emergency breathing**:
    - **Fire exposure** — external fire heats tank, contents boil, massive vapor generation.
    - **Equipment failure** — runaway exotherm, vacuum collapse.
- **Normal vent sizing** (API 2000):
  - **Out-breathing rate**:
    - Liquid pumping IN displaces vapor (1 m³ liquid in = 1 m³ vapor out).
    - Thermal expansion (T rise) — small contribution.
    - **Formula**: Q_out = pump rate + thermal contribution.
  - **In-breathing rate**:
    - Liquid pumping OUT requires air IN (1:1 volumetric).
    - **Thermal contraction** — air entering as vapor cools.
    - Often **larger** than out-breathing dla same pump rate.
- **PV Vent (Pressure/Vacuum Vent / Conservation Vent)**:
  - **Spring-loaded valve** z two pallets:
    - **Pressure pallet** opens at slight overpressure (typically 2-4 inches water column = 0.5-1 kPa).
    - **Vacuum pallet** opens at slight vacuum (typically -1 to -2 inches water column).
  - **Cel**: minimize vapor loss (volatile products like gasoline) + prevent atmospheric ingress (oxidation, moisture).
  - **Wibracja** — closes quickly when pressure stabilizes.
  - **Materials**:
    - **Body**: aluminum lub stainless steel.
    - **Pallets**: light alloy z elastomer seals (FKM dla hydrocarbon, EPDM dla water).
- **Emergency Vent** — additional protection dla fire scenarios:
  - **Fire heat input**: massive (1000-10,000 kW/m² possible) → enormous vaporization.
  - **Emergency vent rate**: 10-100× normal venting capacity required.
  - **Types**:
    - **Frangible roof** — designed weak roof-to-shell weld; fails before tank shell ruptures. Common dla **larger tanks**.
    - **Emergency vent valve** — pop-up disc, large diameter (DN 600-1200 typowo).
    - **Hatchway dla emergency** — manual access lifted by overpressure.
- **API 2000 emergency vent sizing**:
  - **Heat input calculation** od fire (varies z tank surface area).
  - **Vapor generation rate** (lb/h lub kg/h) z heat / latent heat of vaporization.
  - **Vent area** sized dla peak vapor rate at acceptable backpressure.
  - **Example**: 30 m diameter tank, gasoline, full envelopment fire → typical emergency vent ~36" diameter.
- **Flame arrester** (FA):
  - **Cel**: prevent **flame propagation** od atmospheric ignition source (lightning, ESD, external fire) **into** tank.
  - **Konstrukcja**: matrix of metal **crimped ribbons** lub **expanded metal**:
    - Channels narrower than **MESG (Maximum Experimental Safe Gap)** dla specific gas group.
    - Flame quenched as it passes — heat absorbed by metal mass.
  - **MESG values** (NEC/IEC gas groups):
    - Group A/IIC (H₂, acetylene): MESG <0.5 mm — narrow channels required.
    - Group B/IIB (ethylene): MESG 0.5-0.9 mm.
    - Group C/IIA (most hydrocarbons): MESG >0.9 mm.
- **Flame arrester types**:
  - **End-of-line** — at vent outlet to atmosphere.
  - **In-line** — w piping between vapor sources/sinks.
  - **Detonation arrester** — different design dla supersonic flame front.
- **API 2028** — flame arrester applications + selection.
- **Pressure loss** flame arrester:
  - **Adds backpressure** to vent system — must account in sizing.
  - **Fouling**: dirt, polymer condensation can plug arrester → bypass venting fails.
  - **Inspect regularly** + clean per maintenance schedule.
- **Inert gas blanketing** (alternative):
  - **N₂ blanket** maintained on tank vapor space — slight positive pressure prevents air ingress.
  - **N₂ supply regulator** — adds N₂ as needed.
  - **N₂ vent regulator** — exhausts excess.
  - **Advantage**: eliminates explosion atmosphere within tank (no O₂ → no ignition possible).
  - **Disadvantage**: cost N₂ + asphyxiation hazard near vents.
- **Tank gauging instrumentation** (related, briefly):
  - **Servo-driven gauges** — physical contact float lub disc.
  - **Radar gauges** — non-contact, microwaves measure surface.
  - **Hydrostatic** — bottom pressure proportional to liquid head.
  - **Differential pressure** — between bottom + top.
  - **High-level alarm switches** — independent od level transmitter (separate SIF).
  - **High-high level**: triggers ESD (close inlet valves, alarm operator).
- **Common errors**:
  - **Undersized PV vent** — overpressure damage tank roof.
  - **Skipped emergency vent** — fire scenario could rupture tank.
  - **Plugged flame arrester** — vent ineffective → tank damage.
  - **Wrong MESG** for product — flame propagates through arrester.
  - **No blanketing** for volatile flammable — explosion atmosphere always present.
  - **Set pressure too high** PV vent — opens late, overpressure already damage.
- **Practical fitter rules**:
  - **Annual inspection** flame arresters — disassemble + clean.
  - **PV vent test** dla setpoint accuracy.
  - **Emergency vent visual** — verify free movement, no corrosion.
  - **Liquid product label** + flame group — confirm correct arrester installed.
  - **Tank breather pipe** routed away from buildings — release direction.
  - **Vacuum breaker** as backup — second-line vacuum relief w case PV vent fails.
  - **Tank vent line** materials — corrosion resistant (vapor often corrosive).
  - **Periodic vent capacity verification** — calculate vs current pump rates (capacity creep risk).

### VVVVVVVVVVV. Boiler Feedwater (BFW) Piping — Economizer, Drum, Pumps
- **Po co**: industrial + power plant boilers wymagają **continuous water supply** do replace water converted do steam. BFW system **critical** dla operation — failure = boiler trip.
- **System flow path**:
  1. **Make-up water** (treated) → **deaerator** (removes O₂, CO₂).
  2. **Deaerator outlet** (105-110°C, saturated) → **BFW pump suction**.
  3. **BFW pump** discharges high-P water → economizer.
  4. **Economizer** — recovers flue gas heat (250-300°C exit T).
  5. **Steam drum** — separates steam od water, recirculates.
  6. **Downcomers** → water walls → riser tubes (steam-water mixture).
  7. **Superheater** — sets steam to design T (520-580°C).
- **Operating conditions** (typical industrial):
  - **Pressure**: low-pressure boilers 15-40 bar, HP industrial 60-100 bar, **utility boilers 150-250 bar**.
  - **Temperature**:
    - **Saturation T** (drum): 250-365°C depending on P.
    - **Economizer outlet**: just below saturation.
    - **Final steam**: 520-580°C (HP boilers).
- **BFW piping materials**:
  - **CS pipe (A106 Gr B)** — most common, do ~400°C.
  - **A335 P11 / P22** — alloy steel, for higher T (T >450°C w superheater connections).
  - **Schedule**: typically **Sch 80 / 160** dla high pressure, sometimes **XXS** (extra-extra strong) dla ultra-HP.
  - **NACE compliance** — generally not required (BFW deaerated, no H₂S).
- **Welding wymagania**:
  - **B31.1 governing code** (Power Piping).
  - **PWHT mandatory** dla CS welds w wall thickness >19 mm OR carbon equivalent.
  - **NDT**: 100% RT lub UT dla critical lines.
  - **Hardness**: <250 HV typically (boiler external piping, IBR — Indian Boiler Regulations strict).
- **Deaerator**:
  - **Cel**: remove dissolved O₂ + CO₂ z make-up water → prevent boiler corrosion.
  - **Mechanizm**: water heated z LP steam do saturation T → gas solubility drops → O₂ + CO₂ vented z steam.
  - **2 sections**:
    - **Deaerating heater** (top) — water cascades over trays / contact z steam.
    - **Storage tank** (bottom) — holds BFW reserve, supplies pumps.
  - **Outlet O₂ specs**: <7 ppb (parts per billion) — extremely low.
  - **Pressure**: 1-3 bar gauge typical (atmospheric + slight pressure).
- **BFW pump** — critical equipment:
  - **Type**: multistage centrifugal (5-15 stages), high-head requirement (1500-3000 m head dla HP boilers).
  - **Speed**: 3000-5500 RPM (electric) lub 5000-8000 RPM (steam turbine drive).
  - **NPSHa critical** — pump suction must satisfy NPSHa requirements:
    - **Deaerator** typically **elevated 15-25 m** above pump dla flooded suction.
    - **No air entrainment** w suction line.
  - **Materials**: stainless steel internals (304, 316, 17-4PH dla some impellers).
  - **Mechanical seals** — high-T capability, typically tungsten carbide.
- **Pump auxiliary systems**:
  - **Minimum flow recirculation** (covered iter 84) — protection dla low-flow.
  - **Cooling water** dla bearings + seal flush.
  - **Lubrication system** — forced oil dla high-RPM bearings.
- **Discharge piping**:
  - **Isolation valve** + **check valve** — standard configuration.
  - **Check valve location**: between pump + isolation (prevents reverse flow during outage).
  - **Pressure relief** — protect pump dla blocked discharge.
- **Economizer** layout:
  - **Multiple rows finned tubes** w boiler back-pass (flue gas exit zone).
  - **Tube material**: CS for low-T boilers, alloy dla higher T.
  - **Tube ID**: 32-50 mm typically.
  - **Water side**: BFW flowing through tubes.
  - **Gas side**: flue gas cooling 350°C → 130-150°C.
- **Steam drum**:
  - **Vessel**: large diameter (1-2 m), horizontal cylindrical.
  - **Internals**:
    - **Steam separators** (cyclone, baffle) — remove water od steam.
    - **Distribution piping** — feedwater spray, level control.
    - **Chemical injection** points — boiler chemistry treatment.
  - **Pressure-temperature ratings**: extremely high (HP boilers ASME III rating).
- **Boiler water chemistry**:
  - **pH 9.0-10.5** dla CS protection (magnetite layer formation).
  - **Phosphate** (Na₃PO₄) — buffer pH + scale prevention.
  - **Hydrazine (N₂H₄)** lub **carbohydrazide** — oxygen scavenger (residual O₂ removal).
  - **Amine (morpholine, AVT)** — pH control + CO₂ neutralization in steam-condensate cycle.
  - **Continuous blowdown** — drain dla solids removal (TDS control).
  - **Intermittent blowdown** — drum bottom drain for sludge.
- **BFW piping issues**:
  - **Erosion-corrosion** (FAC — Flow Accelerated Corrosion):
    - Mechanism: high-velocity hot water dissolves magnetite layer faster than it forms.
    - **Locations**: elbows, reducers, tees — high-turbulence zones.
    - **Catastrophic failures** historicznie (Surry NPP 1986 — 4 deaths).
    - **Mitigation**: alloy upgrade (Cr-Mo steels resist FAC) + chemistry control (DOH).
  - **Carryover** — boiler water droplets w steam → deposits w superheater, turbine.
  - **Carryover prevention**: clean steam separators, proper water level, no overload.
- **Common errors**:
  - **Wrong material dla high-T BFW** — FAC failures.
  - **Insufficient deaeration** — dissolved O₂ corrodes economizer + drum.
  - **NPSHa inadequate** — pump cavitation, premature failure.
  - **Incorrect chemistry** — scaling, foaming, carryover.
  - **No blowdown** — TDS accumulates → scaling + carryover.
- **Practical fitter rules**:
  - **Hydrotest** thoroughly before commissioning — boiler external piping wymaga rigorous testing.
  - **PWHT records** linked do every weld — IBR audit requirement.
  - **Magnetite passivation** — initial fill z boil-out cleaning chemicals (phosphate + caustic), then drain.
  - **Periodic inspection** elbows + reducers — FAC location surveillance.
  - **Water chemistry sampling** — multiple points (deaerator, BFW, drum, boiler water, steam).
  - **Continuous monitoring** — conductivity, pH, dissolved O₂, hardness — control panels.
  - **Lay-up procedures** — boiler offline >7 days requires preservation (wet lay-up z N₂ blanket OR dry lay-up + desiccant).

---

## Iteration 94 — 2026-05-26

### WWWWWWWWWWW. Distillation Column / Refinery Tower — Piping Configuration
- **Co to**: vertical cylindrical vessel separating liquid mixture **components by boiling point** w continuous counter-current contact między vapor + liquid. Backbone of refinery (crude unit, naphtha hydrotreater, FCC fractionator), petrochemical (ethylene splitter), gas processing (demethanizer, deethanizer).
- **Tower terminology**:
  - **Atmospheric column** — operates ~atmospheric P (refinery CDU/crude distillation, 1-2 bar).
  - **Vacuum column** — operates <0.5 bar (refinery vacuum tower) — separates heavy products without thermal decomposition.
  - **High-pressure column** — gas processing (cryogenic demethanizer 30-40 bar).
- **External piping connections** (typical refinery atmospheric tower):
  - **Feed inlet** — middle of tower, partially-vaporized feed enters.
  - **Reboiler return line** (bottom) — hot vapor regenerated od bottoms.
  - **Bottoms product line** — heavy liquid out z reboiler.
  - **Overhead vapor line** — top, vapor to condenser.
  - **Reflux line** — condensed liquid returned do top.
  - **Distillate (overhead product) line** — net product.
  - **Side draw lines** (multiple) — intermediate products (kerosene, diesel, heavy gas oil w CDU).
  - **Pumparound lines** — internal cooling via side liquid withdraw → cooler → return.
  - **Steam injection lines** (CDU specifically) — bottom + side strippers, lower partial pressure.
- **Feed inlet piping**:
  - **Material**: alloy match service (CS dla CDU feed, SS dla corrosive service).
  - **Diameter**: large (DN 600-1500 dla CDU).
  - **Internal distributor**:
    - **Vapor**: bottom horizontal sparger, distributes flow evenly.
    - **Liquid**: top horizontal trough, drops onto distribution tray.
    - **2-phase**: prevents flashing erosion at nozzle.
  - **Pressure rating**: per design, considerate dla vapor flashing (low-velocity to avoid impact).
- **Overhead system** (top tower → distillate):
  - **Overhead line size**: very large (DN 1000-3500 dla CDU due do low pressure + high vapor volume).
  - **Slope toward condenser** 1:200 — drain condensate.
  - **Velocity limit**: 15-25 m/s (low pressure → high volume → large pipe).
  - **Material**: CS standard, alloy + dla corrosive overheads (CDU overhead → HCl, H₂S, NH₃ → alloy + neutralizer injection).
  - **Vapor line gradients**: pressure drop critical (vacuum towers especially) — limits operating P.
- **Reflux system**:
  - **Reflux pump** od condensate accumulator (overhead drum).
  - **Reflux line** back do top tray of tower.
  - **Reflux flow** controlled — typical L/D ratio (reflux:distillate) 0.5-3.0 depending on separation difficulty.
  - **Pressure required**: pump discharge > tower top pressure + line friction.
  - **Distribution dla tower top** — splash plate lub spray nozzles dla even liquid distribution.
- **Reboiler system**:
  - **Reboiler types**:
    - **Kettle reboiler** — large shell z submerged tubes, kettles vapor naturally.
    - **Thermosiphon (vertical lub horizontal)** — natural circulation via density difference.
    - **Forced circulation** — pumped, dla viscous lub fouling service.
    - **Fired heater** (CDU bottoms) — direct fire, max T 350-380°C.
  - **Heat source**: steam (LP, MP, HP), hot oil, flue gas, lub direct fire.
  - **Reboiler return line** — large diameter (vapor + entrained liquid), velocity limited 15-25 m/s.
  - **Bottom inlet** — separate od reboiler vapor inlet (different elevations dla flow separation).
- **Bottoms product line**:
  - **Pump suction** od bottom of tower OR reboiler.
  - **Material**: high-T alloy (CDU bottoms ~360°C → alloy steel A335 P11/P22).
  - **Vortex breaker** w bottom (covered iter 85) — avoid pump cavitation.
- **Side draw lines** (multi-product CDU):
  - **Naphtha** od upper section.
  - **Kerosene** middle.
  - **Diesel** lower-middle.
  - **HGO (Heavy Gas Oil)** bottom section.
  - **Each draw** routed do side stripper (small tower removing lights), then pump-out to storage.
- **Pumparound lines** — internal cooling/heat balance:
  - **Take hot liquid** od side of tower.
  - **Cool w heat exchanger** (preheat crude feed dla example).
  - **Return cooled liquid** above the draw (provides reflux dla section between draws).
  - **Removes heat** without overhead condenser overload.
- **Steam injection lines** (CDU):
  - **Stripping steam** bottom of tower + side strippers.
  - **Cel**: lower partial pressure of products → more vaporization at lower T (avoids cracking).
  - **Steam quality**: superheated (no condensation w tower).
  - **Distribution**: sparger ring inside tower.
- **Common errors**:
  - **Wrong feed location** — design vs actual tray composition, poor separation.
  - **Pumparound undersized** — heat balance off, overhead overload.
  - **Reflux drum undersized** — process upset, frequent reflux interruption.
  - **Vacuum tower overhead** under-sized — pressure drop excessive, lower operating vacuum.
  - **No vortex breaker** — pump cavitation.
- **Practical fitter rules**:
  - **All flanges in tower piping** — high temperature service requires hot bolting periodically.
  - **Tower bottoms** — heat trace dla low-T service prevention (wax precipitation).
  - **Side stripper** trays accessible — periodic cleaning required.
  - **PSV protection** — both overhead + bottoms protected (column overpressure scenarios).
  - **Sample valves** every product stream — quality monitoring.
  - **Insulation** dla all towers — high-T external surfaces, energy conservation, personnel protection.
  - **Pump alignment + vibration** — critical, towers experience natural sway (wind, seismic).

### XXXXXXXXXXX. TEMA Heat Exchanger Designations — Shell & Tube Types
- **Co to**: **TEMA (Tubular Exchangers Manufacturers Association)** standard dla shell-and-tube heat exchangers. **3-letter code** identifies exchanger type:
  - **First letter** — Front End head type.
  - **Second letter** — Shell type.
  - **Third letter** — Rear End head type.
- **Common combinations**: BEM, AES, BES, BEU, AEL, AKU, NEN, etc.
- **TEMA classes** (severity):
  - **TEMA R** — most stringent (refinery, severe service).
  - **TEMA C** — moderate (commercial).
  - **TEMA B** — chemical, intermediate.
- **Front End head types (A-D, N)**:
  - **A — Channel + Removable Cover**:
    - Removable flat cover dla tube-side access.
    - Channel separable z tube bundle.
    - **Most common** dla refinery + petrochemical.
    - Easy cleaning + maintenance.
  - **B — Bonnet (integral cover)**:
    - Welded bonnet on channel.
    - **Cheaper** vs A type (no flange).
    - Disadvantage: must disconnect piping dla tube access.
    - **Common dla** simple, low-maintenance service.
  - **C — Channel Integral z Tubesheet, Removable Cover**:
    - Tubesheet welded do channel.
    - Removable cover dla access.
    - Used dla high-pressure / hazardous service.
  - **D — Special High-Pressure Closure**:
    - For >700 bar service.
    - Channel + tubesheet integral + special bolting.
  - **N — Channel Integral z Tubesheet + Bonnet** (similar to N rear head):
    - All welded construction.
    - **Very high pressure**.
- **Shell types (E-K, X)**:
  - **E — Single-pass shell**:
    - **Most common** (60-70% of all exchangers).
    - Single inlet, single outlet, longitudinal flow.
    - Good for typical heating/cooling.
  - **F — Two-pass shell z longitudinal baffle**:
    - Internal baffle creates 2 shell passes.
    - **Better dla** high temperature difference applications.
    - Disadvantage: baffle leakage reduces efficiency.
  - **G — Split flow shell**:
    - 2 inlets (sides), 1 outlet (center) — split + recombined.
    - Used dla horizontal thermosiphon reboiler.
  - **H — Double split flow**:
    - Similar to G but 4 inlets.
    - Reduces pressure drop.
  - **J — Divided flow shell**:
    - 1 center inlet, 2 end outlets (or vice versa).
    - **Used dla** condensers (lower pressure drop).
  - **K — Kettle reboiler**:
    - Large diameter top section (vapor disengagement).
    - **Specifically for reboilers**.
    - Submerged tube bundle.
  - **X — Cross flow**:
    - Fluid flows perpendicular to tubes.
    - **Used dla** gas coolers, condensers.
- **Rear End head types (L-W)**:
  - **L — Fixed Tubesheet (integral z shell flange)**:
    - Tubes + tubesheets welded to shell.
    - **Cheapest** design.
    - **CANNOT** pull bundle dla mechanical cleaning.
    - Limited dla thermal expansion (z expansion joint if needed).
  - **M — Fixed Tubesheet (bonneted)**:
    - Similar to L but z bonneted cover.
    - Same limitations.
  - **N — Fixed Tubesheet (integral z bonnet + shell)**:
    - All-welded compact design.
    - **High pressure capability**.
  - **P — Outside Packed Floating Head**:
    - Floating tubesheet z external packing.
    - **Limited dla low P** services.
    - Easy dla bundle removal.
  - **S — Floating Head z Backing Device (split-ring)**:
    - **Most common floating head** dla refinery.
    - Internal split ring allows tube bundle removal.
    - Handles **large thermal expansion**.
    - Dual gaskets (shell + tube side independent).
    - Higher cost vs fixed.
  - **T — Pull-Through Floating Head**:
    - Floating tubesheet smaller than shell ID → bundle pulls through without disassembly.
    - **Cleaning friendly** but less efficient (gap between bundle + shell).
  - **U — U-Tube Bundle**:
    - U-shaped tubes — one tubesheet, no rear head needed.
    - **Thermal expansion accommodated** via U-bend.
    - **CANNOT** mechanically clean inside U tubes (chemical cleaning only).
    - Common dla **clean service** (steam dla example).
  - **W — Externally Sealed Floating Tubesheet**:
    - Special design dla packed expansion joint.
    - Limited application.
- **Specific common combinations**:
  - **BEM** (Bonnet + E shell + Fixed tubesheet):
    - **Cheapest TEMA design**.
    - Simple, fixed bundle.
    - **Low P + non-fouling service**.
    - Limited dla thermal expansion.
  - **AES** (Channel + E shell + S floating head):
    - **Refinery standard** dla fouling service.
    - Removable bundle, mechanical cleaning możliwe.
    - Thermal expansion OK.
  - **AEL** (Channel + E shell + L fixed):
    - Common dla water cooling, simple service.
    - Removable channel cover only.
  - **AKU** (Channel + Kettle + U-tube):
    - **Standard kettle reboiler**.
    - Bundle removable, U-tube design.
  - **NEN** (N front + E shell + N rear):
    - Fully welded — for ultra-high P / radioactive / toxic service.
    - **No mechanical cleaning** options.
  - **BEU** (Bonnet + E shell + U-tube):
    - Compact + cheap.
    - Limited cleaning options (U-tubes only chemical clean).
- **Selection criteria**:
  - **Cleaning requirements**:
    - **Mechanical** both sides → AES, AET preferred.
    - **Chemical** only → fixed (BEM, BEN) lub U-tube (BEU) OK.
  - **Thermal expansion**:
    - **Large** → floating head (S/T) or U-tube preferred.
    - **Limited** → fixed tubesheet OK z expansion joint.
  - **Pressure**:
    - **<60 bar** → standard B/E.
    - **60-200 bar** → reinforced design, careful flange selection.
    - **>200 bar** → N front head, specialty.
  - **Fouling**:
    - **Heavy** → floating head (S) dla cleaning access.
    - **Light** → fixed OK.
  - **Cost** (rough ordering):
    - **Cheapest**: BEM (fixed tubesheet, bonnet).
    - **Mid**: AEM, BEU, AKU.
    - **Most expensive**: AES, NEN (specialty).
- **TEMA standards** (key sizes):
  - **TEMA R** — refinery class — strictest requirements (higher safety factors, tighter tolerances).
  - **TEMA C** — chemical/commercial — moderate.
  - **TEMA B** — general process — between R + C.
- **Practical fitter rules**:
  - **Read drawings carefully** — TEMA code right after "Type" field.
  - **Bundle pulling planning** (covered iter 88) — required dla AES, AET, AKU.
  - **Tube replacement** — U-tubes harder dla repair than straight tubes.
  - **Floating head leak** — Pull bundle dla repair.
  - **Tube vibration** — kettle (K) shell type prone dla vibration, specific baffle design needed.
  - **Foam separation** w K-shell — vapor disengagement adequate dla mist removal.

### YYYYYYYYYYY. Vacuum Tower Piping — Refinery Overhead + Ejector Systems
- **Po co vacuum tower**: refinery wants distill **heavy residue** od atmospheric tower bottoms. **Atmospheric distillation limited** by **thermal cracking** above 360-380°C. **Vacuum operation** lowers boiling point → can distill 480-590°C products at much lower actual T (250-350°C).
- **Vacuum levels**:
  - **Atmospheric**: 1 bar abs (1000 mbar).
  - **Refinery vacuum tower**: **30-80 mbar abs** typically.
  - **Deep vacuum**: <10 mbar (specialty separations).
- **Components**:
  - **Vacuum tower** itself — large diameter (often 8-12 m for crude units).
  - **Overhead piping** — extremely large (DN 1500-3000 due do high volume z low pressure).
  - **Precondenser** — cools overhead, condenses heavy components.
  - **Vacuum-producing system**:
    - **Steam ejectors** (most common).
    - **Liquid ring vacuum pumps** (for some applications).
    - **Combination systems** (ejector + LR pump dla efficiency).
  - **After-condensers** — final condensation.
  - **Hotwell** — collection vessel dla non-condensables + condensate.
- **Why vacuum is hard**:
  - **Low pressure** → low fluid density → **huge volumetric flow** dla same mass flow.
  - **Pipe sizing**: vapor velocity 30-60 m/s typowo (vs 10-20 m/s w atmospheric).
  - **Pressure drop** any restriction → big impact w vacuum (small ΔP could halve actual vacuum).
  - **Air leakage** — even tiny leaks ruin vacuum (atmospheric pressure pushes inward).
- **Overhead line design**:
  - **Material**: CS standard, alloy + dla corrosive (sour vacuum towers — alloy + neutralization).
  - **Length**: keep as short as possible (minimize ΔP).
  - **Bends**: long-radius preferred (lower ΔP).
  - **Insulation**: heavy dla heat retention (no premature condensation w line).
  - **Drains**: every low point — condensate accumulates, blocks vacuum.
- **Velocity limits**:
  - **Vapor lines**: max 30-60 m/s (depending on pressure level).
  - **Higher velocity** → ΔP rises rapidly → vacuum degraded.
  - **Erosion concern** also dla some velocities — entrained liquid droplets impact at high speeds.
- **Steam ejector system**:
  - **Princip**: HP steam expands through nozzle → supersonic velocity → entrains low-P process vapor → mixed stream decelerated w diffuser → discharged at higher P.
  - **Stages**: usually **3-5 stages** dla refinery vacuum tower.
    - Stage 1: 5 mbar → 50 mbar.
    - Stage 2: 50 mbar → 200 mbar.
    - Stage 3: 200 mbar → 1 bar (atmospheric).
  - **Inter-stage condenser** dla each stage — removes condensable z stream, reduces volume dla next stage.
  - **Motive steam**: MP steam (10-15 bar typowo).
  - **Steam consumption**: 0.5-2 kg steam / kg process gas removed.
- **Ejector materials**:
  - **Nozzle**: SS 316 (steam erosion + condensation).
  - **Diffuser body**: CS lub SS depending on service.
  - **Internals**: precision-machined.
- **Inter-stage condensers**:
  - **Direct contact** — water sprayed in, condenses condensables, mixes w water.
  - **Surface (indirect)** — shell-and-tube, no mixing.
  - **Choice depends on** contamination tolerance + water disposal.
- **Hotwell / vacuum drum**:
  - **Collection vessel** dla condensate + non-condensable separation.
  - **Non-condensable** (air + light HC) routed do final stage ejector OR flare.
  - **Condensate (sour water)** routed do sour water stripper.
- **Air leakage management** — critical:
  - **Sources**: flange leaks, valve packing, gauge connections, manhole gaskets, instrument taps.
  - **Specification**: industrial vacuum tower allowable air leakage ~10-30 lb/h (5-15 kg/h).
  - **Higher** = ejector overloaded, vacuum degrades.
  - **Detection**: helium leak detector, ultrasonic, soap bubble test (during shutdown).
- **Liquid ring vacuum pump** (alternative):
  - **Centrifugal pump z liquid seal** (water typically).
  - **Compact** vs ejector system.
  - **Higher initial cost**, lower OPEX (no steam consumption).
  - **Used** dla smaller systems, more deep vacuum (10-50 mbar).
- **Common errors**:
  - **Overhead line undersized** — vacuum drops, products carryover.
  - **Air leakage** unchecked — ejector overloaded, can't reach vacuum.
  - **Condenser fouling** — cooling water side scale → poor heat transfer → vapor not condensed → vacuum drops.
  - **Steam pressure low** — ejector underperforms, vacuum degrades.
  - **No insulation** — overhead line cools → premature condensation → flow blockage.
- **Practical fitter rules**:
  - **Vacuum gauges** at multiple points along overhead — diagnose ΔP issues.
  - **Drain valves** at every low point — open during commissioning.
  - **Flange face inspection** — annual visual + soap test.
  - **Manway gaskets** — replace annually, vacuum-rated material (compressed graphite preferred).
  - **Steam supply** — clean, dry, regulated pressure to ejectors.
  - **Water supply** to inter-stage condensers — adequate flow + cool T.
  - **Performance monitoring** — track vacuum vs design over time, trending degradation.
  - **Spare ejector nozzles** — replacement procedure planned (annual or biennial).

---

## Iteration 95 — 2026-05-26

### ZZZZZZZZZZZ. Pipeline Leak Detection Systems — CPM, DAS, DTS, Vapor Sensing
- **Po co**: pipelines transportują **hazardous + valuable products** — leaks cause:
  - **Environmental damage** (oil spills, gas releases).
  - **Safety incidents** (fire, explosion, asphyxiation).
  - **Economic loss** (product value, cleanup, fines).
  - **Regulatory compliance** (PHMSA, EPA, state).
- **Standardy**:
  - **API 1130** — Computational Pipeline Monitoring (CPM) — primary US standard.
  - **API 1149** — Pipeline Variable Uncertainty + impact on leak detection.
  - **49 CFR 195** (PHMSA hazardous liquid pipelines) — leak detection requirements.
  - **CSA Z662** Canada.
- **2 main approaches**:
  - **Internal (CPM-based)**: measures process variables (flow, P, T) at endpoints → computes mass/volume balance → detects discrepancy = leak.
  - **External**: separate sensors along pipeline (acoustic, fiber optic, vapor) → directly detect leak signature.
- **CPM methods** (API 1130):
  - **Mass balance** (volume balance):
    - **Most common** method.
    - Compares inlet mass flow vs outlet mass flow + corrections (line pack, T expansion).
    - **Sensitivity**: 1-5% flow over hours, smaller leaks impossible to detect short-term.
    - **Response time**: 5-60 min depending on leak size.
  - **Pressure point analysis** (RTTM — Real-Time Transient Model):
    - Models pipeline hydraulics, detects deviations.
    - **Better sensitivity** dla certain leak scenarios.
    - **More complex** — requires accurate model.
  - **Negative pressure wave (NPW)**:
    - Leak creates **rarefaction wave** propagating along pipeline.
    - Pressure sensors detect wave arrival times.
    - **Detection time**: seconds (vs minutes dla mass balance).
    - **Location accuracy**: based on wave arrival timing.
    - **Better dla** quick large leaks.
  - **Statistical analysis** — outlier detection, machine learning approaches.
- **External methods**:
  - **Distributed Fiber Optic Sensing**:
    - **Fiber optic cable** buried alongside pipe (or attached do pipe wall).
    - **Light pulse** transmitted, backscatter analyzed.
    - **2 main types**:
      - **DAS (Distributed Acoustic Sensing)** — detects vibrations / sound (leak makes hiss).
      - **DTS (Distributed Temperature Sensing)** — detects T changes (leak gas / liquid cools via JT effect; oil heating).
    - **Resolution**: 1-10 meters along entire pipeline length.
    - **Detection accuracy**:
      - **DAS**: ±10 m location, 1-2 sec response.
      - **DTS**: ±1 m location, slower (minutes-hours).
    - **Combined DAS+DTS** — best capabilities.
    - **Range**: 30-50 km per fiber, longer z amplifiers.
  - **Vapor sensing tube**:
    - **Permeable tube** along pipeline (buried).
    - **Pump samples** continuously, gas chromatograph analyzes.
    - **Sensitive** dla light hydrocarbons (methane, propane).
    - **Used** dla gas + light liquid pipelines.
  - **Cable-based sensors**:
    - **Liquid-sensing cable** — detects liquid presence (oil, water).
    - **Hydrocarbon-sensing cable** — specific dla HC.
    - **Coverage**: continuous along cable length.
    - **Used dla** containment areas, sumps, tank farms.
  - **Acoustic emission monitoring**:
    - **Microphones** at intervals along pipe.
    - **Detect leak sound** — hiss + flow noise.
    - **Sensitivity** good dla pressurized gas leaks.
- **External vs internal comparison**:
  - **External pros**:
    - Faster response (seconds vs minutes).
    - Better small-leak sensitivity.
    - Locates leak precisely.
  - **External cons**:
    - More hardware (sensors, cables) — expensive.
    - Limited coverage (cable length).
    - Calibration + maintenance intensive.
  - **Internal pros**:
    - Single SCADA system covers entire pipeline.
    - Lower hardware cost.
    - Mature technology.
  - **Internal cons**:
    - Slower response.
    - Larger threshold (cannot detect tiny leaks).
    - Less precise location.
- **Drone / aerial surveys**:
  - **IR camera** od helicopter / drone — detects gas plumes, oil sheen.
  - **Visible camera** + lidar — pipeline marker visibility, encroachments.
  - **Periodic (weekly-monthly)** rather than continuous.
- **Sniffing** (manual + dog-based):
  - **Methane detector** carried by walker / vehicle along ROW.
  - **Dogs** trained dla petroleum smell — used historically.
- **Smart pigging**:
  - **Inline inspection** tools (covered iter 73) — detect:
    - **Geometric pigs**: dents, deformations.
    - **MFL pigs**: corrosion (wall thinning).
    - **UT pigs**: more accurate, but slower.
    - **Caliper pigs**: ID measurement.
- **Performance metrics** (API 1130):
  - **Sensitivity**: minimum detectable leak (e.g., 1% steady-state flow).
  - **Reliability**: false alarm rate vs missed alarm rate.
  - **Accuracy**: location precision (e.g., ±100 m for CPM).
  - **Robustness**: tolerance dla operational transients.
- **Operational considerations**:
  - **False alarms** — operator fatigue → ignore legitimate leaks.
  - **Tuning** — balance sensitivity vs false alarms.
  - **Operator training** — recognize alarms, respond appropriately.
  - **SCADA integration** — alarms displayed prominently w control room.
- **Common errors**:
  - **Wrong threshold** — too tight = false alarms; too loose = miss small leaks.
  - **Sensor drift** — periodic calibration required.
  - **Wrong fiber optic placement** — buried too deep, sensitivity lost.
  - **No correlation z operational data** — single source decisions, not validated.
  - **Operator override** of alarms — accountability needed.
- **Practical fitter rules**:
  - **Pre-commissioning leak test** — high-sensitivity test before service.
  - **Annual functional testing** — simulate leak scenarios, verify detection.
  - **Sensor maintenance** — clean, calibrate annually.
  - **Fiber optic integrity** — periodic OTDR testing (optical time-domain reflectometer).
  - **Response procedures** — operator practice drills, emergency response plan.
  - **Documentation chain** — alarm logs, response times, false alarm tracking.

### AAAAAAAAAAAA. Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC) — Refinery Piping Challenges
- **Co to**: refinery process **cracking heavy gas oil + residue** into **lighter, valuable products** (gasoline, LPG, diesel). **Largest gasoline producer** w modern refineries. Operates at high T + high catalyst circulation rate.
- **3 main units (reactor / regenerator / fractionator)**:
  - **Reactor (riser)**:
    - **Vertical pipe 30-40 m tall**, **DN 1500-3000**.
    - **Hot catalyst** (700°C) + **feed oil** mix at bottom.
    - **Cracking reaction** w 2-5 sec residence time.
    - **Products + spent catalyst** flow up do separator.
  - **Regenerator**:
    - **Burns off carbon** od spent catalyst (catalyst saturated z coke after cracking).
    - **Air injection** combusts coke → CO/CO₂ + heat.
    - **Heat carries back** do reactor (catalyst as heat carrier).
    - **Operating T**: 700-750°C.
    - **Pressure**: 2-3 bar gauge.
  - **Fractionator (main column)**:
    - Atmospheric distillation column separating reactor effluent → gasoline, LCO (light cycle oil), HCO (heavy cycle oil), slurry oil.
- **Catalyst circulation system**:
  - **Standpipe** (downflow leg) — catalyst flows by gravity od regenerator → riser bottom.
  - **Slide valves** — control catalyst flow rate.
  - **Riser** — upward catalyst transport z feed oil.
  - **Reactor / cyclone** — separates catalyst od product vapors.
  - **Stripper** — strips entrained hydrocarbons z catalyst before regenerator.
  - **Spent catalyst standpipe** — flow z reactor → regenerator.
- **Operating challenges**:
  - **Erosion**: catalyst particles (50-100 µm) flow at 5-25 m/s + abrasive nature → wear everywhere.
  - **High T**: ~750°C, structural concerns + material limits.
  - **Coking** + fouling — process side.
  - **Catalyst attrition** — particles break, fines generated → entrained, lost.
  - **Slide valve wear** — high erosion at flow restriction.
- **Materials**:
  - **Carbon steel** — not adequate dla most FCC piping (high T + erosion).
  - **Alloy steels**: P22 dla some piping, **Cr-Mo**.
  - **SS 304/304H** — high T components, austenitic.
  - **Refractory lining**:
    - **Most reactors + regenerators** lined z **dense erosion-resistant refractory** (chromium-alumina based).
    - **Castable refractory** — applied wet, cured.
    - **Brick lining** — high-erosion zones (slide valves, riser elbows).
    - **Anchor system** (covered iter 30 generally) — V-anchors lub Y-anchors w steel shell.
  - **Hardfacing** dla erosion-prone components — Stellite, ceramic coatings, chrome carbide.
- **Slide valves**:
  - **Cel**: control catalyst flow rate między units.
  - **Konstrukcja**: large rectangular gate sliding across pipe.
  - **Material**: hardened steel z hard-faced sealing surfaces.
  - **Erosion zones**: edges of gate where catalyst flows past.
  - **Refactory-lined housing**.
  - **Failure modes**:
    - **Wear**: erosion at gate edge → leakage past valve when closed.
    - **Stuck open lub closed**: thermal expansion, fouling.
    - **Refractory failure**: anchor failure, spalling.
  - **Critical safety device**: ExxonMobil Torrance 2015 — slide valve failure led do refinery explosion.
- **Cyclones**:
  - **Multiple stages** (primary + secondary) — high catalyst recovery (>99.9%).
  - **Wear-prone**: cyclone wall (catalyst impinges), dipleg (catalyst returns down), tip.
  - **Refractory-lined** typically.
- **Air piping** dla regenerator:
  - **Large diameter** (DN 1500-2500) — high air volume.
  - **Material**: CS dla air at ambient → 200°C (compressor discharge).
  - **Distribution grid** w regenerator (refractory-lined nozzles).
- **Steam generation** dla riser:
  - **Steam injection** at riser inlet — atomize feed oil + reduce partial pressure.
  - **MP steam** typowo.
- **CO boiler / WHB (Waste Heat Boiler)**:
  - **Hot flue gas** (CO + CO₂) z regenerator → CO boiler → completed combustion CO → CO₂, generates HP steam.
  - **Tube material**: alloy CS, vapor-side scaling concern.
- **NDT considerations**:
  - **Refractory inspection** — annual visual, hammer test for hollow sounds.
  - **Slide valve inspection** — every turnaround, measure clearances.
  - **UT thickness** — pipe walls (cold side of refractory-lined pipe especially).
- **Common errors**:
  - **Refractory failure** — anchor design inadequate, thermal cycling.
  - **Slide valve wear unchecked** — leakage scenarios → safety risk.
  - **Wrong material grade** — premature failure under high T + erosion.
  - **Operating outside design envelope** — over-circulation rate, higher erosion.
- **Practical fitter rules**:
  - **Refractory specialty contractors** — not standard pipe work.
  - **Hot work permits** — strict, FCC operates at high T even during planned downtime.
  - **Cool-down procedures** — controlled cooling z N₂ purge dla minimum 24-48 h before access.
  - **Spare slide valves** — major component, long lead time.
  - **Inspection windows** w turnarounds — thorough refractory + erosion checks.

### BBBBBBBBBBBB. Geothermal Pipeline — Brine + Scale + Materials
- **Co to**: pipelines dla geothermal energy plants — **hot brine, steam, or 2-phase fluid** od deep wells (1-3 km deep) → power plants lub direct heating systems.
- **Operating conditions**:
  - **T**: 80-300°C (depends on resource).
  - **P**: 5-100 bar (depends on T + saturation).
  - **Fluid composition**: water + dissolved minerals + dissolved gases (CO₂, H₂S, methane, N₂).
- **Types of geothermal systems**:
  - **Hot dry rock** — water injected, returned as steam.
  - **Flash steam** — high-T brine flashes do steam at lower P.
  - **Binary cycle** — geothermal heats secondary working fluid (organic — Rankine cycle).
  - **Direct use** — heating buildings, agriculture.
- **Key challenges**:
  - **Scale formation**:
    - **Silica (SiO₂)** — common dla high-T brine (>180°C). Hardens onto pipe walls.
    - **Calcium carbonate (CaCO₃)** — common dla lower-T brine (boiling point shift).
    - **Calcium sulfate, barium sulfate, iron sulfide** — also occur.
    - **Result**: reduced pipe diameter, pressure drop increases, eventual blockage.
  - **Corrosion**:
    - **H₂S** + chlorides + low pH = aggressive sour service.
    - **Stress corrosion cracking (SCC)** w stressed alloys.
    - **CO₂ corrosion** in lower-T systems.
    - **Galvanic** corrosion at dissimilar metal joints.
- **Material selection**:
  - **Carbon steel** — most common for lower aggressive brine, schedule controlled dla corrosion allowance.
  - **Stainless steel 304/316L** — moderate H₂S + chlorides; SCC concern at higher T.
